disclaimer: Educational reference only — verify every dose, threshold, and technique against current institutional protocols and society guidelines before clinical use.

procedures.title: Percutaneous Abscess Drainage
procedures.overview: Image-guided catheter drainage of infected or symptomatic fluid collections — the minimally invasive answer to source control that has replaced most surgical washouts. Success is measured by clinical recovery, and the durable failures are almost always undrained loculations or an unaddressed underlying communication (fistula) [1].

labs: CBC, coags — deep drains are HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [2]
Blood cultures if septic; review CT/US for size, loculation, and access corridor
Antibiotics started; do not delay drainage in sepsis for perfect labs
Confirm the collection is drainable (liquefied) not solid phlegmon
preop.meds: Broad-spectrum antibiotics before/around drainage; sedation/analgesia; local anesthesia.
equipment: US and/or CT guidance; micropuncture or trocar kit
8–14F locking pigtail/sump catheters (viscosity-dependent — bigger for thick pus/necrosis)
Wires, serial dilators (Seldinger); hydrodissection setup for tight corridors
Specimen tubes (Gram stain, culture, cytology as indicated)
Transgluteal/transhepatic route planning tools as needed
indications: Drainable infected collection: intra-abdominal/pelvic abscess (post-op, diverticular, appendiceal, Crohn), hepatic/renal/splenic abscess
Symptomatic sterile collections (large seroma, lymphocele, biloma, urinoma)
Empyema (see specific management), infected pancreatic necrosis (step-up approach)
Diagnostic aspiration to characterize a collection

absolute: No safe percutaneous window to the collection
Uncorrectable coagulopathy for a deep high-risk target (non-emergent)
relative: Collection that is not yet organized/liquefied (phlegmon — antibiotics, reimage)
Small collections likely to resolve with antibiotics alone
Echinococcal cyst (spillage/anaphylaxis risk — special precautions)

steps.text: US for superficial/accessible collections (real-time, no radiation); CT for deep, gas-containing, or corridor-tricky collections. Plan a route avoiding bowel, vessels, pleura, and nerves.
steps.title: Pick guidance and corridor

steps.text: Access with the needle, aspirate a specimen (Gram stain/culture), confirm the collection is drainable and the needle is truly within it before committing to a catheter.
steps.title: Aspirate to confirm and sample

steps.text: Seldinger for deep/adjacent-to-critical-structures precision, or single-step trocar for large superficial collections. Advance the locking pigtail fully into the cavity and form the loop.
steps.title: Place the catheter

steps.text: Deep pelvic abscess → transgluteal (through the greater sciatic foramen, medial and inferior to avoid the sciatic nerve and gluteal vessels) or transrectal/transvaginal endocavitary routes. Hepatic → transhepatic tamponade.
steps.title: Special corridors

steps.text: Aspirate the cavity to near-dryness; gentle saline irrigation until returns clear (avoid over-distension/bacteremia). Note the volume and character.
steps.title: Evacuate and irrigate

steps.text: Secure to skin, connect to bag/suction (sump for viscous/necrotic). Confirmatory imaging (contrast via catheter or CT) documents position and residual pockets; place additional drains for separate loculations.
steps.title: Secure and image

postop: Continue antibiotics; expect defervescence and clinical improvement in 24–72 h — persistence suggests undrained loculation, dislodged/clogged drain, or fistula
Flush the drain 10 mL saline BID–TID to maintain patency; record daily output
Falling output + clinical resolution + follow-up imaging showing collapse → drain removal; abrupt drop may be a clog, not a cure
Persistent high output → suspect enteric/pancreatic/biliary fistula: sinogram, treat the source (bowel rest, octreotide, definitive repair)
Reimage for non-resolving or complex collections; upsize/reposition as needed

complications.name: Bleeding along tract
complications.management: Avoid vessels on planning; most self-limit; significant bleeding → angiography/embolization.

complications.name: Bowel transgression / enteric fistula
complications.management: Small-bowel transgression often heals with the drain acting as controlled fistula; persistent high output → surgical/medical fistula management.

complications.name: Sepsis / bacteremia during manipulation
complications.management: Antibiotics before drainage; avoid over-irrigation/distension; supportive.

complications.name: Catheter dysfunction (clog/dislodge/kink)
complications.management: Routine flushing; exchange over wire; upsize for viscous collections.

complications.name: Injury to nerves/vessels (transgluteal)
complications.management: Medial-inferior approach at the sacrospinous level; sciatic/gluteal injury managed supportively/surgically.

complications.name: Pleural contamination (upper abdominal)
complications.management: Plan sub-costophrenic windows; empyema → chest tube.

pearls: The drain is only half the cure — the other half is the source. Persistent output means a communication (bowel, pancreas, biliary) until a sinogram says otherwise.
Match catheter caliber to viscosity: thin serous fluid drains through 8F; frank pus and necrosis need 12–14F and sometimes a sump.
Transgluteal safety is geography: go medial and inferior through the sacrospinous ligament, hugging the sacrum, to spare the sciatic nerve and gluteal vessels.
Improving clinically but output suddenly zero? Suspect a clogged tube before declaring victory — flush and reimage.

citations.text: Wallace MJ, Chin KW, Fletcher TB, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for percutaneous drainage/aspiration of abscess and fluid collections. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2010;21(4):431-435.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Adrenal Venous Sampling
procedures.overview: Selective catheterization of both adrenal veins to measure aldosterone and cortisol, distinguishing unilateral (surgically curable) from bilateral aldosterone excess in primary aldosteronism. The single most operator-dependent diagnostic procedure in IR — success hinges on cannulating the elusive right adrenal vein, and the whole study is interpreted through cortisol-corrected ratios [1].

labs: Confirmed primary aldosteronism (aldosterone/renin ratio + confirmatory testing) by endocrinology
Potassium repleted; antihypertensive regimen adjusted per protocol (avoid confounding agents where feasible)
Low bleeding-risk: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0 [3]
Plan cosyntropin (continuous or bolus) vs non-stimulated protocol with the lab
preop.meds: Cosyntropin stimulation (many centers) to boost cortisol gradients and stabilize secretion; avoid unnecessary sedation (stress can affect sampling — light or none).
equipment: 5F sheath, femoral access

Selective catheters: a recurve/side-hole catheter for the right adrenal vein (short, direct off the posterolateral IVC) and a long catheter (e.g., Simmons/multipurpose) for the left (drains into the left renal vein via the inferior phrenic)
Microcatheter for the delicate right adrenal vein
Labeled sample tubes; contrast; CO2 optional
indications: Primary aldosteronism confirmed biochemically, when the patient is a surgical candidate and imaging is discordant/indeterminate (to lateralize before adrenalectomy)
Discordance between CT (nodule vs hyperplasia) and biochemical severity, especially age >35
Bilateral adrenal abnormalities or normal-appearing glands with confirmed hyperaldosteronism

absolute: Primary aldosteronism not biochemically confirmed (do not sample to make the diagnosis)
Patient not a surgical candidate (result would not change management)
relative: Uncontrolled severe hypertension
Contrast allergy (premedicate; small volumes) [2]
Mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonists that could suppress the study — coordinate washout with endocrinology

steps.text: Decide simultaneous vs sequential and stimulated (cosyntropin) vs unstimulated with endocrinology. Cosyntropin infusion raises cortisol and improves selectivity confidence.
steps.title: Plan sampling protocol

steps.text: The left adrenal vein joins the inferior phrenic vein and drains into the left renal vein — catheterize the common phrenic-adrenal trunk. Confirm position with a tiny gentle contrast puff; sample aldosterone and cortisol.
steps.title: Cannulate the left adrenal vein

steps.text: The right adrenal vein is short and enters the posterolateral IVC directly, often at an acute cephalad angle near the accessory hepatic veins — a recurve/side-hole catheter or microcatheter helps. Confirm selectivity with a careful micro-contrast injection (avoid adrenal vein rupture from forceful injection).
steps.title: Cannulate the right adrenal vein (the hard part)

steps.text: Draw aldosterone + cortisol from each adrenal vein and a peripheral/IVC sample. Under cosyntropin, an adrenal-vein-to-peripheral cortisol ratio ≥5:1 (stimulated) confirms selective cannulation.
steps.title: Sample both sides and periphery

steps.text: If the selectivity index (cortisol AV:peripheral) is inadequate, re-cannulate — a non-selective sample is uninterpretable. Confirm both sides are truly adrenal vein blood.
steps.title: Verify selectivity before finishing

steps.text: Cortisol-correct the aldosterone (A/C ratio each side). Lateralization index = higher A/C ÷ lower A/C; ≥4 (stimulated) indicates unilateral disease (adrenalectomy of the high side); <3 suggests bilateral (medical therapy). Document values for endocrinology.
steps.title: Interpret lateralization

steps.text: Hemostasis; the samples ARE the deliverable — label meticulously and send per lab protocol.
steps.title: Close

postop: Routine post-angiography recovery; adrenal hemorrhage is rare but watch for flank pain
Results interpreted with endocrinology/surgery: lateralized → laparoscopic adrenalectomy; bilateral → mineralocorticoid antagonist therapy
Resume/adjust antihypertensives; monitor potassium
If cannulation failed on one side, discuss repeat AVS vs alternative (e.g., adrenal imaging, empiric management) with the team

complications.name: Failed right adrenal vein cannulation
complications.management: The main 'complication' — high-volume operators and cosyntropin improve success; repeat study or alternative strategy.

complications.name: Adrenal vein rupture / dissection
complications.management: Gentle, low-volume contrast injections prevent it; adrenal hemorrhage usually self-limited — supportive, rarely embolization.

complications.name: Adrenal hemorrhage / infarction
complications.management: From forceful injection/venospasm; conservative management; monitor adrenal function if bilateral.

complications.name: Access-site complications
complications.management: Standard care.

complications.name: Contrast reaction / AKI
complications.management: Standard prophylaxis [2].

pearls: AVS is graded on cortisol, not aldosterone: every value is cortisol-corrected, and the selectivity index (cortisol AV:peripheral) is what proves you were actually in the vein.
The right adrenal vein is the whole game — short, posterolateral, near accessory hepatic veins; reach for a recurve or microcatheter early rather than fighting a straight catheter.
A gentle contrast puff confirms position; a forceful one ruptures the vein. Respect the smallest, most delicate target in visceral IR.
Never sample to diagnose primary aldosteronism — biochemistry does that. AVS only answers 'which side?' in a confirmed, surgical patient.

citations.text: Funder JW, Carey RM, Mantero F, et al. The management of primary aldosteronism: case detection, diagnosis, and treatment — an Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(5):1889-1916.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Aortography & Lower Extremity Runoff
procedures.overview: Catheter-based contrast opacification of the abdominal aorta and stepwise imaging of the iliac, femoropopliteal, and tibial runoff — the roadmap study for PAD/CLTI intervention and a foundational fluoroscopy skill [1]. Largely diagnostic today only when CTA/MRA are equivocal or when performed in the same sitting as intervention.

labs: Low bleeding-risk procedure per SIR: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0–3.0 [2]
Creatinine/eGFR for contrast planning; hydration for CKD
Review prior CTA/duplex to plan access side and expected disease level
Document baseline pulses and ABIs
preop.meds: Local anesthesia ± moderate sedation. Hold metformin per institutional contrast policy if eGFR is reduced.
equipment: 5F access sheath; 0.035" starter and hydrophilic wires
Multi-sidehole flush catheter (pigtail/Omni Flush) for aortography
Selective catheters (Cobra, RIM, contralateral crossing catheters) for oblique/selective runs
Power injector (typical aortogram: 15–20 mL/s for 30–40 mL); DSA with bolus-chase or stepping-table capability
CO2 delivery system when iodinated contrast must be minimized
indications: Planning or performing revascularization for claudication or chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) [1]
Equivocal or artifact-degraded CTA/MRA (heavy calcification, stents, renal insufficiency limiting CTA timing)
Aortoiliac occlusive disease assessment (inflow) before femoral/distal bypass
Trauma or acute occlusion when combined with endovascular therapy
Vasculitis or embolic source workup requiring selective views

absolute: No safe arterial access with an uncorrectable coagulopathy
relative: Severe contrast allergy (premedicate; consider CO2 angiography)
eGFR <30 mL/min not on dialysis (minimize contrast; CO2 as an alternative below the diaphragm)
Uncontrolled hypertension increasing access-site risk

steps.text: Retrograde common femoral artery access on the less-diseased side (or radial for aortoiliac work) using US guidance and fluoroscopic confirmation of entry over the femoral head.
steps.title: Access

steps.text: Position the pigtail at ~L1 (above the renals for a full abdominal study; at the renals or lower for runoff planning). Power-inject DSA aortogram; assess renals, IMA, and aortoiliac segments.
steps.title: Aortogram

steps.text: Drop the catheter to the aortic bifurcation. Contralateral anterior oblique projections open the iliac bifurcations and profile the origins of the internal and external iliacs.
steps.title: Pelvic views

steps.text: Bolus-chase or stationed serial DSA of thigh, knee, and tibial stations. Use ipsilateral oblique to open the CFA/profunda bifurcation. Selective injections distal to occlusions improve tibial opacification.
steps.title: Runoff

steps.text: Localize disease by TASC/GLASS segments, measure stenoses and lesion lengths, and identify the target tibial vessel providing in-line flow to the wound (angiosome concept in CLTI) [1].
steps.title: Grade and measure

steps.text: Remove the sheath with manual compression or a closure device; document distal pulses afterward.
steps.title: Closure

postop: Bed rest per access/closure method (typically 2 h with a closure device, 4–6 h with manual compression for arterial sheaths)
Access-site and distal pulse checks; groin hematoma surveillance
Hydration; recheck renal function in CKD if a large contrast load was used
Return precautions: expanding groin mass, cold/painful limb, pulsatile bleeding

complications.name: Distal embolization
complications.management: Aspiration thrombectomy, catheter-directed lysis, or surgical embolectomy.

complications.name: Access-site hematoma / pseudoaneurysm
complications.management: US-guided compression or thrombin injection for pseudoaneurysm; transfuse/repair if large.

complications.name: Contrast-induced AKI
complications.management: Prevention: hydration, minimized contrast, CO2 substitution; supportive care.

complications.name: Dissection at access or catheter tip
complications.management: Usually antegrade flow seals retrograde dissections; stent if flow-limiting.

pearls: Never inject power contrast through an end-hole catheter in the aorta — sidehole flush catheters exist so the jet doesn't dissect the wall.
Oblique the image intensifier toward the side of interest: contralateral oblique for iliac bifurcations, ipsilateral oblique for the CFA/profunda split.
In CLTI the question is not 'is there disease' but 'which tibial vessel gives straight-line flow to the wound' — chase the runoff to the foot.
CO2 is a real contrast agent below the diaphragm: use it early in CKD rather than rationing iodine mid-case.

citations.text: Gerhard-Herman MD, Gornik HL, Barrett C, et al. 2016 AHA/ACC guideline on the management of patients with lower extremity peripheral artery disease. Circulation. 2017;135(12):e726-e779.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Arterial Access & Closure (Femoral / Radial)
procedures.overview: The foundation of every arterial intervention. Modern access is US-guided micropuncture of the common femoral artery (CFA) over the mid-femoral head, or transradial access with a spasmolytic cocktail. Most bleeding complications of angiography are access-site complications — mastered access prevents them [1,2]. The original coaxial wire-exchange concept dates to Seldinger, 1953 [3].

labs: SIR 2019 classifies standard arterial access as low bleeding-risk: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0–3.0, though many operators target ≤1.8 for sheaths ≥6F [1]
Baseline creatinine/eGFR for contrast planning (see calculators)
Document distal pulses / Barbeau before starting
preop.meds: Local lidocaine. Radial: intra-arterial verapamil 2.5 mg + nitroglycerin 100–200 mcg ± heparin 2000–5000 U to prevent spasm and radial artery occlusion.
equipment: Linear US probe, sterile cover
Micropuncture set (21G, 0.018-inch wire, transitional dilator)
4–6F vascular sheath (procedure-dependent); slender hydrophilic sheaths for radial
0.035-inch J/Bentson wire
Closure: manual compression, Angio-Seal (collagen plug), Perclose ProGlide (suture-mediated; pre-close for large bore), or radial compression band (TR Band)
indications: Any transarterial diagnostic or therapeutic procedure
Radial-first is reasonable for many visceral and pelvic interventions; femoral remains standard for large-bore work

absolute: Overlying infection at the planned access site
relative: For radial: abnormal Barbeau test (type D waveform), planned dialysis fistula in that arm, prior radial harvest
For femoral: severe iliofemoral disease or graft at the puncture site (not prohibitive — adjust technique)

steps.text: Fluoro-save the mid-femoral head; the ideal arteriotomy is over its middle third — below the inguinal ligament (retroperitoneal bleeding risk if higher) and above the bifurcation (pseudoaneurysm/AVF risk if lower).
steps.title: Landmark the CFA correctly

steps.text: Visualize the CFA, its bifurcation, and calcified plaque. Puncture the anterior wall at 45° with a 21G micropuncture needle; brisk pulsatile return confirms arterial entry.
steps.title: US-guided single-wall puncture

steps.text: Advance the 0.018-inch wire under fluoro — it should track up the iliac without resistance. Exchange for the transitional dilator, then place the working sheath over a 0.035-inch wire. Aspirate and flush the sheath.
steps.title: Wire and transition

steps.text: Barbeau test first. Puncture 2–3 cm proximal to the radial styloid, give the spasmolytic cocktail through the sheath, and use hydrophilic slender sheaths. Left radial shortens the path to visceral arteries and avoids the great vessels' origin.
steps.title: Radial variant

steps.text: Pull the sheath, compress just proximal to the skin nick over the arteriotomy: firm 3 minutes per French size as a rule of thumb, then a pressure check. Bedrest 2–6 h depending on sheath size and anticoagulation.
steps.title: Closure — manual

steps.text: Femoral angiogram through the sheath first: confirm puncture location and vessel caliber ≥5 mm without circumferential calcium. Deploy Angio-Seal or Perclose per IFU. Devices shorten bedrest to 1–2 h but do not lower major complication rates — technique still matters.
steps.title: Closure — device

steps.text: TR Band with patent hemostasis technique: inflate to occlusion, then deflate until plethysmographic flow returns while hemostasis holds — this halves radial artery occlusion.
steps.title: Radial hemostasis

postop: Access-site and distal pulse checks: q15min ×4, q30min ×2, then hourly per protocol
Bedrest per closure method; head of bed ≤30° after femoral manual compression
Hypotension + back/flank pain after femoral access is retroperitoneal hemorrhage until proven otherwise — do not wait for the hematocrit
Radial: remove band per patent-hemostasis schedule; check hand perfusion

complications.name: Groin hematoma
complications.management: Re-compress, mark margins, hold anticoagulation if feasible. Expanding → duplex US for pseudoaneurysm.

complications.name: Retroperitoneal hemorrhage
complications.management: High stick + anticoagulation classic. Resuscitate, CT if stable, reverse anticoagulation; ongoing bleeding → contralateral access, balloon tamponade ± covered stent at the puncture site.

complications.name: Pseudoaneurysm
complications.management: Duplex diagnosis. <2 cm may thrombose spontaneously; symptomatic/larger → US-guided thrombin injection (into the sac, away from the neck); wide neck or infected → surgery.

complications.name: Arteriovenous fistula
complications.management: Low puncture through artery and vein. Most small AVFs close or remain trivial; enlarging/symptomatic → covered stent or surgical repair.

complications.name: Acute limb ischemia / distal embolization
complications.management: Cold pulseless limb after closure device suggests footplate/plug occlusion or embolus: urgent angiography, aspiration/thrombectomy, surgical consult.

complications.name: Radial artery occlusion
complications.management: Usually asymptomatic due to ulnar supply. Prevent with heparin + patent hemostasis. Symptomatic ischemia is rare — anticoagulation, vascular consult.

pearls: The strongest predictor of femoral complications is puncture location — one fluoro spot of the femoral head before you stick is the cheapest insurance in IR.
Never dilate against resistance: if the 0.018 wire will not advance smoothly, re-image; you may be subintimal or in plaque.
Pre-close with two ProGlides at 10 and 2 o'clock before upsizing to large-bore (EVAR, large thrombectomy systems).
For anticoagulated patients needing urgent angiography, remember SIR 2019: most access can proceed without reversal — plan closure strategy instead [1].

citations.text: Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk — Part II: recommendations. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.
Sherev DA, Shaw RE, Brent BN. Angiographic predictors of femoral access site complications: implication for planned percutaneous coronary intervention. Catheter Cardiovasc Interv. 2005;65(2):196-202.
Seldinger SI. Catheter replacement of the needle in percutaneous arteriography: a new technique. Acta Radiol. 1953;39(5):368-376.

procedures.title: Catheter-Directed Arterial Thrombolysis
procedures.overview: Infusion of a fibrinolytic (historically urokinase, now typically alteplase/tPA) through a multi-sidehole catheter embedded in arterial thrombus, for acute limb ischemia in a limb that can tolerate the hours lysis takes. Trial data (STILE, TOPAS) established it as an alternative to surgery for acute (<14 day) occlusions [1,2] — the whole game is patient selection by Rutherford ALI category [3].

labs: Baseline CBC, coags, fibrinogen — then serial fibrinogen during infusion (trend, and slow/stop if it falls precipitously, commonly <100–150 mg/dL)
High bleeding-risk classification while lytics run [4]
Neurovascular limb exam documented (motor/sensory) — this is your Rutherford category and your trigger to abandon lysis
preop.meds: tPA typically 0.5–1 mg/h via the catheter with subtherapeutic heparin through the sheath (target anti-Xa low / PTT mildly elevated) to prevent pericatheter thrombosis. ICU/stepdown admission mandatory.
equipment: Contralateral or brachial access; long sheath over the bifurcation
Multi-sidehole infusion catheter sized to thrombus length ± infusion wire
Guidewire traversal test gear (a wire that crosses thrombus predicts lysability)
Mechanical/aspiration thrombectomy devices as adjuncts or bailout
indications: Acute limb ischemia, Rutherford I or IIa (viable/marginally threatened) from in-situ thrombosis or embolism, symptoms <14 days [1,2]
Thrombosed bypass grafts (unmasks the culprit stenosis for definitive treatment)
Thrombosed popliteal aneurysm to clear tibial runoff before repair
Adjunct to mechanical thrombectomy when residual thrombus persists

absolute: Rutherford IIb–III with immediately threatened or irreversible limb (needs immediate surgical/mechanical revascularization or amputation, not hours of infusion)
Active internal bleeding; recent stroke/intracranial surgery (~<3 months); intracranial tumor/AVM
relative: Recent major surgery/trauma or organ biopsy (<10 days)
Severe hypertension; pregnancy; endocarditis-source embolus (mycotic risk)
Coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia

steps.text: Motor and sensory exam + Doppler signals = Rutherford ALI class. IIb or III → this is not a lysis patient; call vascular surgery [3].
steps.title: Categorize the limb first

steps.text: Define the occlusion's top, length, embolic vs thrombotic morphology, and the runoff. Perform the guidewire traversal test.
steps.title: Diagnostic angiogram

steps.text: Lace the multi-sidehole catheter through the entire thrombus burden; infusion length should match clot length. Pulse-spray a small lytic bolus to start.
steps.title: Embed the infusion system

steps.text: tPA at 0.5–1 mg/h with sheath heparin; ICU monitoring with serial limb exams, access checks, and fibrinogen every 4–6 h.
steps.title: Infuse and protect

steps.text: Return to the lab: reposition the catheter into residual thrombus, escalate to mechanical adjuncts, or — the payoff — angioplasty/stent the unmasked culprit lesion.
steps.title: Lysis check at 12–24 h

steps.text: Stop when flow is restored and the culprit fixed, when fibrinogen/bleeding forces the issue, or at ~48 h (yield beyond this falls, complications climb).
steps.title: Endpoint

postop: Continue ICU-level monitoring during the entire infusion; serial limb exams — worsening ischemia or new motor deficit = immediate return or surgery
Watch for reperfusion injury and compartment syndrome after flow restoration (pain out of proportion, tense calf → fasciotomy consult)
Access-site and systemic bleeding surveillance (including neuro checks for ICH)
Transition to therapeutic anticoagulation once catheters are out; workup embolic sources

complications.name: Major bleeding (access, retroperitoneal, remote)
complications.management: Stop lytics, resuscitate, reverse (cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen), control the site.

complications.name: Intracranial hemorrhage
complications.management: Stop everything, stat CT, reversal, neurosurgery consult — the complication that kills.

complications.name: Distal embolization during lysis
complications.management: Often lyses with continued infusion; aspiration if flow-limiting.

complications.name: Compartment syndrome after reperfusion
complications.management: Fasciotomy.

complications.name: Pericatheter thrombosis
complications.management: Prevented by sheath heparin; exchange system if it occurs.

pearls: The Rutherford exam is the whole consult: viable and marginally threatened limbs buy the hours lysis needs; a IIb limb loses muscle while tPA drips [3].
Lysis is a diagnostic maneuver as much as therapy — the graft or artery thrombosed for a reason, and the lysis check exists to find and fix that lesion.
The guidewire traversal test is honest: thrombus a wire won't enter is usually thrombus tPA won't dissolve.
Urokinase is the historical answer (TOPAS trial [2]); tPA is today's drug — the principles (embed, infuse low-dose, recheck, fix the culprit) are unchanged.

citations.text: The STILE Investigators. Results of a prospective randomized trial evaluating surgery versus thrombolysis for ischemia of the lower extremity (STILE trial). Ann Surg. 1994;220(3):251-268.

Ouriel K, Veith FJ, Sasahara AA. A comparison of recombinant urokinase with vascular surgery as initial treatment for acute arterial occlusion of the legs (TOPAS). N Engl J Med. 1998;338(16):1105-1111.
Rutherford RB, Baker JD, Ernst C, et al. Recommended standards for reports dealing with lower extremity ischemia: revised version. J Vasc Surg. 1997;26(3):517-538.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Arthrography (Shoulder, Hip, Knee, Wrist, Ankle)
procedures.overview: Image-guided intra-articular injection of contrast to distend a joint before cross-sectional imaging — dilute gadolinium for MR arthrography (labral and ligament detail), iodinated contrast for CT arthrography or fluoroscopic confirmation. A quick, safe, high-throughput procedure whose entire value rests on getting the needle unequivocally intra-articular and mixing the gadolinium at the right (very dilute) concentration [1].

labs: Essentially none — one of the lowest-risk procedures [2]
Confirm the imaging plan (MR vs CT) so the correct contrast is drawn up
Screen for prior contrast reactions; MRI safety screening if MR arthrogram follows
preop.meds: Local anesthesia only. Many protocols add intra-articular lidocaine/bupivacaine (diagnostic pain response) and sometimes steroid if therapeutic.
equipment: Fluoro or US guidance (US common for shoulder/hip; CT for select joints)
22–25G spinal needle (hip/deep joints) or shorter needle (superficial)
MR arthrogram cocktail: gadolinium diluted ~1:200 in saline (+ iodinated contrast + lidocaine per recipe) — dilution is the classic pitfall
Iodinated contrast for fluoro/CT confirmation
indications: Shoulder: labral tears (Bankart, SLAP), rotator cuff, instability — MR arthrogram
Hip: labral tear, femoroacetabular impingement, cartilage assessment; diagnostic anesthetic component to localize pain
Wrist: TFCC and intrinsic ligament (scapholunate/lunotriquetral) tears
Knee: post-operative meniscus (re-tear vs healing), cartilage
Ankle: ligament and osteochondral lesion assessment; CT arthrogram when MRI contraindicated

absolute: Overlying cellulitis/infection (seeding a joint); known severe reaction to the specific contrast
relative: Bleeding diathesis (minor for a compressible joint) [2]
Suspected native joint infection (aspirate for cultures — don't inject gadolinium into possible septic arthritis)

steps.text: Standard approaches: shoulder — rotator interval or anterior glenohumeral; hip — femoral head-neck junction (avoid the medial neurovascular bundle); wrist — radiocarpal 3-4 portal; knee — retropatellar; ankle — anteromedial tibiotalar.
steps.title: Position and target

steps.text: Advance to bone/joint, inject a test of iodinated contrast under fluoro — intra-articular contrast flows away along the joint contour; a static blob means soft-tissue/extra-articular, so reposition before committing gadolinium.
steps.title: Confirm intra-articular position

steps.text: Instill the joint-appropriate volume of dilute gadolinium (or CT/iodinated contrast) to distend without overdistension — shoulder ~10–15 mL, hip ~10–15 mL, wrist ~3–5 mL, knee ~20–40 mL, ankle ~6–10 mL.
steps.title: Inject the arthrographic volume

steps.text: Send promptly to MRI (or CT) — contrast begins to be resorbed and decompress, so timely scanning preserves capsular distension.
steps.title: Move to cross-sectional imaging

postop: Warn of transient fullness/ache from distension and a possible post-injection flare (24–48 h); ice and NSAIDs suffice
If anesthetic was injected, the pain-relief response is diagnostic data — have the patient report it
Return precautions for the rare septic joint: escalating pain, swelling, fever, redness
No activity restriction beyond comfort

complications.name: Septic arthritis
complications.management: Sterile technique; escalating hot painful joint + fever = urgent aspiration and antibiotics.

complications.name: Post-injection pain flare
complications.management: Self-limited; ice, NSAIDs; reassure.

complications.name: Vasovagal reaction
complications.management: Supine, fluids, observation.

complications.name: Contrast reaction
complications.management: Standard management; dilute intra-articular gadolinium is very low risk.

complications.name: Extra-articular injection (non-diagnostic study)
complications.management: The test-injection confirmation step prevents it — always verify flow before the gadolinium.

pearls: Test-inject a drop of iodinated contrast first: intra-articular contrast runs away with the joint contour, extravasated contrast pools — this two-second check saves the whole study.
MR arthrogram gadolinium is extraordinarily dilute (~1:200) — injecting MRI-strength gadolinium ruins the scan with T2 shortening (black joint).
The hip needle targets the head-neck junction laterally, deliberately away from the medial femoral neurovascular bundle.
If the joint might be infected, aspirate and culture — never inject arthrographic contrast into a possible septic joint.

citations.text: Steinbach LS, Palmer WE, Schweitzer ME. Special focus session: MR arthrography. RadioGraphics. 2002;22(5):1223-1246.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Breast Biopsy & Preoperative Localization
procedures.overview: Image-guided sampling and pre-surgical targeting of breast lesions: US-guided core biopsy for sonographically visible masses, stereotactic (x-ray/tomosynthesis) vacuum-assisted biopsy for calcifications, and MRI-guided biopsy for MR-only findings — plus preoperative localization (wire or radioactive/magnetic seed) of non-palpable lesions for the surgeon. Two non-negotiables: leave a clip marker at every biopsy, and confirm radiologic-pathologic concordance afterward [1].

labs: Low bleeding-risk; hold/adjust anticoagulation only per standard low-risk guidance [2]
Confirm the target lesion and BI-RADS category; decide guidance modality by best visualization
For localization: coordinate timing and laterality with the operating surgeon
preop.meds: Local anesthesia (with epinephrine to limit bleeding/obscuration). No sedation typically.
equipment: US probe + spring-loaded core device (US-guided); stereotactic/tomosynthesis table + vacuum-assisted device (calcifications); MRI-compatible grid/device (MR)
Biopsy clip markers (mandatory)
Localization hardware: hookwire, or radioactive seed (I-125)/magnetic/RFID reflector systems
Specimen radiography unit (to confirm calcifications/seed within the specimen)
indications: Tissue diagnosis of a suspicious lesion (BI-RADS 4–5): mass, suspicious calcifications, architectural distortion, or MRI-detected enhancement
US-guided core for sonographically visible masses/nodes (also axillary staging)
Stereotactic vacuum-assisted biopsy for calcifications/distortion seen only mammographically
MRI-guided biopsy for MR-only findings
Preoperative localization (wire or seed) of a non-palpable lesion before excision/lumpectomy

absolute: None absolute for a compressible superficial target
relative: Anticoagulation (compressible, low-risk — but a breast hematoma obscures future imaging; manage per protocol) [2]
Lesion not reliably visible on the chosen modality (switch guidance)
Inability to position for stereotactic (thin breast, posterior lesion) — consider alternative approach

steps.text: US for masses (fast, no radiation, real-time); stereotactic vacuum-assisted for calcifications/distortion; MRI for MR-only lesions. The modality is dictated by where the lesion is best seen.
steps.title: Choose guidance by visibility

steps.text: Local with epinephrine; enter parallel to the chest wall (US) or per stereotactic coordinates, keeping the device trajectory away from the pectoralis and implants.
steps.title: Anesthetize and access

steps.text: Multiple cores (US) or contiguous vacuum-assisted samples (stereotactic) targeting the lesion/calcifications; radiograph vacuum specimens to confirm calcifications were captured.
steps.title: Sample

steps.text: Place a marker clip at the site every time — it lets the surgeon find a small or post-chemo lesion and identifies the biopsied site on future imaging [1].
steps.title: Deploy a clip marker

steps.text: Position the hookwire or seed/reflector at the lesion (or its clip) under the same guidance; document distance from skin/nipple for the surgeon; obtain a post-placement mammogram.
steps.title: Preoperative localization (when indicated)

steps.text: After pathology returns, decide radiologic-pathologic concordance: a benign result for a BI-RADS 5 lesion is discordant and mandates rebiopsy or excision [1].
steps.title: Confirm concordance

postop: Compression + ice; expect bruising — significant hematoma is uncommon (epinephrine + compression prevent)
Mammogram after biopsy/localization documents clip/wire position
Communicate results and the concordance determination explicitly; benign-discordant results are surgically excised
For localization: patient proceeds to surgery same day; specimen radiograph confirms the target and marker are within the excised tissue

complications.name: Hematoma
complications.management: Compression, ice; large hematomas obscure follow-up imaging — prevention matters more than treatment.

complications.name: Infection
complications.management: Sterile technique; antibiotics for the rare cellulitis/abscess.

complications.name: Clip/wire migration
complications.management: Post-placement mammogram documents position; surgeon informed of any migration; specimen radiograph confirms retrieval.

complications.name: Vasovagal reaction (upright stereotactic)
complications.management: Recline, fluids; prone tables reduce it.

complications.name: Radiologic-pathologic discordance
complications.management: The critical post-procedure judgment: discordant benign results are re-biopsied or excised, never accepted [1].

complications.name: Pneumothorax
complications.management: Keep the trajectory parallel to the chest wall; manage per size.

pearls: A clip goes in every biopsy cavity — post-chemo complete responders and tiny cancers vanish on imaging, and the surgeon needs a target left behind [1].
Concordance is a required read-out, not an afterthought: a 'benign' core from a BI-RADS 5 mass is wrong until re-excision proves otherwise.
Radiograph the vacuum specimen for calcifications before you leave — no calcifications in the specimen means you missed the target.
Match guidance to visibility: don't force a stereotactic biopsy on a mass you can see beautifully on US, and don't chase MR-only enhancement with ultrasound.

citations.text: Bruening W, Fontanarosa J, Tipton K, et al. Systematic review: comparative effectiveness of core-needle and open surgical biopsy to diagnose breast lesions. Ann Intern Med. 2010;152(4):238-246.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Bronchial Artery Embolization
procedures.overview: Embolization of hypertrophied bronchial and nonbronchial systemic collaterals for life-threatening or recurrent hemoptysis — most commonly from bronchiectasis, TB (active or sequelae), cystic fibrosis, or aspergilloma. Death from hemoptysis is by asphyxiation, not exsanguination; airway control comes first. The feared complication is spinal cord ischemia from anterior medullary branches [1].

labs: CTA chest: identifies bleeding side, bronchial artery anatomy at T5–T6, nonbronchial collaterals (intercostal, internal mammary, thyrocervical, phrenic)
CBC, coags; correct toward platelets ≥20–50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0 [3]
ABG/oxygenation; anesthesia consult — consider intubation, bleeding-side-down positioning, bronchial blocker
Sputum/micro data (TB isolation status matters for the team)
preop.meds: Airway first: intubation for massive hemoptysis, affected side down. Moderate sedation only for stable patients.
equipment: 5F sheath, aortic flush pigtail
Selective catheters: Mickelson, Cobra, Simmons 1–2 for bronchial ostia
Microcatheter (essential — embolize distal to spinal/mediastinal branches)
Particles 300–500 μm PVA or spheres (NOT coils as primary therapy; not liquid unless expert)
CO2 detector-level vigilance for reflux; cone-beam CT helpful
indications: Massive/life-threatening hemoptysis (commonly >100–300 mL/24 h or any volume with airway compromise)
Recurrent moderate hemoptysis impairing quality of life
Preoperative control before definitive resection
CF-related hemoptysis (recurrence is the rule — embolization is disease management)

absolute: Culprit artery giving rise to a visible spinal medullary branch that cannot be avoided distally
relative: Bleeding from pulmonary artery source (Rasmussen aneurysm) — different target, treat the PA
Severe contrast allergy without time to premedicate [2]

steps.text: Airway protected, bleeding side down. Review CTA for culprit side and every systemic feeder — chronic inflammatory lung recruits nonbronchial collaterals through pleural adhesions.
steps.title: Stabilize and localize

steps.text: Optional but useful when CTA is unavailable: pigtail at T4–T5 shows hypertrophied bronchial ostia (normal bronchial artery ≤1.5 mm; abnormal >2–3 mm).
steps.title: Thoracic aortogram

steps.text: Typical origins at T5–T6: right often from a common intercostobronchial trunk (ICBT), left directly from the aorta. Gentle selective injection: look for hypervascularity, shunting, extravasation (rare — its absence does not exclude the culprit).
steps.title: Select the bronchial artery

steps.text: On every run, look for the hairpin turn of an anterior medullary artery (artery of Adamkiewicz, classically left T8–L1, but medullary branches can arise from the ICBT). If present, advance the microcatheter distal to its origin — or abandon that vessel.
steps.title: Hunt the spinal branch

steps.text: Microcatheter beyond spinal/esophageal/mediastinal branches, then 300–500 μm particles in dilute contrast injected under continuous fluoro until near-stasis. No reflux. Coils are avoided proximally — they block future access to a recurrent bleeder.
steps.title: Embolize with particles

steps.text: Systematically interrogate and treat all abnormal vessels on the bleeding side, including nonbronchial systemic collaterals (intercostals, IMA, thyrocervical trunk). Recurrence usually means an untreated feeder.
steps.title: Treat every feeder

steps.text: Final runs confirm devascularization of the hypervascular territory. Neuro check on the table (feet moving?) before and after.
steps.title: Completion

postop: ICU observation; serial neuro checks documenting lower extremity function
Expect minor residual blood-streaked sputum for days; recurrent frank hemoptysis → re-imaging/re-angio
Chest pain and dysphagia are common transient post-embolization effects
Treat the underlying disease (TB therapy, CF care, antifungals for aspergilloma — embolization is a bridge)
Recurrence 10–30% by 6–12 months — counsel and arrange follow-up [1]

complications.name: Spinal cord ischemia
complications.management: The catastrophe. Prevent: microcatheter position distal to any medullary branch, particles ≥300 μm, no reflux. If deficits occur: emergent MRI, blood pressure augmentation, steroids controversial, neurology consult.

complications.name: Chest pain / dysphagia
complications.management: Intercostal and esophageal branch ischemia — supportive, self-limited days.

complications.name: Recurrent hemoptysis
complications.management: Repeat CTA and embolization; look for recanalization, missed nonbronchial collaterals, or PA-source bleeding; consider surgery for localized resectable disease.

complications.name: Bronchial/aortic dissection
complications.management: From ostial engagement — usually managed conservatively; lose the vessel, come back another day.

complications.name: Nontarget embolization (esophagus, mediastinum)
complications.management: Supportive; prevention is distal position and controlled injection.

pearls: Absence of extravasation is expected — treat the abnormal vessel and territory, not just active bleeding.
The right intercostobronchial trunk is the highest-stakes vessel in the chest: it is the classic origin of medullary branches. Distal microcatheter position is non-negotiable.
In CF, plan for repeat visits over a lifetime: particles, not coils, preserve the roadmap.
Persistent hemoptysis after good systemic embolization? Think Rasmussen pseudoaneurysm — swing to the pulmonary artery.

citations.text: Panda A, Bhalla AS, Goyal A. Bronchial artery embolization in hemoptysis: a systematic review. Diagn Interv Radiol. 2017;23(4):307-317.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Carotid & Cerebral Angiography
procedures.overview: Selective catheterization and DSA of the great vessels and intracranial circulation — still the gold standard for aneurysm characterization, vasospasm, AVMs/AVFs, and pre-treatment planning, though CTA/MRA now handle most screening. Meticulous technique matters more here than anywhere else in angiography: the complication that defines this study is stroke [1,2].

labs: Low bleeding-risk per SIR: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0–3.0 [3]
Creatinine/eGFR; neuro exam documented pre-procedure as the comparison baseline
Review CTA/MRA to plan arch strategy (bovine arch, type III arch, tortuosity)
preop.meds: Local ± light sedation (keep the patient examinable). Systemic heparinization is used by many operators for prolonged selective work.
equipment: 5F sheath; continuously flushed (heparinized saline) coaxial system — double-flush discipline throughout
Diagnostic catheters: vertebral/Berenstein for favorable arches, Simmons 2 for difficult arches
0.035" hydrophilic wire; biplane DSA with 3D rotational capability
Meticulous air management: bubble-free syringes, closed flush systems
indications: Aneurysm detection/characterization after SAH; follow-up after coiling/clipping
Vasospasm assessment ± intra-arterial therapy after aneurysmal SAH
AVM/dural AV fistula characterization and treatment planning
Carotid stenosis when noninvasive studies disagree, or pre-CAS planning
Acute stroke thrombectomy (as the diagnostic run preceding intervention)
Tumor embolization planning (meningioma, paraganglioma)

absolute: No safe arterial access
relative: Severe contrast allergy (premedicate)
Renal insufficiency (biplane DSA reduces contrast; stage the study)
Uncorrected coagulopathy — low-risk procedure, but intracranial consequences of thromboembolism amplify everything

steps.text: Femoral (or radial) access. Review or perform an arch aortogram (LAO 30–45°) if anatomy is unknown to choose catheter shape and plan vessel order.
steps.title: Access and arch assessment

steps.text: Select each target origin over the wire — never advance a catheter in the arch without wire leadership. Reform the Simmons in the arch or subclavian when needed.
steps.title: Great vessel selection

steps.text: AP and lateral cervical DSA of each common carotid: bifurcation stenosis (NASCET measurement), dissection flaps, ulceration.
steps.title: Common carotid runs

steps.text: Advance to the ICA under roadmap if intracranial detail is required. Standard intracranial projections: AP (Towne), lateral; add obliques/3D rotational for aneurysm necks.
steps.title: Selective ICA/ECA and intracranial views

steps.text: Select the dominant vertebral (usually left); image the vertebrobasilar system and posterior fossa in AP/lateral.
steps.title: Posterior circulation

steps.text: Double-flush after every wire exchange, aspirate before injecting, keep the sheath on pressurized flush — thromboembolism is the enemy.
steps.title: Continuous anticoagulation hygiene

steps.text: Close the access site and perform/document a focused neuro exam on the table.
steps.title: Closure and immediate neuro check

postop: Neuro checks with vitals (e.g., q15 min × 1 h, then spaced) — any new deficit is a code-stroke evaluation
Access-site surveillance and bed rest per closure method
Hydration for contrast
Return precautions: focal weakness, speech/visual change, severe headache

complications.name: Neurologic event (TIA/stroke)
complications.management: Immediate stroke workup; if embolic occlusion is seen on the table, proceed to thrombectomy/IA therapy [1,2].

complications.name: Access-site hematoma/pseudoaneurysm
complications.management: Compression or thrombin injection.

complications.name: Air embolism
complications.management: Prevention is everything: bubble-free technique; if it occurs — 100% O2, consider hyperbaric therapy.

complications.name: Cortical blindness / transient contrast neurotoxicity
complications.management: Self-limited over hours–days; supportive; exclude embolic infarct first.

complications.name: Carotid/vertebral dissection
complications.management: Usually heals with antithrombotics; stent if flow-limiting.

pearls: Risk lives in catheter time and wire exchanges in the arch — plan every run before you start and keep selective time short.
The higher-risk patients (recent stroke, frequent TIAs, SAH) are exactly the ones being studied — quote the neurologic risk honestly during consent [2].
Reform the Simmons once and keep it: repeated reformations scrape the arch.
A neuro exam costs nothing — do one before the drapes go on and the moment they come off.

citations.text: Willinsky RA, Taylor SM, TerBrugge K, et al. Neurologic complications of cerebral angiography: prospective analysis of 2,899 procedures and review of the literature. Radiology. 2003;227(2):522-528.
Kaufmann TJ, Huston J 3rd, Mandrekar JN, et al. Complications of diagnostic cerebral angiography: evaluation of 19,826 consecutive patients. Radiology. 2007;243(3):812-819.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Central Venous Catheters (PICC, Non-Tunneled & Tunneled Lines)
procedures.overview: The family of central venous access devices short of a port: PICCs, non-tunneled centrally inserted catheters (CICCs, including temporary dialysis and pheresis lines), and cuffed tunneled catheters (Hickman/Broviac for infusion, pheresis catheters for apheresis). Device choice is a matching problem — duration, infusate, flow rate, and infection risk — codified in the MAGIC criteria [1]. Removal of cuffed lines is its own small procedure with its own complications.

labs: Low bleeding-risk per SIR: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0–3.0 [2]
Review prior lines/venograms — chronically catheterized patients have occluded stems; US the neck before promising a side
For dialysis-pathway patients: right IJ preferred, arms untouched
preop.meds: Local anesthesia; sedation optional for tunneled lines. No routine antibiotic prophylaxis for placement.
equipment: US for puncture; fluoro for tip positioning
Micropuncture set; peel-away sheath sized to device
Device: 4–6F PICC, 7–8F CICC, 13.5–14.5F temporary dialysis/pheresis, cuffed tunneled catheter (Hickman single/double, large-bore staggered-tip pheresis)
Tunneling device for cuffed lines; ChloraPrep, maximal sterile barrier (CLABSI bundle)
indications: PICC: intermediate-duration IV therapy (weeks — antibiotics, TPN) in patients without CKD concerns [1]
Non-tunneled CICC: short-term ICU access, temporary dialysis/pheresis, emergent access
Tunneled cuffed catheter (Hickman/Broviac): months of therapy — chemotherapy with frequent draws, stem-cell infusion, long-term TPN
Tunneled pheresis catheter: apheresis/stem-cell collection requiring high flows (large-bore, staggered tips)
Removal: therapy complete, infection (CLABSI), malfunction, or fracture

absolute: Infection overlying the planned tract; no patent central veins on the planned side
relative: CKD stage ≥3b or dialysis candidacy — spare the arm veins, avoid PICCs entirely (vein preservation for future fistula) [1]
Bacteremia when placing a tunneled device — defer until cultures clear (48 h negative) unless emergent
Severe coagulopathy (though this is a low-risk, compressible procedure with US)

steps.text: Run the MAGIC logic: duration, infusate (vesicant/TPN needs central tip), flow requirement (pheresis/dialysis need large bore), renal future (spare arms). Wrong-device consults are the most common consult of all [1].
steps.title: Match device to indication

steps.text: Basilic/brachial vein above the elbow for PICC; low right IJ for CICC/tunneled lines. Micropuncture, wire to the cavoatrial junction under fluoro.
steps.title: US-guided puncture

steps.text: Create a lateral chest wall tunnel exit; pull the catheter through so the cuff sits ~1–2 cm inside the exit site. Cuff position determines both security and ease of future removal.
steps.title: Tunnel (cuffed lines)

steps.text: Tip at the cavoatrial junction (right tracheobronchial angle + ~2 vertebral bodies as a fluoro landmark); dialysis/pheresis tips ride deeper (high right atrium) for flow. Staggered tips: arterial lumen up.
steps.title: Measure and position the tip

steps.text: Peel away, flush/lock all lumens (heparin or citrate per protocol), secure (StatLock/suture), sterile dressing. Document tip location and external length.
steps.title: Secure and document

steps.text: Local anesthesia around the cuff, blunt dissection to free the cuff from scar (the actual work of removal), gentle traction, pressure, occlusive dressing. Never yank a stuck catheter — cutdown on the cuff instead.
steps.title: Removal of cuffed lines

postop: Line usable immediately once tip position is confirmed
CLABSI vigilance: any fever in a line patient = cultures ± line evaluation; tunnel/exit-site erythema exam
Teach the patient: keep dry, no submersion; heparin/citrate locks per device
After removal: occlusive dressing over the tract 24–48 h (air embolism prevention); retained cuff fragments are benign and can stay

complications.name: Central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI)
complications.management: Cultures; salvage with antibiotic locks for tunneled lines in selected organisms, otherwise remove. Prevention = insertion bundle + fewest lumens.

complications.name: Catheter-related venous thrombosis
complications.management: Anticoagulate with the line in if still needed; remove when therapy allows. Smallest catheter in the largest vein prevents.

complications.name: Pneumothorax
complications.management: US guidance made subclavian blind sticks obsolete; manage per size/symptoms.

complications.name: Air embolism
complications.management: Trendelenburg + left lateral decubitus, 100% O2, aspirate via catheter if possible. Prevent with valved sheaths, humming/Valsalva during exposure, occlusive dressing after removal.

complications.name: Malposition / tip migration
complications.management: Reposition under fluoro; PICCs migrate with arm motion — recheck when infusion alarms.

complications.name: Catheter fracture/embolization (pinch-off in subclavian lines)
complications.management: Endovascular snare retrieval; avoid medial subclavian insertion route.

complications.name: Stuck tunneled catheter at removal
complications.management: Cutdown over the cuff, sharp dissection; never apply escalating traction (fracture/embolization risk).

pearls: The most important decision happens before the drape: right device, right duration, right patient — memorize the MAGIC 'don'ts' (no PICC for <5 days; no PICC ever in a CKD/dialysis-pathway arm) [1].
Cavoatrial junction on fluoro ≈ two vertebral bodies below the carina — carina alone leaves TPN and vesicants too high in the SVC.
Fever + tunneled line: exit-site infections may be salvaged with antibiotics; tunnel-tract infections mean the line comes out, full stop.
Air embolism is a removal complication too: flat or Trendelenburg positioning, Valsalva at withdrawal, and immediate occlusive dressing every single time.

citations.text: Chopra V, Flanders SA, Saint S, et al. The Michigan Appropriateness Guide for Intravenous Catheters (MAGIC): results from a multispecialty panel using the RAND/UCLA appropriateness method. Ann Intern Med. 2015;163(6 Suppl):S1-S40.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Implantable Chest Port Placement
procedures.overview: A totally implanted venous access device: a subcutaneous reservoir on the anterior chest wall connected to a central venous catheter with the tip at the cavoatrial junction (CAJ). Preferred for long-term intermittent therapy (chemotherapy, prolonged antibiotics) because of low infection rates and full implantation. Classified as a low bleeding-risk procedure by SIR [1].

labs: CBC: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L per SIR low-risk threshold; many operators prefer ≥50 for pocket hemostasis [1]
INR ≤2.0–3.0 acceptable for low bleeding-risk procedures [1]
Anticoagulation generally does NOT need to be held for low-risk access procedures per SIR 2019 [1]
Review prior chest imaging and any prior central lines for stenosis/occlusion
preop.meds: Single-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., cefazolin 1 g IV) is common practice though not uniformly required by SIR guidance; follow institutional policy [2]. Local lidocaine; moderate sedation typical.
equipment: High-frequency linear US probe with sterile cover
Micropuncture set (21G needle, 0.018-inch wire, 4–5F transitional dilator)
0.035-inch J-wire or Bentson wire
Port kit: single- or dual-lumen port, 6–9.5F silicone/polyurethane catheter, peel-away sheath, tunneler
Non-coring Huber needle for access check
10 U/mL–100 U/mL heparin or saline lock per institution
2-0/3-0 absorbable sutures; skin adhesive or Steri-Strips
indications: Long-term intermittent venous access for chemotherapy
Prolonged antibiotic courses (e.g., cystic fibrosis, osteomyelitis)
Frequent transfusions or phlebotomy with poor peripheral access
Serial infusion therapy (e.g., immunoglobulin, biologics)

absolute: Active bacteremia or untreated systemic infection
Cellulitis or radiation dermatitis overlying the planned pocket site
relative: Severe uncorrectable coagulopathy
Profound neutropenia (ANC <500–1000) — coordinate timing with oncology
Central venous occlusion on the planned side (check prior imaging)
Anticipated ipsilateral surgery, radiation, or dialysis fistula

steps.text: Supine, head turned slightly away. Right IJ preferred (straightest course to the CAJ). Prep neck and chest in one field. US survey confirms IJ patency and compressibility.
steps.title: Time-out and positioning

steps.text: Access the low internal jugular vein with a 21G micropuncture needle under real-time US. Advance the 0.018-inch wire under fluoroscopy; exchange for the transitional dilator, then a 0.035-inch wire advanced to the IVC to secure position.
steps.title: US-guided venous access

steps.text: Infiltrate lidocaine generously on the anterior chest 2–3 cm below the clavicle, medial to the deltopectoral groove. Make a 2.5–3 cm incision and develop a subcutaneous pocket on the pectoralis fascia sized to the port — snug enough to prevent flipping.
steps.title: Create the pocket

steps.text: Tunnel the catheter from the pocket to the neck venotomy site. Measure against the wire under fluoro so the tip lands at the cavoatrial junction (roughly 2 vertebral bodies below the carina); cut to length.
steps.title: Tunnel and measure

steps.text: Serially dilate; advance the peel-away sheath over the wire. Remove dilator and wire with the patient in slight Valsalva or breath-hold (air embolism precaution — pinch the sheath). Advance the catheter as the sheath is peeled and withdrawn.
steps.title: Peel-away sheath and catheter delivery

steps.text: Trim and lock the catheter to the port stem per kit design. Access the septum with a Huber needle: brisk blood aspiration from each lumen, flush without pocket swelling, then lock with heparinized saline or saline per institution.
steps.title: Connect and test

steps.text: Fluoroscopic spot image documents tip position at the CAJ without kinks. Close the pocket in two layers (deep dermal absorbable, then subcuticular), adhesive dressing. Neck site needs only Steri-Strips.
steps.title: Confirm tip and close

postop: Port may be used immediately if accessed at placement; otherwise allow initial edema to settle before first access
Access only with non-coring (Huber) needles
Flush/lock every 4–6 weeks when not in use, per institutional protocol
Incision care: keep dry 48 h; no submersion until healed
Educate patient on red flags: fever, pocket erythema/swelling, ipsilateral arm swelling

complications.name: Pneumothorax
complications.management: Upright expiratory CXR if suspected; small/asymptomatic → observe; enlarging or symptomatic → small-bore chest tube.

complications.name: Pocket hematoma
complications.management: Pressure, ice, hold antiplatelets if feasible. Rarely requires evacuation — expanding hematoma threatens skin flap and device.

complications.name: Catheter-associated bloodstream infection
complications.management: Blood cultures peripheral + through port. S. aureus, fungal, or tunnel/pocket infection → explant. Some coagulase-negative staph infections may be salvaged with antibiotic lock therapy.

complications.name: Catheter malfunction / fibrin sheath
complications.management: Withdrawal occlusion classic for fibrin sheath: tPA 2 mg dwell 30–120 min, repeat once; refractory → catheter venogram, sheath disruption/stripping, or exchange.

complications.name: Catheter tip migration
complications.management: Suspect with new withdrawal failure or neck/ear flushing sensation; CXR confirms. Reposition with snare or exchange.

complications.name: Venous thrombosis (catheter-related)
complications.management: Duplex US; anticoagulate ≥3 months if functional and needed; device removal if infected, malfunctioning, or therapy completed.

pearls: Tip position is dynamic: it moves cephalad when the patient stands and with arm adduction — err deep (CAJ to upper RA) especially in obese patients or women with large breasts.
Avoid the subclavian route when possible: pinch-off syndrome between clavicle and first rib can fracture the catheter with fragment embolization.
Left-sided ports must cross the SVC — ensure the tip is not abutting the lateral SVC wall (erosion risk); a gentle curve to the CAJ is the goal.
If aspiration works but the patient reports gurgling or the injection is sluggish, screen the entire catheter for kinks at the venotomy or tunnel angle.

citations.text: Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions — Part II: recommendations. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Chehab MA, Thakor AS, Tulin-Silver S, et al. Adult and pediatric antibiotic prophylaxis during vascular and interventional radiology procedures: Society of Interventional Radiology practice parameter. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2018;29(11):1483-1501.
Dariushnia SR, Wallace MJ, Siddiqi NH, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for central venous access. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2010;21(7):976-981.

procedures.title: Percutaneous Cholecystostomy
procedures.overview: Image-guided decompression of the gallbladder for acute cholecystitis in patients too ill or high-risk for immediate cholecystectomy. A temporizing bridge to interval surgery (or definitive therapy in the acalculous/critically ill). The transhepatic route through the bare area is the workhorse for tract stability [1].

labs: HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [3]
CBC, LFTs, blood cultures if septic
US/CT confirming cholecystitis and planning transhepatic window
Antibiotic coverage in progress
preop.meds: Broad-spectrum antibiotics; sedation/analgesia; local anesthesia.
equipment: US + fluoro; micropuncture or one-stick trocar kit
8–10F locking pigtail drainage catheter
0.018/0.035 wires, dilators (Seldinger technique)
Contrast for cholangiogram; specimen tubes for bile culture
indications: Acute calculous cholecystitis in a poor surgical candidate (critically ill, severe comorbidity)
Acute acalculous cholecystitis (classically the ICU patient — often definitive)
Gallbladder empyema/perforation temporization; sepsis source control
Access for subsequent stone removal or cholangiographic evaluation

absolute: No safe access window; uncorrectable severe coagulopathy in non-emergent setting
relative: Large-volume ascites (drain/tamponade issues)
Gallbladder packed with stones leaving no lumen to target
Coagulopathy — correct given high bleeding-risk classification [2]

steps.text: Transhepatic (through hepatic segments IVb/V and the gallbladder bare area) is preferred — the liver tamponades the tract and reduces bile leak/gallbladder mobility. Transperitoneal is an alternative when transhepatic is unsafe (e.g., interposed structures).
steps.title: Choose the route

steps.text: US window through the liver to the gallbladder body/neck. Local anesthesia along the tract, breath-hold coaching.
steps.title: Plan and anesthetize

steps.text: Seldinger: micropuncture needle transhepatically into the gallbladder lumen, aspirate bile (send culture), advance the 0.018 then 0.035 wire, coil in the lumen. (Trocar technique is a faster one-step alternative for large, superficial GBs.)
steps.title: Access the gallbladder

steps.text: Serially dilate the tract; advance the 8–10F locking pigtail over the wire into the gallbladder lumen and form/lock the pigtail.
steps.title: Dilate and place drain

steps.text: Aspirate to decompress. Defer or minimize cholangiographic contrast injection acutely — raising pressure in an inflamed, infected gallbladder promotes bacteremia and leak.
steps.title: Decompress, don't over-distend

steps.text: Secure the catheter to skin; gentle contrast (later, when stable) confirms intraluminal position and can assess cystic-duct patency for management planning.
steps.title: Secure and confirm

postop: Continue antibiotics; expect clinical improvement within 24–72 h (fever/WBC/pain) — failure to improve suggests wrong diagnosis, obstructed drain, or another source
Maintain drain to gravity; flush protocol per institution; monitor output
Leave the tube in ≥2–6 weeks to mature a tract before removal or exchange (early removal risks bile peritonitis)
Cystic duct patency and cholecystogram guide interval cholecystectomy vs tube removal vs long-term management
In surgical candidates, interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy remains definitive [1]

complications.name: Bile leak / bile peritonitis
complications.management: Transhepatic route and mature tract before removal reduce risk; established peritonitis → drainage, antibiotics, surgery if severe.

complications.name: Hemorrhage / hemobilia
complications.management: Transhepatic vascular injury: usually self-limited; persistent bleeding → angiography/embolization of hepatic artery branch.

complications.name: Vagal/hemodynamic reaction
complications.management: From gallbladder manipulation — pause, atropine ready, fluids.

complications.name: Tube dislodgement
complications.management: Before tract maturation this can cause peritonitis; secure well, counsel patient; replace over wire if tract established.

complications.name: Catheter-related sepsis flare
complications.management: Avoid over-distension; antibiotics; ensure decompression.

complications.name: Pleural transgression (high approach)
complications.management: Plan the window below the pleural reflection; manage pneumothorax if it occurs.

pearls: Transhepatic through the bare area is the default: the liver plugs the tract and anchors a mobile organ — fewer leaks, fewer dislodgements.
Decompress, then investigate: contrast under pressure in an infected gallbladder is how you turn cholecystitis into bacteremia.
Don't pull the tube early. Two-to-six weeks for tract maturation is the difference between a clinic visit and a bile-peritonitis admission.
Acalculous cholecystitis in the ICU is the classic 'definitive' cholecystostomy — many never need surgery.

citations.text: Saad WEA, Wallace MJ, Wojak JC, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography, biliary drainage, and percutaneous cholecystostomy. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2010;21(6):789-795.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Dialysis Access Intervention (Fistulogram, Angioplasty, Declot)
procedures.overview: Maintenance of hemodialysis access: diagnostic fistulography, angioplasty of stenoses, thrombectomy of clotted circuits, and stent-grafts for recalcitrant lesions. The access is the patient's lifeline — KDOQI frames every intervention around the long-term access life-plan [1].

labs: K+ and volume status — the declot patient often needs same-day dialysis after you finish
Platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0–3.0 (low bleeding-risk) [3]
Access history: creation date, prior interventions, surveillance flow trends
Physical exam: thrill/pulse character localizes the lesion before any needle (water-hammer pulse = outflow stenosis; flat = inflow)
preop.meds: Heparin 3000–5000 U for declots; local anesthesia; light sedation. Prophylactic antibiotics for declots in many practices (clot cultures positive surprisingly often).
equipment: Micropuncture; 6–7F sheaths (often two, crossed toward arterial and venous limbs)
High-pressure balloons (up to 20–30 atm) sized to vessel (6–8 mm cephalic/graft; 8–12 mm central)
Drug-coated balloons for recurrent lesions (IN.PACT AV data) [4]
Declot tools: Fogarty/over-the-wire balloon for arterial plug, aspiration catheters, ± rheolytic device
Stent-grafts (e.g., covered nitinol) for graft-vein anastomotic restenosis, ruptures, cephalic arch
tPA 2–4 mg aliquots for lyse-and-wait or intra-procedural lacing
indications: Dysfunction: flows <500–600 mL/min (graft) or falling trend, prolonged bleeding after decannulation, elevated venous pressures, difficult cannulation, arm swelling
Thrombosed fistula/graft (declot — ideally within days to preserve options)
Immature fistula (assisted maturation: treat juxta-anastomotic stenosis, ligate competing veins)
Steal syndrome / central stenosis evaluation

absolute: Access infection (do not instrument through infected graft — surgical problem)
Right-to-left shunt with planned declot (paradoxical embolization risk) — individualized
relative: Severe hyperkalemia/volume overload → dialyze first via temporary access if needed; coordinate timing
Contrast allergy (premedicate; CO2 works well in the arm with caution) [2]

steps.text: Physical exam predicts the lesion. Fistulogram must cover arterial anastomosis → cannulation zone → outflow → central veins to the right atrium. Central stenosis hides above the collar bone.
steps.title: Examine, then image the whole circuit

steps.text: Two access points aimed at each other cover the whole circuit: one toward the arterial anastomosis, one toward the outflow/central veins.
steps.title: Crossed-sheath setup

steps.text: High-pressure balloon sized 1:1 (or slightly over) to adjacent normal vein; prolonged 1–2 min inflations; efface the waist completely. Juxta-anastomotic (fistula) and graft-vein anastomosis (graft) are the classic lesions.
steps.title: Angioplasty the culprit

steps.text: For thrombosed access: heparinize; lace clot with tPA or aspirate; angioplasty the underlying venous stenosis (there is always one — usually the anastomosis/outflow) before restoring inflow.
steps.title: Declot: clear the venous limb

steps.text: The rubbery platelet-rich plug at the arterial anastomosis is the flow switch: pull it with an over-the-wire Fogarty into the graft, macerate/aspirate. Back-bleeding then a thrill confirm inflow.
steps.title: Declot: mobilize the arterial plug

steps.text: Recoil >30% or rupture → stent-graft (covered) across the lesion (avoid jailing cannulation zones when possible; cephalic arch is a classic stent-graft site). Rupture: immediately re-tamponade with the balloon, then covered stent.
steps.title: Treat residuals and rescue problems

steps.text: Endpoint: restored thrill (not pulse), brisk flow on completion imaging, resolution of the pressure gradient. Hemostasis with purse-string sutures preserves the access for tomorrow's cannulation.
steps.title: Confirm function

postop: Access can typically be used for dialysis the same day — communicate directly with the unit
Document the circuit map: lesions treated, balloon/stent sizes — the next operator inherits your notes
Purse-string sutures out after dialysis; monitor for pseudoaneurysm at cannulation zones
Surveillance per KDOQI: clinical monitoring primary; flow-based surveillance where practiced [1]
Recurrent same-lesion restenosis <3 months → escalate: DCB, stent-graft, or surgical revision conference

complications.name: Vein rupture during angioplasty
complications.management: Balloon tamponade immediately (low pressure, prolonged), then covered stent if persistent extravasation; most are controlled without surgery.

complications.name: Symptomatic PE during declot
complications.management: Limit clot burden strategies for large-volume thrombosis; treat hemodynamically significant PE per PE pathways.

complications.name: Arterial embolization (hand)
complications.management: Cold painful hand after inflow restoration: back-bleeding maneuver, aspiration via retrograde catheter, or balloon retrieval of the embolus.

complications.name: Early re-thrombosis
complications.management: Usually an undertreated anatomic lesion — repeat with IVUS-honesty about residual stenosis; discuss surgical revision at conference.

complications.name: Access infection
complications.management: Graft infections need surgical management; blood cultures + antibiotics; never ignore pus at a cannulation site.

complications.name: Steal exacerbation
complications.management: Improving inflow can unmask distal hypoperfusion — vascular surgery consultation (banding, DRIL).

pearls: Treat the circuit, not the image: a 50% stenosis with normal flows and a happy dialysis run may deserve surveillance, not a balloon (KDOQI: clinical indicators drive intervention) [1].
In every declot, the venous outflow lesion is the 'why' and the arterial plug is the 'switch' — clear the why before flipping the switch, or you embolize clot arterially.
A thrill is the only acceptable endpoint. A pulse means obstruction persists downstream — keep looking.
Central veins: angioplasty generously, stent reluctantly (jailing future options); symptomatic central stenosis in a catheter-scarred patient is a life-plan conversation, not just a lesion.

citations.text: Lok CE, Huber TS, Lee T, et al. KDOQI clinical practice guideline for vascular access: 2019 update. Am J Kidney Dis. 2020;75(4 Suppl 2):S1-S164.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.
Lookstein RA, Haruguchi H, Ouriel K, et al. Drug-coated balloons for dysfunctional dialysis arteriovenous fistulas (IN.PACT AV). N Engl J Med. 2020;383(8):733-742.

procedures.title: Iliofemoral DVT Thrombolysis & Thrombectomy
procedures.overview: Catheter-based clot removal for iliofemoral DVT — mechanical thrombectomy, catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT), or both. ATTRACT tempered enthusiasm (no overall reduction in post-thrombotic syndrome) but supported treatment of iliofemoral disease for severity reduction; phlegmasia remains an emergency indication. Uncover and stent the culprit lesion (May-Thurner) or the clot returns [1,2].

labs: Duplex US defining clot extent; CTV/MRV for iliocaval and May-Thurner assessment
CBC, INR, fibrinogen baseline (trend fibrinogen during any lytic infusion; many hold/reduce when <150–200 mg/dL)
Creatinine/eGFR; type & screen
Full anticoagulation initiated at diagnosis unless contraindicated
preop.meds: Therapeutic heparin throughout (target anti-Xa/aPTT per protocol). CDT: alteplase 0.5–1 mg/h through multi-sidehole catheter. Anxiolysis/moderate sedation; anesthesia for large-bore mechanical work in sick patients.
equipment: US-guided popliteal (prone) or tibial/femoral access; 7–10F sheath for CDT, up to 13–16F for large-bore mechanical
Multi-sidehole infusion catheter sized to clot length (CDT)
Mechanical: ClotTriever/other large-bore venous thrombectomy systems; AngioJet (pharmacomechanical) with hemolysis awareness
IVUS — the standard for sizing and confirming iliac vein lesions
Dedicated venous stents 12–16 mm (or Wallstent) for iliocaval lesions
Compression stockings ordered for after
indications: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens / limb-threatening venous congestion (emergent)
Acute (<14 day) iliofemoral DVT with severe symptoms in patients with low bleeding risk and good functional status
Proximal DVT propagating or symptomatic despite anticoagulation
Symptomatic chronic iliocaval obstruction (elective recanalization pathway)

absolute: For lytics: active internal bleeding, CNS surgery/trauma/hemorrhage <3 months, absolute lytic contraindications
Inability to anticoagulate at all
relative: Isolated femoropopliteal DVT with mild symptoms (anticoagulation alone usually suffices [1])
Severe renal impairment (contrast; consider CO2 adjuncts)
Pregnancy (mechanical-only strategies preferred when intervention truly indicated)
