steps.text: Prone popliteal vein access under US is the workhorse (posterior tibial for calf-extension clot). Sheath sized to strategy. Venogram defines the clot column from below.
steps.title: Access below the clot

steps.text: Wire + catheter through the thrombosed segment into the IVC. Acute clot crosses easily; chronic segments need persistence (angled glide, supported by IVUS knowledge of the target lumen).
steps.title: Cross the occlusion

steps.text: Deploy the thrombectomy device through the clot per IFU (e.g., ClotTriever coring/collection passes from IVC-ward to sheath). Repeat until venographic and extracted-clot endpoints plateau. Track blood loss across passes.
steps.title: Mechanical thrombectomy passes

steps.text: For residual adherent clot or as primary strategy: multi-sidehole catheter across the clot, alteplase 0.5–1 mg/h + subtherapeutic heparin via sheath; ICU monitoring; re-look venogram at 12–24 h. Fibrinogen and neuro checks q6h.
steps.title: Or/and CDT

steps.text: After clot clearance, hunt the causative lesion: May-Thurner compression (left CIV under right CIA), post-thrombotic webs/trabeculation. IVUS quantifies area stenosis (>50% typically treated) far better than venography.
steps.title: Interrogate the iliac vein with IVUS

steps.text: Dedicated venous stent sized to normal adjacent vein (commonly 14–16 mm CIV), landing zones from healthy IVC-confluence margin into a disease-free inflow segment — cover the lesion, spare the contralateral CIV inflow when possible, extend below the ligament only when disease demands.
steps.title: Stent the culprit

steps.text: Post-dilate; confirm brisk inline flow and profunda/femoral inflow (inflow is the stent's life insurance). Hemostasis; compression wrap.
steps.title: Optimize inflow and finish

postop: Therapeutic anticoagulation continues — duration per VTE etiology (provoked vs unprovoked); many add antiplatelet for stents (institutional)
Ambulate early; 30–40 mmHg compression stockings
Duplex surveillance of stent at 1, 6, 12 months
Counsel on post-thrombotic syndrome trajectory: intervention reduces severity in iliofemoral disease (ATTRACT subgroup) but is not a guarantee [1]
Re-thrombosis presents early — new swelling → immediate duplex

complications.name: Major bleeding (lytic strategies)
complications.management: Stop lytic, fibrinogen/cryoprecipitate, reverse per protocol; ICH → neurocritical care. Mechanical-first strategies exist largely to avoid this.

complications.name: Early stent thrombosis
complications.management: Usually inflow failure or undersized/mislanded stent: re-intervene, correct the mechanical problem, intensify antithrombotics.

complications.name: Hemoglobinuria/AKI (rheolytic devices)
complications.management: Hydration, limited run-time; avoid in renal impairment.

complications.name: PE during manipulation
complications.management: Mostly small/asymptomatic; hemodynamic PE → escalate to PE intervention algorithms. Routine filter placement NOT indicated.

complications.name: Access site hematoma/AVF
complications.management: US-guided access minimizes; manage expectantly.

complications.name: Bradycardia/arrhythmia (AngioJet in central veins)
complications.management: Adenosine-release phenomenon — pause runs, atropine ready, pace rarely.

pearls: Phlegmasia is the one true venous emergency: elevate, heparinize, and get clot out the same day — fasciotomy-level urgency thinking.
The clot is the symptom; the iliac lesion is the disease. Skipping IVUS is how 'successful' thrombectomies re-occlude by clinic follow-up.
Extend stents into clean inflow, not just across the lesion — a stent draining a diseased femoral confluence thromboses regardless of diameter.
ATTRACT nuance for boards: overall PTS not reduced; iliofemoral subgroup had less moderate-severe PTS and better severity scores [1].

citations.text: Vedantham S, Goldhaber SZ, Julian JA, et al. Pharmacomechanical catheter-directed thrombolysis for deep-vein thrombosis (ATTRACT). N Engl J Med. 2017;377(23):2240-2252.

Vedantham S, Sista AK, Klein SJ, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for the treatment of lower-extremity deep vein thrombosis with use of endovascular thrombus removal. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2014;25(9):1317-1325.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Fluoroscopic NG/OG & Enteric Feeding Tube Placement
procedures.overview: Fluoroscopic placement of nasogastric, orogastric, and post-pyloric feeding tubes when bedside attempts fail or when the tip must land beyond the pylorus (aspiration risk, gastroparesis, pancreatitis). Wire-and-catheter technique converts a blind bedside chore into a five-minute directed procedure — and fluoroscopic confirmation eliminates the misplaced-tube disasters that plague blind placement [1].

labs: Low-risk procedure; coags only if deranged and mucosal bleeding risk matters [2]
Review prior imaging for hiatal hernia, diverticula, prior gastric surgery — the map explains the bedside failures
NPO if sedation used (often none needed)
preop.meds: Topical lidocaine to the nare/pharynx is usually the only anesthesia; glucagon 0.5–1 mg IV relaxes the pylorus for post-pyloric work.
equipment: Fluoroscopy; 5F angled catheter (vertebral/MPA) + hydrophilic wire for steering
Feeding tube (8–12F Dobhoff type with stylet) or larger NG/decompression tube
Water-soluble contrast for confirmation; bridle kit for the serial self-extubator
indications: Failed bedside NG/Dobhoff placement (altered anatomy, hiatal hernia, uncooperative course)
Post-pyloric feeding requirement: high aspiration risk, gastroparesis, gastric outlet obstruction, severe pancreatitis (jejunal feeding) [1]
Decompression of obstruction with a directed tube
Placement across known strictures/tumors of the esophagus (over a wire)

absolute: Suspected esophageal perforation; recent skull base/facial fracture for nasal route (use oral); complete obstruction the tube cannot safely traverse
relative: Esophageal varices with recent banding (gentle technique, soft tubes), severe coagulopathy (mucosal trauma), basal skull surgery

steps.text: Topicalize; pass the tube/catheter through the nare with the neck slightly flexed, watching laterally on fluoro to stay out of the airway — coughing and desaturation mean trachea, not stomach.
steps.title: Steer past the pharynx

steps.text: Advance under fluoro; use the angled catheter + wire to cross hernias, diverticula, or strictures rather than pushing blindly.
steps.title: Negotiate the esophagus

steps.text: Right-anterior-down positioning, glucagon, and the angled catheter directed along the antral curve; the wire leads through the pylorus and duodenal sweep.
steps.title: Cross the pylorus (post-pyloric tubes)

steps.text: Gastric tubes: tip in the body/antrum. Post-pyloric: beyond the ligament of Treitz (D4 or proximal jejunum) for true aspiration protection.
steps.title: Reach the target

steps.text: Exchange the catheter for the feeding tube over the wire, remove the stylet, and document tip position with a contrast puff — the image is the tube's birth certificate.
steps.title: Exchange and confirm

steps.text: Tape/securement device; nasal bridle for patients with a history of pulling tubes.
steps.title: Secure

postop: Cleared to use immediately once position is documented — no auscultation guessing games
Flush protocol to prevent clogging (before/after feeds and meds; nothing crushed inadequately)
Replace dislodged tubes promptly under fluoro — the tract of a fresh surgical anastomosis is not the place for bedside heroics
Aspiration precautions unchanged: post-pyloric position reduces but does not abolish risk

complications.name: Airway placement (prevented by fluoro)
complications.management: The reason IR gets these consults: fluoroscopy watches the tube dodge the trachea in real time [1].

complications.name: Epistaxis
complications.management: Topical vasoconstrictor, pressure; use the other nare.

complications.name: Esophageal/pharyngeal perforation
complications.management: Wire-led gentle technique across strictures; NPO, antibiotics, surgical consult if it occurs.

complications.name: Tube clogging
complications.management: Flush discipline; enzymatic declogging; exchange over wire.

complications.name: Dislodgment
complications.management: Bridle for repeat offenders; prompt fluoro replacement.

pearls: The lateral fluoro view at the neck is the two-second maneuver that proves esophagus vs trachea before the tube is committed.
Glucagon + right-side-down positioning does more for pyloric crossing than any amount of pushing.
Past the ligament of Treitz or it isn't jejunal feeding — a tip parked in D2 refluxes right back into the stomach.
Save the final contrast image to PACS every time; 'the tube was confirmed' without an image is a sentence nobody wants to defend.

citations.text: Itkin M, DeLegge MH, Fang JC, et al. Multidisciplinary practical guidelines for gastrointestinal access for enteral nutrition and decompression from the Society of Interventional Radiology and American Gastroenterological Association. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2011;22(8):1089-1106.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Intravascular Foreign Body Retrieval
procedures.overview: Percutaneous snare retrieval of embolized intravascular objects — fractured catheter fragments (classically port catheters via pinch-off syndrome), lost guidewires, migrated coils, filter components, and misdeployed devices. Success rates exceed 90–95% in experienced hands with minimal morbidity, which is why virtually every intravascular foreign body deserves an endovascular attempt before surgery [1].

labs: Low bleeding-risk per SIR [1 — see citation 2]; standard access labs
CT or fluoro localization immediately pre-procedure — fragments migrate between imaging and table time
ECG review; tell anesthesia/nursing to expect ectopy during right-heart manipulation
preop.meds: Local + moderate sedation; arrhythmia drugs and defibrillator pads available for intracardiac work.
equipment: Gooseneck snare set (multiple sizes) — the workhorse; triloop/EN Snare for off-axis captures
Long sheath (federal-to-large: 8–12F) reaching near the object, sized to swallow the captured fragment
Adjuncts: endovascular forceps, deflecting wires, pigtail catheter to reposition a free end, balloon to displace
Femoral venous access standard; jugular as alternative/second access
indications: Catheter fragment embolization (port/PICC fracture, pinch-off syndrome) — typically lodged in RA, RV, or pulmonary artery
Lost/migrated guidewires, coils, plugs, or filter fragments
Misdeployed or migrated stents and devices requiring repositioning or removal
Retained catheter tips after difficult line removals

absolute: Object chronically endothelialized/incorporated where traction risks vessel or cardiac injury (weeks–months old, fixed on imaging) — individualized risk/benefit
relative: Fragment in a position where retrieval risk exceeds the (often low) risk of leaving it; active endocarditis on the object favors surgery

steps.text: Fluoro in two projections: where is it, which end is free, and is anything (chordae, valve, branch PA) trapping it?
steps.title: Localize and study the object

steps.text: Femoral venous access; advance a long sheath close to the object so the captured fragment travels inside plastic, not naked through the heart.
steps.title: Deliver the sheath to the neighborhood

steps.text: Both ends embedded? Use a pigtail or deflecting wire to hook mid-body and tug a tip free — the single most useful trick in retrieval.
steps.title: Create a free end if none exists

steps.text: Open the gooseneck perpendicular to the fragment axis, lasso the free end, cinch firmly at the tip (mid-body grabs fold and jam in the sheath).
steps.title: Snare the free end

steps.text: Pull snare + fragment into the sheath and remove everything together under continuous fluoro. Resistance = stop and reassess, never force.
steps.title: Retract as one unit

steps.text: Fluoro the entire path and the retrieved specimen — measure it against the known missing length. Completion venogram if vessel injury is a concern.
steps.title: Confirm completeness

postop: Brief cardiac monitoring after right-heart manipulation (ectopy resolves once the hardware is out)
Access-site care; same-day discharge typical
Document retrieved fragment length photographically against the device record
If retrieval failed/partial: CT to document final position and a written risk/benefit note re: surgical referral vs surveillance

complications.name: Arrhythmia during intracardiac manipulation
complications.management: Usually stops when the catheter moves; treat sustained arrhythmia per ACLS.

complications.name: Vessel/cardiac injury from traction
complications.management: Never force an incorporated fragment; echo/CT if suspected, surgery for tamponade.

complications.name: Fragment migration to a worse position
complications.management: Chase distally (PA branches) with coaxial systems; convert to jugular access for angle.

complications.name: Failed retrieval
complications.management: Surgical retrieval vs observation — many small, stable, distal PA fragments are watched safely.

complications.name: Access complications
complications.management: Standard care.

pearls: Snare the tip, not the middle: a tip-grab streamlines the fragment for the sheath; a mid-body grab folds it into an anchor.
No free end? Make one — the pigtail-twist to deliver a tip into snaring position is the move that separates quick cases from long ones.
Bring a sheath big enough to swallow the catch; dragging an uncovered fragment through the tricuspid valve is how retrievals create new consults.
Pinch-off syndrome is the exam pearl: a port catheter compressed between clavicle and first rib fatigues, fractures, and embolizes — look for the 'pinch' on prior CXRs [1].

citations.text: Gabelmann A, Kramer S, Gorich J. Percutaneous retrieval of lost or misplaced intravascular objects. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 2001;176(6):1509-1513.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Percutaneous Radiologic Gastrostomy / Gastrojejunostomy
procedures.overview: Image-guided placement of a feeding tube directly into the stomach (± jejunal extension) for enteral nutrition or gastric decompression, when endoscopic (PEG) placement is impossible or contraindicated (e.g., obstructing head-and-neck or esophageal tumor, ALS with respiratory compromise). Gastropexy anchors the stomach to the wall for a secure tract [1].

labs: HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [2]
Review CT for colon/liver position relative to the anterior stomach
Aspiration/nutrition assessment; confirm indication with the care team
Antibiotic plan
preop.meds: Single-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin) is commonly given [3]; glucagon 0.5–1 mg IV to reduce gastric peristalsis during puncture; local anesthesia + moderate sedation.
equipment: NG/orogastric tube for air insufflation; fluoroscopy
Gastropexy T-fasteners (typically 3–4 in a diamond/triangle)
Micropuncture/access needle, wires, serial dilators, peel-away sheath
Gastrostomy tube (12–16F) or GJ tube with jejunal extension; retention balloon/pigtail
Contrast for confirmation; US to check for interposed liver edge
indications: Long-term enteral nutrition: neurologic dysphagia (stroke, ALS), head/neck or esophageal cancer obstructing endoscopic access
Gastric decompression in malignant bowel obstruction (venting gastrostomy)
Inability to pass an endoscope (stricture, prior surgery)
GJ tube when gastric feeding is unsafe (aspiration risk, gastroparesis)

absolute: No safe window (colon or liver interposed across the entire anterior stomach)
Uncorrectable coagulopathy (non-emergent)
relative: Large-volume ascites (drain first; impairs gastric apposition and tract healing)
Prior gastric surgery/partial gastrectomy, portal hypertension with gastric varices
Total esophageal obstruction preventing NG tube for insufflation (use alternative CO2/needle access technique)

steps.text: Advance an NG/orogastric tube and insufflate air to distend the stomach against the anterior abdominal wall, displacing colon inferiorly and liver superiorly. Glucagon reduces peristalsis for a stable target.
steps.title: Insufflate the stomach

steps.text: Fluoroscopy (± prior CT / US for the liver edge) confirms the distended stomach body is directly beneath the planned puncture with no interposed colon or liver. Mark a point over the body, lateral to the rectus/epigastric vessels.
steps.title: Confirm a safe window

steps.text: Deploy T-fasteners to tack the anterior gastric wall to the abdominal wall in a triangle/diamond around the planned entry — this apposition secures the tract and reduces leak/dislodgement, especially important without endoscopic bumper.
steps.title: Gastropexy

steps.text: Puncture the distended stomach within the gastropexy anchors under fluoro; confirm intragastric position with contrast/air aspiration; place a wire and coil it in the fundus.
steps.title: Access the stomach

steps.text: Serially dilate the tract; advance the peel-away sheath and deliver the gastrostomy tube, inflating the retention balloon or forming the pigtail in the stomach.
steps.title: Dilate and place the tube

steps.text: For jejunal feeding, direct a wire/catheter through the pylorus into the proximal jejunum and place the jejunal extension past the ligament of Treitz.
steps.title: GJ extension (if indicated)

steps.text: Contrast injection confirms intraluminal position without leak; secure the external retention device at the skin (not too tight — allow for wall edema). Document tube type and length.
steps.title: Confirm and secure

postop: Delay feeding per protocol (often start within 12–24 h for radiologic gastrostomy with gastropexy; institutional variation)
Site care; avoid over-tight external bolster (necrosis/buried bumper risk)
Monitor for peritonitis (pain, fever, leak) — early tube dislodgement before tract maturation is a surgical emergency
Gastropexy sutures typically release/removed at ~2 weeks
Educate caregivers on flushing, feeding, and troubleshooting; replacement over tract once mature

complications.name: Tube dislodgement (pre-maturation)
complications.management: Before the tract matures this risks intraperitoneal feeding/peritonitis — do not blindly reinsert; image to confirm intragastric position, surgical consult if peritonitis.

complications.name: Peristomal leak / infection
complications.management: Site care, avoid over-tight bolster; treat cellulitis; optimize tube fit.

complications.name: Colonic transgression / gastrocolic fistula
complications.management: Prevented by insufflation + CT window check; presents late (feeds in stool/diarrhea) → reposition/surgery.

complications.name: Hemorrhage
complications.management: Avoid epigastric vessels/gastric varices; most minor; significant → endoscopy/embolization.

complications.name: Aspiration
complications.management: Elevate head of bed; GJ conversion for high aspiration risk; treat pneumonitis.

complications.name: Buried bumper / balloon malfunction
complications.management: Loosen external retention; exchange tube; check balloon volume periodically.

pearls: Insufflate, then verify: colon creeping across the anterior stomach is the interposition that turns a G-tube into a gastrocolic fistula — CT window check earns its keep.
Gastropexy is what makes radiologic gastrostomy safe without a bumper — anchor first, puncture within the anchors.
Head-and-neck/esophageal obstruction is the classic 'why IR and not GI' — no scope can pass, but fluoroscopic access can.
An over-tightened external bolster is a slow ischemic ulcer — leave room for post-procedure wall edema.

citations.text: Itkin M, DeLegge MH, Fang JC, et al. Multidisciplinary practical guidelines for gastrointestinal access for enteral nutrition and decompression from SIR and AGA. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2011;22(8):1089-1106.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Chehab MA, Thakor AS, Tulin-Silver S, et al. Adult and pediatric antibiotic prophylaxis during vascular and interventional radiology procedures: SIR practice parameter. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2018;29(11):1483-1501.

procedures.title: GI Bleed Embolization
aliases: Transcatheter arterial embolization for GI hemorrhage
procedures.overview: Transcatheter embolization for acute GI hemorrhage after failed or infeasible endoscopy (upper GI) or as first-line for CTA-positive lower GI bleeding. Upper GI targets tolerate empiric embolization thanks to rich collaterals; lower GI embolization must be superselective at the vasa recta to avoid bowel infarction [1,2].

labs: CTA immediately before angio when feasible — detects bleeding ≥0.3–0.5 mL/min and maps the culprit vessel
CBC, INR, fibrinogen; transfuse toward hemostatic targets but do not delay for perfect numbers [4]
Type & cross; large-bore IVs; involve endoscopy/surgery early — this is a team sport
Lactate/vitals to track resuscitation
preop.meds: Resuscitation per massive transfusion protocol as needed; correct coagulopathy concurrently. Glucagon 0.5–1 mg IV can reduce bowel motion artifact during runs.
equipment: 5F sheath; visceral selective catheters (SOS Omni, Cobra, Simmons 1)
High-flow microcatheter + 0.014–0.018 microwire
Detachable/pushable microcoils (2–6 mm), NBCA glue or Onyx for experienced operators, gelfoam slurry
Particles 300–500 μm reserved for tumor bleeding
Power injector for DSA runs
indications: Upper GI bleed refractory to endoscopic therapy (classically duodenal ulcer eroding the GDA)
Lower GI bleed with active extravasation on CTA or ongoing hemodynamic instability
Recurrent bleeding after endoscopic treatment; surgical high-risk patients
Hemobilia or hemosuccus pancreaticus (pseudoaneurysm targets)

absolute: None in exsanguinating hemorrhage — resuscitation proceeds in parallel
relative: Severe contrast allergy (premedicate if time allows) [3]
Diffuse mucosal bleeding (e.g., hemorrhagic gastritis) — embolization rarely helps
Prior bowel resection or radiation compromising collaterals raises ischemia risk for LGIB embolization

steps.text: Localize extravasation to a vascular territory (celiac/SMA/IMA) before puncture. In duodenal ulcer bleeding the GDA is the presumed culprit even without active extravasation.
steps.title: Review CTA and plan the target

steps.text: Femoral (or radial) access, 5F sheath. Selective celiac and SMA angiograms — extended venous-phase runs; IMA if LGIB not localized. Look for extravasation, pseudoaneurysm, hyperemic focus, or vessel cutoff.
steps.title: Access and survey angiography

steps.text: Coaxial microcatheter to the bleeding branch. In UGIB: catheterize the GDA. In LGIB: track distally to the vasa recta feeding the bleeding point — the closer to the wall, the smaller the infarct risk.
steps.title: Superselect the culprit

steps.text: Embolize distal-to-proximal — coil the 'back door' (distal GDA at the gastroepiploic/superior pancreaticoduodenal junction) first, then the ulcer segment, then the 'front door' (GDA origin). Skipping the back door invites retrograde refill from the SMA via the pancreaticoduodenal arcade [1].
steps.title: Upper GI: sandwich the GDA

steps.text: Deposit microcoils (or a small glue cast) in the vasa recta / terminal marginal branch supplying the extravasation. Limit territory to 1–2 vasa recta. Post-embolization run confirms hemostasis with preserved adjacent arcades.
steps.title: Lower GI: superselective occlusion

steps.text: Re-run the most suspicious territory with provocation only in refractory, transfusion-dependent cases at experienced centers. Otherwise: empiric GDA (duodenal) or left gastric (gastric) embolization based on endoscopic localization is well supported [2].
steps.title: No source found?

steps.text: Final territorial angiogram; hemodynamics reassessed with anesthesia/ICU. Leave the sheath if instability persists and re-look is plausible.
steps.title: Complete and reassess

postop: ICU monitoring; serial hemoglobin q6h and lactate clearance
Watch for rebleeding (15–30% overall) — melena may continue from evacuating old blood; rising transfusion needs matter
LGIB embolization: serial abdominal exams; pain out of proportion, rising lactate/WBC → CT and surgical consult for ischemia
PPI infusion and repeat endoscopy for ulcer disease; treat H. pylori
Resume anticoagulation via structured multidisciplinary decision once stable

complications.name: Bowel ischemia/infarction
complications.rate: <5% (superselective LGIB); higher with proximal or extensive embolization
complications.management: Serial exams; CT with peritonitis/lactate rise → surgical resection. Prevention: embolize as distal as the microcatheter allows, minimal territory.

complications.name: Rebleeding
complications.management: Repeat CTA/angiography — look for collateral refill (missed back door), a second lesion, or coagulopathy. Repeat embolization, endoscopy, or surgery.

complications.name: Nontarget embolization
complications.management: Prevention: know reflux behavior of your embolic; glue and gelfoam demand experience. Manage ischemic sequelae supportively; hepatic/splenic nontarget usually tolerated.

complications.name: Vessel dissection or spasm during selection
complications.management: Spasm mimics cutoff — nitroglycerin 100–200 mcg IA and patience. Dissection: usually settle with time; avoid treating artifacts.

complications.name: Contrast-associated AKI
complications.management: Volume resuscitation is protective; minimize runs; accept diagnostic-quality over beauty [3].

pearls: Negative angiography is common (bleeding is intermittent) — CTA timing is everything; go to angio while the CTA is hot.
Duodenal ulcer + hemodynamic instability = GDA sandwich, extravasation or not. The collateral circulation makes empiric embolization safe and effective [2].
In LGIB, coils at the vasa recta level are the classic safe depth; glue travels — respect it.
Always image the entire territory after embolization: pancreaticoduodenal and marginal artery collaterals are the classic sources of 'unexplained' rebleeding.

citations.text: Loffroy R, Rao P, Ota S, et al. Embolization of acute nonvariceal upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage resistant to endoscopic treatment: results and predictors of recurrent bleeding. Cardiovasc Intervent Radiol. 2010;33(6):1088-1100.
Laine L, Barkun AN, Saltzman JR, Martel M, Leontiadis GI. ACG clinical guideline: upper gastrointestinal and ulcer bleeding. Am J Gastroenterol. 2021;116(5):899-917.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Image-Guided Percutaneous Biopsy
procedures.overview: Targeted tissue sampling under image guidance — the diagnostic backbone of modern oncology and infection workup. Success is defined at the intersection of a safe corridor, adequate tissue for modern molecular panels, and low complication rate. Coaxial technique lets you take multiple cores through one capsular puncture [1].

labs: Solid-organ core biopsy is HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8; hold anticoagulants/antiplatelets per SIR agent-specific windows [2]
Superficial/low-risk biopsies (e.g., thyroid FNA, superficial node) are low-risk: less stringent thresholds [2]
Review prior imaging for target, corridor, and enhancement characteristics
Confirm required sample type with pathology (cytology vs core, cultures, flow, molecular)
preop.meds: Local anesthesia; moderate sedation as needed. No routine antibiotics for solid-organ biopsy.
equipment: US (superficial/vascular targets) or CT (deep/lung/retroperitoneum)
Coaxial introducer + core biopsy gun (typically 18–20G core; 22–25G for FNA)
Specimen media: formalin, saline for cultures, RPMI for flow, cytology fixative; slides for ROSE
Hydrodissection setup (saline ± D5W) and 22G needle to create safe corridors
Gelfoam/tract embolic for high-risk organs; hemostatic plan
indications: Tissue diagnosis of a mass (malignancy, characterization, molecular/genomic profiling)
Diagnosis of diffuse organ disease (non-targeted liver/renal parenchymal biopsy)
Microbiologic sampling of suspected infection
Restaging/mutation profiling of a known cancer

absolute: No safe access route to the target
Uncorrectable severe coagulopathy for a high-bleeding-risk deep organ target
relative: Uncooperative patient unable to breath-hold (sedation/anesthesia)
Vascular lesion/pheochromocytoma suspicion (biopsy can precipitate crisis — biochemical workup first)
Contrast needs if lesion only visible with enhancement

steps.text: Choose the shortest safe path avoiding bowel, major vessels, pleura, and non-target organs. For lung, minimize aerated lung crossed and avoid fissures. Position and mark; plan breath-hold.
steps.title: Plan the safest corridor

steps.text: Local down to the capsule. Advance the coaxial introducer to the lesion edge under intermittent imaging, confirming the tip short of the target before firing.
steps.title: Anesthetize and place the coaxial

steps.text: Hydrodissection to displace bowel/nerves; angle the gantry or use decubitus positioning to open a window. Consider contrast/PET fusion for the viable (non-necrotic) portion of a mass.
steps.title: Optimize with adjuncts

steps.text: Fire the core through the coaxial into the target; take multiple cores at slightly varied angles for heterogeneity and molecular adequacy. ROSE (if available) confirms lesional, adequate tissue before you stop.
steps.title: Sample

steps.text: Allocate to formalin/cultures/flow/molecular as pre-agreed with pathology — the best core is wasted in the wrong tube.
steps.title: Distribute specimens correctly

steps.text: For high-risk organs (deep liver, kidney), inject gelfoam/hemostatic through the coaxial as you withdraw. Lung: consider tract sealant / blood patch and roll biopsy-side-down.
steps.title: Tract management

steps.text: Immediate scan for hematoma or pneumothorax before the patient leaves the table.
steps.title: Post-procedure imaging check

postop: Recovery observation per organ: solid-organ typically 2–4 h with vitals; document a stable post exam
Lung biopsy: upright CXR at ~1 and 3–4 h to detect delayed pneumothorax
Return precautions: pain, hemoptysis, hematuria, hypotension, shortness of breath
Communicate specimen adequacy pathway; arrange result follow-up
Resume anticoagulation per hemostasis and SIR guidance

complications.name: Hemorrhage / hematoma
complications.management: Most self-limit; hemodynamic change or expanding hematoma → CT, resuscitate, angiography/embolization. Tract embolics and correct coags are prevention.

complications.name: Pneumothorax (lung biopsy)
complications.management: Small/asymptomatic → observe; enlarging/symptomatic → aspiration or small-bore chest tube. Fewer pleural passes and biopsy-side-down reduce rate.

complications.name: Tumor seeding
complications.rate: very rare (<0.01–0.05%; higher for certain tumors)
complications.management: Coaxial single-capsule technique minimizes; relevant for HCC/mesothelioma — coordinate corridor with surgical resection plan.

complications.name: Infection
complications.management: Sterile technique; treat established infection; drainage if abscess seeded.

complications.name: Pancreatitis (pancreatic biopsy)
complications.management: Supportive; minimize passes through normal pancreas.

complications.name: Non-diagnostic sample
complications.management: ROSE reduces it; target viable/enhancing tissue; repeat biopsy or escalate to surgical biopsy if needed.

pearls: Sample the rim, not the necrotic core: PET/contrast tells you where the diagnostic (and molecular-adequate) tissue lives.
Coaxial is a philosophy: one capsular hole, many cores, and a route to embolize the tract — safer and more productive.
Talk to pathology before you biopsy: modern panels demand enough cores and the right media; the plan is made before the needle.
Suspected pheochromocytoma or a vascular lesion? Biochemistry and imaging first — a needle can trigger a hypertensive crisis or brisk bleed.

citations.text: Gupta S, Wallace MJ, Cardella JF, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for percutaneous needle biopsy. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2010;21(7):969-975.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: IVC Filter Placement
procedures.overview: Mechanical PE protection for patients with venous thromboembolism who cannot be anticoagulated. PREPIC2 showed no mortality benefit when filters are added to anticoagulation — so the modern indication is narrow, and every retrievable filter placed creates an obligation to retrieve it [1,2].

labs: Review CT/duplex documenting VTE and clot extent (iliocaval involvement changes deployment level)
Platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0–3.0 (low bleeding-risk) [3]
Creatinine — CO2 cavography is an excellent zero-contrast alternative
Document WHY anticoagulation is contraindicated in the chart, plus a retrieval plan
preop.meds: Local ± light sedation. No routine antibiotics.
equipment: Filter kit (jugular or femoral configuration — check the label, they are not interchangeable)
5F pigtail/straight catheter for cavography
0.035 wire; US for access
CO2 or iodinated contrast for cavogram
indications: Acute VTE with an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation (active major bleeding, recent intracranial hemorrhage, imminent major surgery)
Recurrent PE despite therapeutic anticoagulation (confirm compliance/levels first)
Complication of anticoagulation forcing cessation in acute VTE
Selected: severe cardiopulmonary reserve limitation with acute VTE peri-intervention (individualized)

absolute: No access route to the IVC
Complete IVC thrombosis (nothing to protect)
relative: Bacteremia (relative — device seeding)
Anticipated inability to follow up for retrieval — weigh permanent implications
Prophylactic placement without VTE (e.g., trauma) — weak evidence; guidelines discourage routine use [2]

steps.text: Right IJ (avoids crossing iliac clot; required if bilateral iliofemoral DVT) or right femoral. US-guided micropuncture; 5F catheter into the IVC.
steps.title: Choose access

steps.text: Power-injected inferior vena cavogram at the confluence: measure caval diameter, locate renal vein inflow (unopacified streams), and exclude caval thrombus. This one run prevents most placement errors.
steps.title: Cavogram

steps.text: Duplicated IVC (~0.3–3%: left iliac drains a left cava to the left renal vein — a single infrarenal filter protects only one side), circumaortic left renal vein (place below the lower limb), retroaortic renal vein, mega cava >28 mm (bird's-nest or bilateral iliac filters).
steps.title: Interrogate for variants

steps.text: Advance the sheath to target and deploy per device IFU so the filter apex sits at/just below the lowest renal vein — renal inflow discourages apex thrombus stagnation. Suprarenal placement only for specific indications (renal/gonadal vein thrombus, pregnancy, IVC clot to the renals).
steps.title: Deploy infrarenal

steps.text: Completion cavogram or spot: centered, tilt <15°, at target level. Document the filter type and cranio-caudal landmarks.
steps.title: Confirm

steps.text: Hemostasis; enter the patient in a filter-tracking registry/clinic pathway before they leave the department. The best retrieval plan is made on placement day [2].
steps.title: Close the loop

postop: Resume/start anticoagulation the moment the contraindication resolves — the filter is a bridge, not a substitute [1]
Filter clinic tracking: reassess retrieval eligibility at 1–3 months
Educate the patient: device card, why it was placed, that it should come out
For permanent filters: periodic imaging only if symptoms

complications.name: Filter tilt / malposition
complications.management: Significant tilt embeds the hook and complicates retrieval; if immediately recognized and severe, reposition/exchange while access persists.

complications.name: IVC thrombosis / filter thrombosis
complications.management: Leg swelling ± phlegmasia: anticoagulate if possible; iliocaval recanalization/thrombectomy for severe symptoms.

complications.name: Strut penetration
complications.rate: increases with dwell (imaging penetration common; symptomatic rare)
complications.management: Most asymptomatic. Aortic/duodenal/vertebral involvement with symptoms → advanced retrieval referral.

complications.name: Filter fracture ± fragment embolization
complications.management: Cardiac/pulmonary fragments: multidisciplinary decision — endovascular retrieval for accessible fragments in symptomatic patients.

complications.name: Recurrent PE despite filter
complications.management: Filters reduce, not abolish, PE. Optimize anticoagulation; assess filter thrombus.

complications.name: Access site DVT
complications.management: US-guided single-wall puncture reduces risk; treat per DVT standards.

pearls: The cavogram is the intellectual step: renal vein level, variants, caval size — everything else is deployment mechanics.
Unexplained 'two IVCs' on the left? Duplication. Protecting only the right side is a classic miss — options: bilateral filters or suprarenal placement.
Retrievable filters left forever behave worse than permanent designs — if nobody will chase retrieval, choose device and counseling accordingly.
PREPIC2 in one line: anticoagulated patients gain nothing from an added filter [1]. Quote it when consults push.

citations.text: Mismetti P, Laporte S, Pellerin O, et al. Effect of a retrievable inferior vena cava filter plus anticoagulation vs anticoagulation alone on risk of recurrent pulmonary embolism: the PREPIC2 randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;313(16):1627-1635.

Kaufman JA, Barnes GD, Chaer RA, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology clinical practice guideline for inferior vena cava filters in the treatment of patients with venous thromboembolic disease. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2020;31(10):1529-1544.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: IVC Filter Retrieval
procedures.overview: Removal of a retrievable IVC filter once PE protection is no longer required. Standard snare retrieval succeeds in most filters with short dwell; embedded, tilted, or fractured filters need advanced techniques (loop-snare, rigid forceps, laser sheath) at experienced centers. FDA and SIR guidance: retrieve as soon as clinically appropriate [1].

labs: Anticoagulation status: therapeutic and tolerated — document
Pre-retrieval imaging (recent CT if long dwell: penetration, fracture, position)
Platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0–3.0 [2]
Dwell time from the device registry — predicts difficulty
preop.meds: Local + moderate sedation; anesthesia backup for planned complex retrieval.
equipment: Right IJ access: 8–11F sheath for routine; up to 14–16F for complex
Gooseneck snare (routine hook engagement)
Advanced: sling/loop-snare wire technique, rigid endobronchial forceps, excimer laser sheath (photothermal ablation of fibrous cap)
Balloon (displace tilted apex off the wall)
Retrieval cone/capture sheath per filter design
indications: Filter no longer indicated: anticoagulation established/tolerated ≥several weeks, or VTE risk resolved
Symptomatic filter (penetration pain, caval thrombosis after treatment, fracture)
Device-related complication prophylaxis in young patients with long life expectancy

absolute: Ongoing need for PE protection (contraindication to anticoagulation persists)
Large trapped thrombus in the filter cone (>25% of filter volume) — anticoagulate and re-image before retrieval
relative: Life expectancy shorter than complication horizon
Uncorrected significant coagulopathy [2]

steps.text: From the jugular sheath: confirm filter position, exclude significant trapped thrombus, assess tilt and hook embedding against the caval wall.
steps.title: Pre-retrieval cavogram

steps.text: Engage the retrieval hook with a gooseneck snare, apply steady tension, and advance the sheath over the collapsing filter until fully captured. Smooth continuous motion beats jerky force.
steps.title: Routine snare retrieval

steps.text: Balloon between filter and wall to free the apex, or use the loop-snare (sling) technique — pass a stiff wire through the filter struts, snare its free end, and use the loop as a handle to align and collapse the apex.
steps.title: Tilted/embedded: displace and engage

steps.text: Long-dwell filters grow a neointimal cap over the hook. Rigid forceps dissect and grasp the apex; the excimer laser sheath photoablates adherent tissue circumferentially while the filter is under tension — high success in referral series [3].
steps.title: Fibrous cap: forceps or laser

steps.text: Count the struts on the ex vivo device (missing tip? fragment search). Completion cavogram: extravasation, dissection flap, stenosis, or retained fragments.
steps.title: Inspect the filter and the cava

steps.text: Small intimal irregularities are common and observed. Extravasation → balloon tamponade, resuscitate, covered stent rarely. Focal stenosis after chronic filters usually needs nothing acutely.
steps.title: Manage findings

postop: Short recovery; resume anticoagulation same day per plan
Counsel on transient neck/back discomfort after complex retrievals
Document device, dwell, technique, and completion findings for the registry
Failed retrieval: refer to a complex-retrieval center rather than serial local attempts

complications.name: Failed retrieval
complications.management: Escalate to advanced techniques or refer; a functioning benign filter can also be left with surveillance if risk-benefit favors it.

complications.name: Caval injury/extravasation
complications.management: Usually self-limited retroperitoneal containment: resuscitate, balloon tamponade, covered stent/surgery exceptionally.

complications.name: Filter fracture during retrieval
complications.management: Retrieve accessible fragments with snare/forceps; embedded asymptomatic wall fragments often observed.

complications.name: Intimal dissection/stenosis
complications.management: Observation; rarely flow-limiting.

complications.name: Arrhythmia / vagal events
complications.management: From wire/sheath manipulation — monitor, pause, resume.

pearls: Retrieval difficulty is mostly a function of dwell time — the cheapest 'advanced technique' is a filter clinic that calls patients at 6 weeks.
If the hook is buried, stop pulling harder with the same tool: each failed high-tension attempt builds scar for the next one.
Trapped clot is not a wall: >25% cone thrombus → anticoagulate 2–4 weeks and re-image, don't strip a PE into the lungs.
Photograph and count the explanted filter — the missing strut you didn't look for is the lawsuit you didn't anticipate.

citations.text: Kaufman JA, Barnes GD, Chaer RA, et al. SIR clinical practice guideline for inferior vena cava filters in patients with venous thromboembolic disease. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2020;31(10):1529-1544.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.
Kuo WT, Odegaard JI, Rosenberg JK, Hofmann LV. Excimer laser-assisted removal of embedded inferior vena cava filters: a single-center prospective study. Circ Cardiovasc Interv. 2013;6(5):560-566.

procedures.title: Lower Extremity Angiography & PAD Intervention
procedures.overview: Catheter-based diagnosis and treatment of peripheral arterial disease, from lifestyle-limiting claudication to chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). Endovascular-first is reasonable for most anatomy, though BEST-CLI showed advantages for bypass with good saphenous vein in surgical candidates — patient selection is the intellectual core of this procedure [1,2].

labs: Noninvasives first: ABI/TBI, segmental pressures, duplex ± CTA to plan access and targets
Creatinine/eGFR; hydrate and minimize contrast when eGFR <45; prophylaxis threshold eGFR <30 [3]
Platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0 practical for intervention [4]
Type & screen for complex recanalizations
preop.meds: Heparin 70–100 U/kg IV once access secured for intervention (target ACT 250–300 s); intra-arterial nitroglycerin 100–200 mcg for spasm, especially tibials. Dual antiplatelet therapy after stent/DCB per institutional protocol.
equipment: 5–6F sheath; up-and-over (contralateral) 45 cm sheath or antegrade access for distal work
Omni flush / pigtail for aortography; Glidewire + angled Glide catheter for crossing
0.035, 0.018, and 0.014 platform balloons; CTO crossing/re-entry devices for chronic occlusions
Self-expanding nitinol stents ± covered stents for SFA; balloon-expandable for ostial iliac
Drug-coated balloons for femoropopliteal; atherectomy for heavy calcium (adjunctive)
Closure device sized to sheath
indications: Lifestyle-limiting claudication (Rutherford 1–3) refractory to exercise therapy and cilostazol
CLTI: ischemic rest pain (Rutherford 4), nonhealing ulcer/tissue loss (5), gangrene (6)
Acute limb ischemia (Rutherford I–IIb) — thrombolysis/thrombectomy pathway
Graft/stent surveillance abnormality with recurrent symptoms

absolute: Nonviable limb (Rutherford III acute ischemia) — primary amputation
Active uncontrolled systemic infection
relative: Severe untreated contrast allergy (premedicate) [3]
eGFR <30 — minimize contrast, consider CO2 angiography [3]
Asymptomatic disease — do not treat images

steps.text: Contralateral CFA access (retrograde) with up-and-over technique for most unilateral disease; antegrade CFA for isolated distal targets. Abdominal aortogram with bilateral oblique pelvic views, then stationed runoff of the index limb to the foot.
steps.title: Access and diagnostic runoff

steps.text: Grade stenoses, measure lengths and reference diameters, assess inflow-outflow. Treat inflow (aortoiliac) before outflow. In CLTI, plan angiosome-directed tibial revascularization to the wound when feasible.
steps.title: Define the lesion and plan

steps.text: Heparinize. Cross stenoses with wire + support catheter. For CTOs: loop (knuckle) wire subintimal tracking with re-entry at the distal cap; confirm intraluminal re-entry with contrast before treating.
steps.title: Anticoagulate and cross

steps.text: Prolonged (2–3 min) inflation sized 1:1 to reference vessel. Tibials: long low-profile balloons, generous nitroglycerin. Assess for recoil and dissection with orthogonal views.
steps.title: Angioplasty

steps.text: Femoropopliteal: DCB or stent per lesion character; stent for flow-limiting dissection or recoil; avoid stenting across the knee flexion point when possible. Iliac: primary stenting for most (balloon-expandable at the ostium). Heavy calcium: consider vessel prep (atherectomy, scoring, intravascular lithotripsy).
steps.title: Definitive therapy

steps.text: Runoff to the foot: confirm brisk inline flow, exclude distal embolization (compare pre/post tibial views), treat any new filling defects with aspiration.
steps.title: Completion angiography

steps.text: Device closure after confirming puncture site anatomy; document distal pulses/doppler signals before leaving the room.
steps.title: Closure

postop: Pulse/access checks per protocol; ambulate per closure method
DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) typically 1–3 months after stent/DCB, then aspirin — individualize
Statin + risk-factor optimization: this is systemic disease; the angioplasty is not the treatment of the disease
Surveillance duplex at 1, 6, 12 months for stents/grafts; wound care team engagement in CLTI
Counsel on smoking cessation at every encounter

complications.name: Access-site complications
complications.management: See Arterial Access module — hematoma, pseudoaneurysm, RP bleed.

complications.name: Flow-limiting dissection
complications.management: Prolonged low-pressure re-inflation; persistent → stent.

complications.name: Distal embolization
complications.management: Aspiration thrombectomy via catheter; residual thrombus → catheter-directed lytics; embolic protection filters during high-risk atherectomy.

complications.name: Vessel rupture/perforation
complications.management: Immediately re-inflate a balloon across the defect for tamponade, then place a covered stent; hold heparin reversal until the defect is controlled (you still need the wire). Surgical backup for iliac rupture with instability.

complications.name: Contrast-associated AKI
complications.management: Prevention is the management: hydration, contrast minimization, CO2 substitution below the diaphragm, staged procedures [3].

complications.name: Early restenosis/thrombosis
complications.management: Duplex surveillance; redo angioplasty (DCB) for in-stent restenosis; acute thrombosis → lysis/thrombectomy and correct the underlying lesion.

pearls: Claudication is a medical disease with an interventional option; CLTI is a surgical/interventional emergency with a medical background — different urgency, different risk tolerance.
Always treat inflow first: a beautiful tibial result dies quickly behind an iliac stenosis.
The knuckle wire is your friend in CTOs — but know where you are: subintimal passage is fine, subintimal re-entry failure is the usual reason to stop.
In BEST-CLI, patients with adequate single-segment GSV did better with bypass — discuss surgical candidacy honestly at multidisciplinary conference [2].

citations.text: Gerhard-Herman MD, Gornik HL, Barrett C, et al. 2016 AHA/ACC guideline on the management of patients with lower extremity peripheral artery disease. Circulation. 2017;135(12):e726-e779.
Farber A, Menard MT, Conte MS, et al. Surgery or endovascular therapy for chronic limb-threatening ischemia (BEST-CLI). N Engl J Med. 2022;387(25):2305-2316.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Hepatic Tumor Thermal Ablation (RFA / Microwave)
procedures.overview: Image-guided thermal destruction of liver tumors — curative-intent therapy for early HCC (BCLC 0/A) and small colorectal metastases in non-surgical candidates. Microwave has largely displaced RF (faster, hotter, less heat-sink vulnerability), but the governing concept is unchanged: complete tumor coverage plus a ≥5–10 mm circumferential margin ('A0' ablation), verified on immediate post-contrast imaging [1,2].

labs: High bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [3]
LFTs, bilirubin, AFP (HCC) or CEA (CRLM) baseline; Child-Pugh/MELD for cirrhotics
Multiphase CT/MRI within ~4–6 weeks: size, number, segment, and neighbors of every target
preop.meds: General anesthesia or deep sedation (thermal pain, controlled breath-holds). Antibiotic prophylaxis routine at many centers; mandatory-extended if bilioenteric anastomosis.
equipment: Microwave antenna or RF electrode system (know your device's ablation-zone chart)
US for real-time targeting ± CT/fusion for confirmation and margins; cone-beam or contrast-enhanced US as adjuncts
Hydrodissection setup (D5W for RF; saline acceptable for MW), artificial ascites/pleural infusion kit
Contrast for immediate post-ablation imaging; grounding pads for RF
indications: HCC within Milan-type criteria (classically ≤3 lesions ≤3 cm, or solitary ≤4–5 cm) in patients not undergoing resection/transplant — curative-intent for BCLC 0/A [2]
Colorectal liver metastases: small-volume disease in non-surgical candidates or as part of a combined plan
Bridge to transplant (maintaining patients within criteria)
Other histologies (NET, breast) in selected oligometastatic settings

absolute: Uncorrectable coagulopathy; tumor abutting structures that cannot be protected (central bile ducts — main duct injury is disqualifying); decompensated liver failure without transplant plan
relative: Lesions adjacent to bowel, gallbladder, diaphragm, heart (protective techniques required: hydrodissection, artificial ascites)
Bilioenteric anastomosis or biliary stent (liver abscess risk climbs sharply — extended antibiotics, individualized decision)
Perivascular location next to large veins (heat sink → higher local recurrence, favors microwave/larger margins)

steps.text: Map antenna positions against the device's ablation-zone dimensions so tumor + ≥5 mm margin (10 mm for CRLM) fits inside the planned zone; plan overlapping ablations for anything >2.5–3 cm [1].
steps.title: Plan the burn, not the puncture

steps.text: Hydrodissect bowel/stomach/gallbladder away (D5W with RF), create artificial ascites for diaphragm/capsule lesions, plan transhepatic (never direct) tumor approaches.
steps.title: Protect the neighbors

steps.text: US-guided placement through normal parenchyma into the deep tumor margin first; confirm position in two planes (CT confirmation for US-occult or dome lesions).
steps.title: Place the applicator

steps.text: Run the manufacturer's protocol; watch the echogenic cloud/low-density zone develop. Reposition for overlapping zones deep-to-superficial (gas obscures deeper targets after the first burn).
steps.title: Ablate with monitoring

steps.text: Cauterize the applicator track (hemostasis + tract-seeding prevention).
steps.title: Track ablation on withdrawal

steps.text: Contrast-enhanced CT/MR (or CEUS) on the table: enhancing residual tumor or a thin margin gets re-treated now, not at 3-month follow-up [1].
steps.title: Verify A0 immediately

postop: Observation/overnight stay; post-ablation syndrome (fever, malaise, pain 2–5 days) is expected and self-limited
LFT bump proportional to ablated volume; delayed fever spike >1 week = think abscess
Baseline contrast MRI/CT at ~1 month, then q3 months — benign peri-ablational enhancement fades, nodular enhancement is recurrence (LI-RADS TR algorithm)
Tumor markers trended (AFP/CEA)

complications.name: Hemorrhage (subcapsular/peritoneal)
complications.management: Track ablation and transhepatic routes prevent; embolization when significant.

complications.name: Liver abscess
complications.management: Drainage + antibiotics; risk-stratify and prophylax before, not after.

complications.name: Bile duct injury/biloma/stricture
complications.management: Respect a 1 cm buffer from central ducts; drain bilomas, stent strictures.

complications.name: Thermal injury to bowel/diaphragm/gallbladder
complications.management: Hydrodissection/ascites are prevention; perforation needs surgery — colon is the least forgiving neighbor.

complications.name: Tumor track seeding
complications.rate: <1% (higher with subcapsular HCC + no track ablation)
complications.management: Track ablation, avoid direct superficial punctures; treat seeded deposits focally.

complications.name: Local tumor progression
complications.management: Re-ablation is usually feasible — margins are the modifiable risk factor [1].

complications.name: Pneumothorax/hepatic-pleural issues (dome lesions)
complications.management: Artificial ascites/pleural effusion technique; chest tube if needed.

pearls: The margin is the operation: local recurrence tracks with margin <5 mm far more than with device choice — plan A0, verify A0 on the table [1].
Heat sink is real physics: a ≥3 mm vessel wicks heat off the ablation zone; answer with microwave power, repositioning, or accepting that segment needs another modality.
Never lance a subcapsular tumor directly — route through 2–3 cm of normal liver so the track can be ablated and the capsule protected.
Bilioenteric anastomosis changes the risk calculus completely: gut flora colonize the biliary tree and abscess rates jump — extended antibiotics or reconsider ablation.

citations.text: Ahmed M, Solbiati L, Brace CL, et al. Image-guided tumor ablation: standardization of terminology and reporting criteria — a 10-year update. Radiology. 2014;273(1):241-260.
European Association for the Study of the Liver. EASL clinical practice guidelines: management of hepatocellular carcinoma. J Hepatol. 2018;69(1):182-236.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Lung Tumor Ablation (RFA / Microwave / Cryo)
procedures.overview: CT-guided thermal ablation of primary and metastatic lung tumors for patients who can't lose more lung: medically inoperable stage I NSCLC and small oligometastases (classically colorectal). Aerated lung insulates — so tumors heat well but margins are judged by the ground-glass halo, and pneumothorax is less a complication than an expected event to manage [1,2].

labs: High bleeding-risk labs per SIR [3]; PFTs to quantify reserve
PET/CT staging current — ablating an understaged patient helps no one
Review path: shortest aerated-lung route avoiding fissures, bullae, and vessels >3 mm
preop.meds: General anesthesia or deep sedation; ipsilateral-side-down positioning afterward. Antibiotics not routine.
equipment: CT (± fluoro-CT) guidance; RF electrode, microwave antenna, or cryoprobes (cryo favored near chest wall/mediastinum for pain and visible ice-ball margins)
Chest tube/pleural drainage kit on the table — not in the cabinet
Grounding pads for RF; device-specific ablation charts for margin planning
indications: Medically inoperable stage I NSCLC (alternative/complement to SBRT) [1]
Pulmonary oligometastases ≤3 cm (colorectal, sarcoma, renal) with controlled primary — lung-parenchyma-sparing local control [2]
Recurrence after radiation/surgery where re-treatment options are exhausted
Palliation of a symptomatic chest-wall-invading mass (pain control)

absolute: Uncorrectable coagulopathy; tumor encasing hilar structures/main bronchi; pneumonectomy contralateral to target with no pulmonary reserve
relative: Severe emphysema/bullae along the path (pneumothorax near-certain, plan for it)
Tumors >3–4 cm (local control falls off; consider combination therapy)
Central lesions near heart, esophagus, phrenic/recurrent laryngeal nerves
Prior pneumonectomy (any pneumothorax is a crisis — pleural blood patch, lowest-risk trajectory)

steps.text: Plan the shortest path through aerated lung that avoids fissures, bullae, and sizable vessels; entry site positioned so the patient can lie puncture-side-down post-procedure.
steps.title: Trajectory planning

steps.text: Advance the electrode/antenna in a single deliberate pass under CT (each pleural re-puncture adds pneumothorax risk); confirm within-target position in orthogonal planes.
steps.title: Applicator placement

steps.text: Run the protocol until ground-glass opacity encircles the tumor by ≥5 mm (ice ball ≥5 mm beyond margin for cryo) — the GGO halo is the lung's version of the ablation zone [1,2].
steps.title: Ablate to a visible margin

steps.text: Reposition for overlapping zones on larger/irregular tumors; treat the deep aspect first before GGO obscures it.
steps.title: Overlap as needed

steps.text: Ablate the track on withdrawal (hemostasis/seeding); immediate CT for pneumothorax, hemorrhage, and zone coverage.
steps.title: Track ablation and withdrawal

steps.text: Small stable pneumothorax: observe with repeat CT/CXR. Enlarging/symptomatic: aspirate or place the chest tube you already opened.
steps.title: Manage the pleura

postop: Puncture-site-down bed rest; CXR at ~1–2 h and again before discharge (delayed pneumothorax happens)
Expect fever/malaise (post-ablation syndrome) and mild hemoptysis for a few days — brisk hemoptysis is not expected, return immediately

Baseline contrast CT/PET at ~1 month then q3 months: the ablation zone starts LARGER than the tumor and involutes — size alone doesn't equal recurrence; nodular/eccentric enhancement or growing PET avidity does [1]
Overnight observation common; same-day discharge feasible in selected patients

complications.name: Pneumothorax
complications.management: The expected event: observe small, drain enlarging/symptomatic; delayed presentation possible — counsel before discharge.

complications.name: Pulmonary hemorrhage/hemoptysis
complications.management: Usually self-limited; position bleeding-side-down; bronchial artery embolization for the rare massive bleed.

complications.name: Pleural effusion / pleuritis
complications.management: Analgesia; drain large symptomatic effusions.

complications.name: Bronchopleural fistula / persistent air leak
complications.management: Prolonged drainage, blood patch, endobronchial valves for the refractory.

complications.name: Infection/abscess in the ablation cavity
complications.management: Cavitation is normal healing; fever + air-fluid level is not — antibiotics ± drainage.

complications.name: Nerve injury (phrenic, recurrent laryngeal, brachial plexus, intercostal)
complications.management: Respect anatomic danger zones; cryo + hydrodissection near nerves.

complications.name: Local progression
complications.management: Re-ablation, SBRT, or systemic therapy — surveillance discipline finds it treatable.

pearls: Consent for pneumothorax as an expectation, not a possibility — and have the chest tube open on the back table before the first pleural pass.
The GGO halo is your margin gauge: no circumferential 5 mm halo, no complete ablation — keep burning or add an overlapping position [1].
Post-ablation zones grow before they shrink; a 'bigger lesion' at 1 month is physiology, a nodular enhancing rim at 6 months is tumor.
One pleural puncture, one track: dithering at the pleura with multiple partial passes is the fastest route to a chest tube.

citations.text: Dupuy DE. Image-guided thermal ablation of lung malignancies. Radiology. 2011;260(3):633-655.
Ahmed M, Solbiati L, Brace CL, et al. Image-guided tumor ablation: standardization of terminology and reporting criteria — a 10-year update. Radiology. 2014;273(1):241-260.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Musculoskeletal Tumor Ablation (Osteoid Osteoma & Bone Mets)
procedures.overview: CT-guided thermal ablation for benign bone tumors (osteoid osteoma — essentially curative) and for palliation of painful bone metastases, often combined with cementoplasty for structural support in weight-bearing bone. Rapid, durable pain relief that spares the morbidity of surgery or the latency of radiation [1,2].

labs: Low bleeding-risk: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0–3.0 [3]
Diagnostic imaging (thin-section CT for osteoid osteoma nidus; MRI/CT for metastasis extent and cortex)
Biopsy plan for metastases of unknown primary (sample before/at ablation)
Neuro exam for spinal/near-nerve lesions
preop.meds: General anesthesia is common (bone drilling/ablation is painful and requires stillness); analgesia; no routine antibiotics.
equipment: CT guidance; bone access/drill system to reach intraosseous targets
RF or microwave probes (osteoid osteoma) / cryoablation probes (larger metastases, visible iceball, less painful)
Thermal protection: hydrodissection, CO2, warm/cold saline; thermocouples for nerve monitoring; skin warming/cooling
Cementoplasty (PMMA) system for structural augmentation after ablation
Neuromonitoring for lesions near neural structures
indications: Osteoid osteoma (classic night pain relieved by NSAIDs) — RFA/cryo of the nidus is first-line, highly effective
Painful bone metastasis refractory to or awaiting radiation, or previously irradiated
Oligometastatic local control (selected)
Impending/painful lesions in weight-bearing bone (ablation + cementoplasty for stabilization)

absolute: Lesion immediately adjacent to critical unprotectable structure (spinal cord, major nerve/vessel) without a protection strategy
Uncorrectable coagulopathy; active infection
relative: Large lytic lesion with cortical breakthrough and instability (may need surgical fixation ± cement)
Lesion in a location where thermal protection is difficult
Diffuse metastatic pain (systemic therapy/radiation better)

steps.text: Thin-section CT pinpoints the osteoid osteoma nidus (lucent center ± calcification) or defines the painful metastasis and its cortex. Plan a corridor and any protection needs.
steps.title: Localize the target

steps.text: Drill/coaxial bone needle to the nidus or into the tumor. For osteoid osteoma, position the probe within the nidus itself — that is the therapeutic target.
steps.title: Access the bone

steps.text: Core the lesion before ablation when histology is needed (ablation precludes later sampling).
steps.title: Biopsy (metastases)

steps.text: For lesions near nerves/skin/bowel: hydrodissection or gas insufflation to displace, thermocouples to monitor neural temperature, warm/cold saline to buffer, and active skin thermal protection. This is what makes near-nerve ablation safe.
steps.title: Deploy thermal protection

steps.text: Osteoid osteoma: RFA to ~90°C for ~5–6 min covers a typical <1 cm nidus — near-100% clinical success. Metastases: cryoablation (freeze-thaw cycles, iceball engulfing the painful bone-soft tissue interface) or heat, sized to the symptomatic margin rather than always the whole tumor for palliation.
steps.title: Ablate

steps.text: For weight-bearing lytic lesions (acetabulum, vertebral body, proximal femur selected), inject PMMA under fluoro/CT after ablation to restore load-bearing and add pain relief.
steps.title: Cementoplasty (if structural)

steps.text: Post-ablation imaging documents coverage; track-manage probe removal (thaw cryoprobes fully). Neuro check on emergence.
steps.title: Confirm and withdraw

postop: Osteoid osteoma: dramatic, often immediate night-pain relief; discharge same day; return to activity quickly
Metastasis palliation: pain relief over days; continue systemic therapy/radiation as planned
Monitor for post-ablation pain flare (transient), skin/nerve injury, and — for weight-bearing lesions — mechanical stability
Neuro checks for spine/near-nerve ablations; delayed deficits warrant imaging
Follow-up imaging for local control (metastases) and to confirm resolution (osteoid osteoma recurrence is uncommon but retreatable)

complications.name: Thermal injury to nerve
complications.management: Thermocouple monitoring, hydro-/gas dissection, and staying a safe margin from neural structures are prevention; deficits → steroids, time, neurology.

complications.name: Skin burn / frostbite
complications.management: Active skin thermal protection (warming/cooling), monitor superficial temperatures; wound care if injured.

complications.name: Post-ablation pain flare
complications.management: Analgesia/short steroid taper; self-limited.

complications.name: Fracture through treated bone
complications.management: Cementoplasty/surgical fixation for weight-bearing lesions reduces risk; manage established fracture per orthopedics.

complications.name: Infection
complications.management: Sterile technique; treat established infection.

complications.name: Incomplete ablation / recurrence
complications.management: Retreatable — repeat ablation for recurrent nidus or symptomatic residual metastasis.

pearls: Osteoid osteoma is one of IR's cleanest wins: hit the nidus, cook it ~90°C for a few minutes, and the crippling night pain is usually gone by morning.
For metastatic palliation, treat the painful bone–tumor interface, not necessarily the whole tumor — the goal is analgesia, not radiographic cure.
Cryoablation near nerves is often preferable: the iceball is visible and less painful, and thermocouples plus dissection keep neural temps in the safe zone.
Weight-bearing bone gets cement: ablation kills pain, cementoplasty carries load — pair them in the acetabulum and selected vertebrae.

citations.text: Rosenthal DI, Hornicek FJ, Torriani M, Gebhardt MC, Mankin HJ. Osteoid osteoma: percutaneous treatment with radiofrequency energy. Radiology. 2003;229(1):171-175.
Callstrom MR, Dupuy DE, Solomon SB, et al. Percutaneous image-guided cryoablation of painful metastases involving bone: multicenter trial. Cancer. 2013;119(5):1033-1041.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Myelography
procedures.overview: Fluoroscopically guided intrathecal injection of non-ionic contrast followed by CT (CT myelography) to opacify the thecal sac and nerve roots — reserved for patients who can't get MRI (hardware, pacemakers, claustrophobia) or where dynamic/positional information and CSF leak localization are needed. The two absolutes: only myelographic-approved non-ionic contrast intrathecally, and keep the head up afterward [1].

labs: Manage anticoagulation as for neuraxial/lumbar puncture — INR normalized, antiplatelets/anticoagulants held per neuraxial windows (more conservative than routine low-risk procedures) [2]
Confirm the contrast on the tray is myelography-labeled non-ionic (iohexol/iopamidol myelographic formulation)
Review prior imaging for level of pathology and safe puncture level
preop.meds: Local anesthesia; light sedation optional. Hold seizure-threshold-lowering drugs (some phenothiazines, certain antidepressants) per protocol pre/post.
equipment: Fluoroscopy table with tilt; 20–22G spinal needle (atraumatic/pencil-point reduces headache)
Myelographic non-ionic contrast ONLY — double-checked
CT scanner for post-myelogram imaging; decubitus positioning aids for CSF leak studies
indications: Spinal stenosis/nerve root compression evaluation when MRI is contraindicated or degraded by hardware
CSF leak localization (spontaneous intracranial hypotension, post-surgical/traumatic dural tear) — often dynamic/decubitus CT myelography
Post-operative spine with extensive instrumentation
Surgical planning requiring bony detail + neural compression (CT myelogram)

absolute: Use of ionic/non-approved contrast intrathecally (causes seizures/death — never); overlying skin infection; markedly elevated ICP with mass effect (herniation risk)
relative: Coagulopathy/anticoagulation (spinal/epidural hematoma) — treat as a neuraxial procedure [2]
History of seizures (hold lowering-threshold meds; use lowest effective contrast dose)
Contrast allergy (premedicate)

steps.text: Prone (or decubitus for leaks); target a widely patent level, commonly L2–L3 or L3–L4, below the conus and away from the tightest stenosis.
steps.title: Position and select level

steps.text: Fluoro-guided midline or paramedian puncture; confirm free CSF return. A small test of contrast under fluoro confirms intrathecal (contrast layers/flows within the sac) vs subdural/epidural (loculated, static).
steps.title: Access the thecal sac

steps.text: Inject the approved non-ionic contrast intrathecally; observe the column flow and fill nerve root sleeves.
steps.title: Instill contrast

steps.text: Tilt the table to run contrast to the levels of interest (head slightly up to keep contrast from pooling intracranially); acquire fluoro spot films in relevant projections.
steps.title: Distribute to the region of interest

steps.text: Transfer promptly to CT for thin-section imaging; decubitus/dynamic acquisitions for CSF leak localization.
steps.title: CT myelogram

steps.text: Keep the head of bed elevated (~30–45°) for several hours — the single most important instruction to limit intracranial contrast pooling and seizures.
steps.title: Post-procedure positioning

postop: Head-of-bed elevated ~30–45° for 6–8 h; encourage hydration
Post-dural-puncture headache surveillance (positional headache) — conservative care first; epidural blood patch for refractory leaks
Seizure precautions per contrast load; resume held meds per protocol
Return precautions: severe headache, fever/neck stiffness (meningitis), new neuro deficit, seizure

complications.name: Post-dural puncture headache
complications.management: Hydration, caffeine, analgesia; epidural blood patch if persistent.

complications.name: Seizure / neurotoxicity
complications.management: Prevention is absolute: correct non-ionic agent, dose limits, head-up positioning; benzodiazepines to abort.

complications.name: Wrong-agent intrathecal injection
complications.management: Catastrophic (ascending myoclonus/death) — the double-check of the vial is the safeguard.

complications.name: Infection (meningitis)
complications.management: Sterile technique; fever + meningismus → LP and antibiotics.

complications.name: Spinal/epidural hematoma
complications.management: Neuraxial anticoagulation precautions; emergent MRI + neurosurgery for new deficit.

complications.name: Contrast reaction
complications.management: Premedicate the allergic; standard treatment.

pearls: Read the vial out loud: intrathecal contrast must be a myelography-approved non-ionic agent — an ionic vial in the thecal sac is lethal.
Head up, not down, afterward: gravity keeps contrast off the brain and blunts both seizures and headache.
CT myelography earns its place mostly where MRI can't go — extensive hardware, non-MRI-compatible devices, or dynamic CSF-leak hunting.
Treat the puncture like any neuraxial procedure for anticoagulation — this is not a garden-variety low-risk stick [2].

citations.text: Sanelli PC, Deshmukh M, Ougorets I, et al. Safety and feasibility of using same-day contrast agents for CT myelography and cisternography. AJNR Am J Neuroradiol. 2005;26(3):529-532.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Percutaneous Nephrostomy
procedures.overview: Image-guided access to the renal collecting system to relieve obstruction, divert urine, or establish a tract for stone/ureteral intervention. In an obstructed, infected kidney (pyonephrosis) it is an emergency drainage that outperforms retrograde stenting for source control. Access through a posterior calyx via Brödel's relatively avascular plane minimizes bleeding [1].

labs: HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8; correct before elective PCN [3]
CBC, creatinine/K+ (obstruction → hyperkalemia), blood/urine cultures if infected
Cross-sectional imaging: degree of hydronephrosis, stone burden, anatomy, and a safe posterior approach
Antibiotics (essential in infection)
preop.meds: Antibiotics before manipulation — instrumenting an infected system causes urosepsis [4]. Sedation/analgesia; local anesthesia.
equipment: US (access) + fluoro (tract/tube); micropuncture/one-stick nephrostomy set
21–22G access needle, 0.018 then 0.035 stiff wire (Amplatz)
Serial fascial dilators, peel-away sheath
8–10F locking pigtail nephrostomy catheter (larger/sump for pus or PCNL access)
Contrast for pyelogram (inject sparingly if infected)
indications: Obstructive uropathy relief (stone, malignancy, stricture) — especially with infection or renal failure
Pyonephrosis / infected obstructed system (urologic emergency: decompress now)
Urinary diversion for leak/fistula, hemorrhagic cystitis, or ureteral injury
Access for antegrade ureteral stenting, stone removal (PCNL), or endopyelotomy

absolute: Uncorrectable severe coagulopathy in a non-emergent setting
relative: Hyperkalemia/severe metabolic derangement — may need dialysis first, but infected obstruction still needs drainage
Terminal illness where drainage does not serve goals of care
Contrast allergy (limited/CO2 pyelogram) [2]

steps.text: Prone or prone-oblique. US identifies a posterior-inferior calyx (not the renal pelvis) as the target — entering through a calyx via the relatively avascular Brödel plane, not the pelvis, avoids the interlobar vessels and the posterior segmental artery.
steps.title: Position and target a posterior calyx

steps.text: US-guided micropuncture into the chosen posterior calyx; aspirate urine (send culture in infection), confirming intracalyceal position. Avoid a direct pelvic puncture (higher vascular injury and tube instability).
steps.title: Access the calyx

steps.text: Gentle contrast (or air/CO2) confirms collecting-system anatomy. In pyonephrosis, aspirate/decompress and inject minimally — over-distending an infected system drives septic shock.
steps.title: Opacify carefully

steps.text: Advance the 0.018 wire, place the transitional dilator, then exchange for a stiff 0.035 wire coiled in the renal pelvis or down the ureter for support.
steps.title: Secure the wire

steps.text: Serially dilate to the chosen catheter size (larger for PCNL access, planned in coordination with urology). Keep the tract through the calyx/parenchyma, not across the pelvis.
steps.title: Dilate the tract

steps.text: Advance the locking pigtail over the wire into the renal pelvis; form and lock the pigtail. Confirm the loop sits in the pelvis, not the ureter or a peripheral calyx.
steps.title: Place and form the tube

steps.text: Nephrostogram/spot confirms position and drainage; secure to skin, connect to a bag. Document urine character and output.
steps.title: Confirm and secure

postop: Expect transient hematuria that clears over hours — persistent gross hematuria or clots suggests vascular injury
Antibiotics per infection; monitor for post-drainage urosepsis (rigors, hypotension) in the first 24 h
Track output and creatinine trend; post-obstructive diuresis can be brisk — monitor volume/electrolytes
Flush only if ordered; secure fixation to prevent dislodgement before tract maturation (~2–4 weeks)
Plan definitive management with urology: antegrade stent, stone therapy, or tube removal once obstruction resolved

complications.name: Hemorrhage / vascular injury
complications.management: Posterior calyceal (Brödel) access is prevention. Persistent bleeding/clot colic or dropping Hgb → CT angio; pseudoaneurysm/AVF → selective embolization. Tamponade by upsizing/clamping the tube temporarily.

complications.name: Urosepsis
complications.management: Antibiotics first, decompress with minimal injection; established sepsis → resuscitation, escalate antibiotics, ICU.

complications.name: Pleural injury (upper pole access)
complications.management: Below the 12th rib when possible; pneumo/hydrothorax → chest tube.

complications.name: Adjacent organ injury (colon, spleen, liver)
