complications.management: CT planning for retro/ interposed colon; manage per injury, surgical consult.

complications.name: Tube dislodgement/dysfunction
complications.management: Secure fixation; exchange over wire; before tract maturation, re-access may be needed rather than blind reinsertion.

complications.name: Post-obstructive diuresis / electrolyte shifts
complications.management: Monitor urine output and electrolytes; replace fluids judiciously.

pearls: Target a posterior calyx, never the pelvis: Brödel's avascular plane is the whole reason PCN is safe — a pelvic puncture rides the big vessels.
Pyonephrosis is a drain-now emergency: antibiotics, minimal contrast, decompress — the classic mistake is a beautiful pyelogram that seeds septic shock.
Below the 12th rib keeps you out of the pleura; supracostal upper-pole access buys stone-surgery angles at the cost of thoracic risk.
New pseudoaneurysm should be on your mind for any delayed post-PCN bleeding — embolization is definitive, transfusion alone is not.

citations.text: Ramchandani P, Cardella JF, Grassi CJ, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for percutaneous nephrostomy. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2001;12(11):1247-1251.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Chehab MA, Thakor AS, Tulin-Silver S, et al. Adult and pediatric antibiotic prophylaxis during vascular and interventional radiology procedures: SIR practice parameter. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2018;29(11):1483-1501.

procedures.title: Paracentesis & Thoracentesis
procedures.overview: Ultrasound-guided aspiration of peritoneal or pleural fluid for diagnosis (SAAG, cell counts, cultures, cytology, Light criteria) or therapeutic relief of symptomatic effusions. Among the most common bedside IR-adjacent procedures — ultrasound guidance has markedly reduced complications [1,2].

labs: Both are LOW bleeding-risk: routine coagulation testing/correction NOT required in most patients per SIR; do not reflexively transfuse for ascitic taps [3]
Send fluid appropriately: ascites (cell count/differential, albumin for SAAG, culture, cytology, total protein); pleural (LDH, protein, pH, cell count, culture, cytology — apply Light criteria)
Paired serum albumin/LDH/protein for gradient calculations
Ultrasound to mark the pocket and depth
preop.meds: Local anesthesia. For large-volume paracentesis, prepare albumin replacement (see steps).
equipment: US with sterile technique; local anesthetic
Paracentesis: catheter-over-needle kit + vacuum bottles/drainage bag
Thoracentesis: dedicated kit (catheter-over-needle, three-way stopcock, drainage line)
Manometer (optional, thoracentesis) to monitor pleural pressure
Albumin 25% for large-volume paracentesis (~6–8 g per liter removed beyond 5 L)
indications: Diagnostic: new ascites/effusion, suspected SBP or malignancy, characterize transudate vs exudate
Therapeutic: symptomatic large-volume ascites (dyspnea, early satiety) or effusion (dyspnea)
Monitoring response to therapy; sampling for cytology in known malignancy

absolute: No safe fluid pocket on ultrasound
relative: Overlying cellulitis; severe uncorrected coagulopathy (routine taps are low-risk — see labs)
Loculated/complex fluid (image-guided catheter may be better)
For thoracentesis: very small effusion, mechanical ventilation (higher pneumothorax risk — use US, small volume)

steps.text: Identify the largest, safest fluid pocket. Paracentesis: classically the left lower quadrant lateral to the rectus (avoid the inferior epigastric vessels, distended bladder/bowel). Thoracentesis: posterolateral, above the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle, over a pocket that persists on quiet breathing.
steps.title: Ultrasound survey

steps.text: Sterile prep; local anesthesia down to the peritoneum/pleura, aspirating as you go. Z-track (paracentesis) reduces post-procedure leak.
steps.title: Anesthetize and enter

steps.text: Advance the catheter-over-needle into the pocket under (or immediately after) US localization; thread the catheter and withdraw the needle to avoid lacerating structures on movement.
steps.title: Access the fluid

steps.text: Collect diagnostic samples first. Therapeutic: drain to symptomatic relief. Thoracentesis: stop for chest tightness, persistent cough, or if pleural pressure drops sharply — limit to ~1–1.5 L to avoid re-expansion edema.
steps.title: Sample and/or drain

steps.text: For large-volume paracentesis (>5 L), give albumin (~6–8 g/L removed) to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction, especially in cirrhotics [1].
steps.title: Volume replacement (paracentesis)

steps.text: Withdraw the catheter; the Z-track/skin closure limits leak. Apply dressing; document volume and fluid character.
steps.title: Remove and dress

postop: Brief observation; vitals check
Thoracentesis: post-procedure CXR not routinely required after uncomplicated US-guided taps in asymptomatic patients — image for symptoms, difficult tap, or aspirated air [2]
Watch for hypotension after large-volume paracentesis (circulatory dysfunction) — albumin mitigates
Return precautions: dyspnea, chest pain, persistent leak, fever
Route diagnostic results (SAAG >1.1 g/dL = portal hypertension; Light criteria for exudate) to the managing team

complications.name: Post-procedure fluid leak (paracentesis)
complications.management: Z-track technique prevents; persistent leak → single suture, ostomy bag, or drain.

complications.name: Pneumothorax (thoracentesis)
complications.management: US guidance and above-the-rib technique minimize; small/asymptomatic observe, larger → aspiration/chest tube.

complications.name: Re-expansion pulmonary edema
complications.management: Limit removal to ~1–1.5 L, stop for symptoms/pressure drop; supportive care/O2 if it occurs.

complications.name: Bleeding (abdominal wall/intercostal vessel, hemoperitoneum)
complications.management: Avoid epigastric/intercostal vessels with US; significant bleeding → CT, angiography/embolization of the culprit vessel.

complications.name: Post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction
complications.management: Albumin replacement is prevention; treat hypotension with volume/albumin.

complications.name: Visceral (bowel/spleen/liver) injury
complications.management: US pocket confirmation prevents; manage per injury.

pearls: Ultrasound turned these into safe procedures — never tap a pocket you have not personally seen persist through the respiratory cycle.
Routine coags/plasma before an ascitic tap are usually unnecessary: SIR classes it low-risk, and cirrhotics' INR does not predict tap bleeding [3].
The intercostal bundle hides under the rib — go over the rib below it, every time, in the chest.
Big pleural drainage, small increments: stop at cough/tightness or ~1–1.5 L to dodge re-expansion edema; big abdominal drainage, replace albumin.

citations.text: Runyon BA; AASLD. Introduction to the revised AASLD practice guideline management of adult patients with ascites due to cirrhosis 2012. Hepatology. 2013;57(4):1651-1653.
Feller-Kopman D, Berkowitz D, Boiselle P, Ernst A. Large-volume thoracentesis and the risk of reexpansion pulmonary edema. Ann Thorac Surg. 2007;84(5):1656-1661.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Catheter-Directed Therapy for Pulmonary Embolism
procedures.overview: Endovascular treatment of acute PE: catheter-directed thrombolysis (± ultrasound-facilitated) or large-bore mechanical thrombectomy. The evidence matured rapidly — PEERLESS (2025) showed comparable mortality between strategies in intermediate-risk PE, and HI-PEITHO (2026) demonstrated that ultrasound-facilitated CDT plus anticoagulation reduces the composite of PE death/decompensation/recurrence versus anticoagulation alone in intermediate-high-risk patients [1,2]. PERT-team triage is the standard of care.

labs: CTA: clot burden and RV/LV ratio; bedside echo for RV function
Troponin, BNP, lactate; CBC, coags, fibrinogen if lytics contemplated
Risk-stratify formally (ESC class / PESI or sPESI) and convene PERT
Type & screen; large-bore access planning (veins on US)
preop.meds: Therapeutic heparin immediately at diagnosis. Minimal sedation for tenuous RV physiology (preload-dependent!); anesthesia presence for high-risk cases; ECMO team aware for massive PE.
equipment: US-guided femoral venous access; 24F DrySeal-type sheath for large-bore aspiration (FlowTriever) or 8–10F for lytic catheters/EKOS
Pigtail + wire for PA catheterization; hemodynamic transducers (RA/PA pressures pre/post)
Aspiration system with blood-return/limiting technology or dual catheters for bilateral disease
Alteplase for CDT protocols (commonly ~0.5–1 mg/h/catheter, total 12–24 mg overnight or bolus-facilitated regimens)
indications: High-risk (massive) PE with contraindication to or failure of systemic lysis (systemic tPA remains first-line when not contraindicated)
Intermediate-high-risk PE: RV strain (CT RV/LV >1.0, echo) + positive biomarkers with clinical concern — via PERT consensus [1,2]
Clot-in-transit (individualized, echo-guided urgency)
Deteriorating intermediate-risk patients on anticoagulation

absolute: For lytics: recent ICH/CNS lesion, active bleeding — favor mechanical-only
Non-acute chronic PE physiology (CTEPH) — different disease, refer for PTE/BPA workup
relative: Severe pulmonary hypertension with RV failure — anesthesia/ECMO backup; induction can arrest these patients
Severe renal impairment (contrast); profound thrombocytopenia [3]

steps.text: Confirm risk class, strategy (mechanical vs lytic vs hybrid), and escalation plan (ECMO criteria) before the patient is on the table.
steps.title: Plan with the PERT

steps.text: US-guided femoral vein access; heparinize to protocol. Cross the RA/RV gently with pigtail + wire — minimize RV touch in a strained heart; measure PA pressure (document baseline mPAP).
steps.title: Access and right heart transit

steps.text: Selective main/lobar injections define target clot correlating with CTA. In sick patients, skip aesthetics: CTA roadmap + minimal runs.
steps.title: Pulmonary angiography

steps.text: Advance the large-bore aspiration catheter over wire to engage proximal clot; controlled aspiration passes in main/lobar branches; inspect extracted thrombus. Track blood loss and hemodynamics each pass; treat both sides when disease dictates.
steps.title: Mechanical thrombectomy

steps.text: Bilateral infusion catheters (± ultrasound-facilitated) embedded in clot: alteplase per protocol with ICU monitoring, fibrinogen q6h, catheter removal at 6–24 h per regimen [2].
steps.title: Or catheter-directed lysis

steps.text: Meaningful endpoints: mPAP reduction, heart rate/pressor trend, oxygenation — not angiographic perfection. Chasing distal clot adds risk with diminishing return.
steps.title: Endpoint assessment

steps.text: Large-bore venous closure: purse-string suture / figure-of-eight ± closure device per practice. ICU handoff with explicit RV-watch parameters.
steps.title: Close

postop: ICU monitoring; continue therapeutic anticoagulation seamlessly
Echo at 24–48 h for RV recovery; formal follow-up echo at 3 months
Screen at follow-up for persistent dyspnea → CTEPH workup (V/Q at 3 months in symptomatic patients)
Anticoagulation duration/agent per VTE etiology; IVC filter only if anticoagulation truly contraindicated
Enroll outcomes in your PE registry — local data drives PERT improvement

complications.name: Major bleeding
complications.rate: GUSTO moderate/severe ~2–7% in trials; ICH <1% with catheter techniques
complications.management: Stop lytics, reverse, transfuse; large-bore access sites need vigilant post checks [1,2].

complications.name: RV perforation / tamponade
complications.management: Hypotension + enlarging silhouette → immediate echo, pericardiocentesis, surgical backup. Prevention: respect the RV with wire-first gentle technique.

complications.name: Hemodynamic collapse on the table
complications.management: The strained RV tolerates little: pre-brief ECMO activation, pressors ready (norepi ± vasopressin), avoid intubation-induced preload crash when possible.

complications.name: Tricuspid/pulmonic valve injury
complications.management: From aspiration catheters crossing chordae — wire-guided passage, avoid free-floating advancement; echo if new murmur/RV failure.

complications.name: Significant procedural blood loss (aspiration)
complications.management: Limit passes, use blood-return systems, transfuse per thresholds.

complications.name: Distal embolization/fragmentation
complications.management: Usually tolerated; hemodynamic worsening → continue aspiration distally or add lytic.

pearls: The RV kills before hypoxia does: every decision (sedation, contrast, pass count) filters through 'what does this do to RV preload/afterload right now?'

PEERLESS in one line: LBMT and CDT are both reasonable in intermediate-risk PE — choose by bleeding risk, physiology, and logistics [1]. HI-PEITHO adds: in intermediate-HIGH risk, catheter lysis beats anticoagulation alone for the composite outcome [2].
Do not chase segmental perfection — the endpoint is physiology (mPAP, HR, pressors), not a clean angiogram.
Anticoagulation alone remains correct therapy for most intermediate-low patients; interventional restraint is also expertise.

citations.text: Jaber WA, Gonsalves CF, Stortecky S, et al. Large-bore mechanical thrombectomy versus catheter-directed thrombolysis in the management of intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism: primary results of the PEERLESS randomized controlled trial. Circulation. 2025;151(3):260-273.

Klok FA, Piazza G, Sharp ASP, et al. Ultrasound-facilitated catheter-directed thrombolysis plus anticoagulation versus anticoagulation alone for intermediate-high-risk pulmonary embolism (HI-PEITHO). N Engl J Med. 2026 (published online).
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.
Konstantinides SV, Meyer G, Becattini C, et al. 2019 ESC guidelines for the diagnosis and management of acute pulmonary embolism. Eur Heart J. 2020;41(4):543-603.

procedures.title: Pelvic Trauma Embolization
procedures.overview: Emergent angiography and embolization of pelvic arterial hemorrhage, usually from internal iliac branches after blunt pelvic ring fracture. This is damage-control IR: speed beats elegance, nonselective gelfoam is acceptable in extremis, and the pelvis's rich collateral supply makes bilateral assessment mandatory [1].

labs: Do not wait for labs to normalize — resuscitate through the procedure (MTP running)
Baseline Hgb/coags/fibrinogen to guide transfusion, not to gate the case
Review the trauma CT: side and level of blush, fracture pattern (APC patterns favor posterior branches — superior gluteal, internal pudendal)
preop.meds: Resuscitation per trauma team; sedation/anesthesia per hemodynamics. No prophylactic antibiotics required emergently.
equipment: 5F sheath, aortic flush catheter, Cobra/RIM + microcatheter system
Gelfoam slurry (workhorse temporary embolic in trauma), coils/plugs for focal injuries or pseudoaneurysms
Power injector for pelvic aortogram; capable rapid-run DSA
indications: Pelvic fracture with contrast extravasation/blush on CT [1]
Hemodynamic instability attributable to the pelvis after other sources are excluded or controlled
Ongoing transfusion requirement with pelvic hematoma
Postoperative or postpartum pelvic hemorrhage; bleeding pelvic tumors (same skill set, elective tempo)

absolute: None in exsanguinating hemorrhage — this is a salvage procedure
relative: Patient in arrest / needs immediate laparotomy for another cavity — coordinate with trauma surgery (REBOA, preperitoneal packing, then angio)

steps.text: Flush run at the bifurcation to survey both internal iliacs and lumbar/median sacral contributors — trauma bleeds are frequently multifocal.
steps.title: Pelvic aortogram first

steps.text: Contralateral femoral approach simplifies internal iliac selection (RIM/Cobra over the bifurcation). Anterior vs posterior division runs in obliques.
steps.title: Select the likely side

steps.text: Focal extravasation/pseudoaneurysm: microcatheter as close as feasible, coils or gelfoam 'sandwich' across the injury to block back-door collateral refill.
steps.title: Embolize what you see

steps.text: Unstable patient with fracture pattern but no visualized bleed: nonselective gelfoam embolization of the internal iliac (uni- or bilateral) is accepted damage control [1].
steps.title: Embolize what you don't see (when unstable)

steps.text: Always interrogate the contralateral internal iliac before finishing — cross-pelvic collaterals resurrect bleeds.
steps.title: Check the other side

steps.text: Completion runs, sheath left in place if coagulopathic or reoperation likely; communicate embolized territory to trauma/ortho (matters for flap and fixation planning).
steps.title: Completion and handoff

postop: ICU-level monitoring; serial Hgb and transfusion needs — rebleeding prompts repeat angiography
Watch for gluteal ischemia (pain, skin changes) after bilateral or posterior-division embolization
Access site checks; DVT prophylaxis discussion with trauma team (pelvic fracture patients are high risk)
Fever/inflammatory response after gelfoam is common and self-limited

complications.name: Rebleeding / missed bleeder
complications.management: Low threshold to return — repeat angiography including lumbar, median sacral, and external iliac branch injections.

complications.name: Gluteal muscle/skin necrosis
complications.management: Wound care; debridement if needed. Risk is driven more by shock and packing than by gelfoam itself.

complications.name: Sexual dysfunction / bladder ischemia
complications.management: Counsel when elective; largely unavoidable in exsanguination.

complications.name: Non-target embolization
complications.management: Careful reflux control; treat ischemic consequence supportively.

complications.name: Access complications
complications.management: Standard management.

pearls: The unstable pelvic fracture pathway is parallel, not serial: binder + resuscitation + (packing or REBOA) + angio — know your institution's sequence [1].
Gelfoam is the trauma embolic: temporary, forgiving, fast — save coils for the discrete pseudoaneurysm you can park in front of.
No blush ≠ no bleed: vasospasm and intermittent bleeding hide injuries; in the unstable patient, treat the territory the fracture pattern implicates.
Embolize distal AND proximal to a discrete injury (front and back door) — the pelvis collateralizes around a proximal-only coil stack within minutes.

citations.text: Cullinane DC, Schiller HJ, Zielinski MD, et al. Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma practice management guidelines for hemorrhage in pelvic fracture — update and systematic review. J Trauma. 2011;71(6):1850-1868.

procedures.title: Percutaneous Transhepatic Biliary Drainage (PTBD)
procedures.overview: Percutaneous access to the biliary tree to relieve obstruction, drain infected bile (cholangitis), or create a route for further biliary intervention when ERCP fails or is impossible (altered anatomy, hilar tumors). A cornerstone rescue procedure and a classic source of biliary sepsis if bile is over-manipulated [1].

labs: HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8, fibrinogen >150; correct before elective drainage [3]
LFTs (bilirubin), blood cultures if septic; CBC
Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRCP) to define level and cause of obstruction and plan the target duct (Bismuth classification for hilar)
Type & screen
preop.meds: Antibiotics are essential — biliary instrumentation seeds bacteremia; give broad-spectrum coverage before manipulation, especially in cholangitis [4]. Sedation/anesthesia; analgesia.
equipment: US + fluoro; 21–22G Chiba needle for initial cholangiogram/access, micropuncture (0.018) system
0.035 stiff wire (Amplatz), transitional dilators, biliary sheath
Biliary drainage catheters: 8–10F external or internal-external (locking pigtail with sideholes spanning the obstruction into the duodenum)
Angled glide catheter + glidewire to cross the stricture
Contrast; consider CO2 to limit ductal distension in septic bile
indications: Obstructive jaundice (malignant or benign) not amenable to/failed ERCP
Acute cholangitis with biliary obstruction needing urgent decompression
Bile leak diversion, benign stricture management, access for stone removal or stent placement
Preoperative biliary decompression in selected patients (e.g., some hilar cholangiocarcinoma workups)

absolute: Uncorrectable severe coagulopathy in a non-emergent setting
relative: Large-volume ascites (drain first — impairs tract tamponade and drain stability)
Multiple isolated obstructed segments (may need multiple drains for source control)
Contrast allergy — CO2/limited contrast possible [2]

steps.text: Right-sided (mid-axillary, below the costophrenic sulcus) or left-sided (subxiphoid, US-guided) approach based on anatomy and obstruction level. Left approach is more ergonomic under US and reduces operator radiation.
steps.title: Plan the approach

steps.text: Under US (or fluoro after fine-needle cholangiogram), puncture a peripheral (not central) bile duct with the micropuncture needle — peripheral access preserves central ducts and reduces vascular injury. Confirm with gentle contrast; avoid overinjecting infected bile.
steps.title: Access a peripheral duct

steps.text: Define the anatomy and obstruction. In florid cholangitis, decompress first and characterize later — raising ductal pressure with contrast drives septic showers.
steps.title: Cholangiogram — minimally in sepsis

steps.text: Advance the 0.018 wire, place the transitional dilator, then exchange to a 0.035 stiff wire coiled centrally. Maintain wire position meticulously — losing access in the biliary tree is costly.
steps.title: Secure the wire

steps.text: With an angled catheter + glidewire, negotiate across the stricture into the duodenum. Success allows an internal-external drain (bile drains internally, catheter maintains access).
steps.title: Attempt to cross the obstruction

steps.text: If crossed: internal-external locking pigtail with sideholes above and below the obstruction, pigtail in the duodenum. If not crossable: external drain with pigtail formed above the obstruction to decompress; re-attempt crossing after days of decompression reduces edema.
steps.title: Place the drain

steps.text: Lock the pigtail, secure to skin, connect to a bag (external portion). Completion cholangiogram/spot confirms position and drainage; document output.
steps.title: Secure and document

postop: Antibiotics continue per cholangitis course; monitor for post-procedure sepsis/rigors (common transient in first 24–48 h)
Track bilirubin fall and drain output; flush protocol (10 mL saline BID) to maintain patency
Watch for hemobilia (blood in the bag / dropping Hgb) — usually self-limits, but persistent needs angiography
Internal-external drains capped to internalize once bile flow re-established and patient stable
Plan definitive management: stent (metal for malignant, per prognosis), stricture dilation, or surgical route; routine exchanges q2–3 months for chronic drains

complications.name: Biliary sepsis / cholangitis flare
complications.management: Antibiotics before manipulation; decompress-first, minimize ductal pressure. Established sepsis: cultures, escalate antibiotics, ensure the drain is decompressing.

complications.name: Hemobilia / vascular injury
complications.management: Peripheral puncture reduces risk. Persistent hemobilia/hemodynamic change → angiography for hepatic artery pseudoaneurysm (embolize) or portal bleeding; upsizing the tube can tamponade.

complications.name: Bile leak / peritonitis
complications.management: Ensure sideholes are within ducts (not across the capsule); adequate drainage; antibiotics; upsizing/repositioning.

complications.name: Pleural complications (right approach)
complications.management: Access below the costophrenic sulcus; pneumothorax/biliopleural fistula → chest tube/reposition.

complications.name: Tube dysfunction (clog/dislodge)
complications.management: Flushing protocol; exchange over wire; secure fixation reduces dislodgement.

complications.name: Catheter-related pain
complications.management: Analgesia; check sideholes not causing capsular irritation.

pearls: In cholangitis, drain first and interrogate later — the cholangiogram can wait, the septic shower cannot. Antibiotics before you touch the ducts.
Puncture peripherally: central duct punctures ride alongside big vessels and cause the serious hemobilia.
Can't cross the stricture today? Decompress with an external drain and come back — days of drainage shrink the edema and the second attempt often succeeds.
New hemobilia + dropping hematocrit after PTBD = hepatic artery pseudoaneurysm until angiography says otherwise.

citations.text: Saad WEA, Wallace MJ, Wojak JC, et al. Quality improvement guidelines for percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography, biliary drainage, and percutaneous cholecystostomy. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2010;21(6):789-795.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Chehab MA, Thakor AS, Tulin-Silver S, et al. Adult and pediatric antibiotic prophylaxis during vascular and interventional radiology procedures: SIR practice parameter. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2018;29(11):1483-1501.

procedures.title: Portal Vein Embolization
procedures.overview: Embolization of the portal branches feeding the liver destined for resection, redirecting portal flow to the future liver remnant (FLR) so it hypertrophies before major hepatectomy. Turns unresectable-by-volume patients into surgical candidates: expect ~30–60% FLR volume gain over 3–6 weeks [1,2]. The procedure exists entirely in service of someone else's operation — coordination with the surgeon defines every decision.

labs: High bleeding-risk (transhepatic access): platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [3]
CT/MRI volumetry: baseline FLR% calculated and documented — this number is the entire indication
LFTs, bilirubin; confirm oncologic plan and timing with surgery (resection usually 3–6 weeks post-PVE)
preop.meds: Moderate sedation or anesthesia; peri-procedure antibiotics common (institutional).
equipment: US for portal branch puncture (typically ipsilateral/right-sided access into a right portal branch)
Micropuncture set, 5–6F sheath, reverse-curve or angled catheter + microcatheter
Embolics: particles + coils/plugs (common), NBCA glue, or vascular plugs per institutional practice
Portography setup; cone-beam CT helpful for segment 4 branches
indications: Planned major hepatectomy with inadequate FLR: <20–25% in normal liver, <30% after chemotherapy, <40% in cirrhosis/fibrosis [1,2]
Typically right or extended right hepatectomy for colorectal metastases, cholangiocarcinoma, or HCC
Extended resections needing segment 4 branch embolization in addition to the right portal vein

absolute: Tumor invading the FLR or its portal supply; portal vein thrombosis of the target system; patient not actually a surgical candidate
relative: Uncorrectable coagulopathy (transhepatic liver puncture) [3]
Portal hypertension (embolization worsens it acutely)
Biliary dilation in the target lobe (drain first)

steps.text: Re-verify the FLR%, the planned resection line, and whether segment 4 branches need embolization (extended right hepatectomy) — embolizing the wrong segments is unfixable.
steps.title: Confirm the plan against volumetry

steps.text: US-guided transhepatic puncture of a peripheral right portal branch (ipsilateral approach keeps the tract in the liver being removed). Advance a sheath into the main portal vein.
steps.title: Access the portal system

steps.text: Flush portogram maps branch anatomy and variants (trifurcation!); measure portal pressure.
steps.title: Baseline portogram + pressures

steps.text: Systematically embolize right portal branches from peripheral to central — particles distally, then coils/plugs centrally; or glue per practice. Add segment 4 branches if planned, protecting the FLR supply at all times.
steps.title: Embolize the target system

steps.text: Confirm complete target occlusion, brisk redirected flow to the FLR, and absolutely no embolic in FLR branches. Repeat pressures.
steps.title: Completion portogram

steps.text: Embolize the transhepatic tract (gelfoam/coils) on withdrawal.
steps.title: Exit with tract embolization

postop: Overnight observation; post-embolization syndrome is milder than arterial embolization (pain, low-grade fever 1–3 days)
LFTs transiently bump; synthetic function should remain stable — decompensation suggests FLR insufficiency
CT volumetry at 3–4 weeks: FLR% and kinetic growth rate determine surgical timing [2]
Inadequate hypertrophy: discuss salvage (segment 4 completion, hepatic vein embolization/liver venous deprivation, or ALPPS referral)

complications.name: Non-target embolization to the FLR
complications.management: The disaster to prevent — meticulous catheter position and reflux control; may cancel the resection.

complications.name: Subcapsular hematoma / hemoperitoneum
complications.management: Tract embolization prevents; transfuse/embolize arterially if significant.

complications.name: Portal vein thrombosis extending to main PV
complications.management: Anticoagulation ± thrombectomy; jeopardizes surgery.

complications.name: Insufficient hypertrophy
complications.management: Salvage options: segment 4 embolization, hepatic vein embolization (LVD), ALPPS, or reassess candidacy [2].

complications.name: Cholangitis/sepsis (especially with biliary obstruction)
complications.management: Drain obstructed systems before PVE; antibiotics.

pearls: The kinetic growth rate (FLR gain per week) predicts post-hepatectomy liver failure better than the static FLR% — calculate both at follow-up volumetry [2].
Ipsilateral access = your tract lives in the specimen; contralateral access risks injuring the very remnant the whole procedure protects.
Portal trifurcation and segmental variants are common — study the portogram before the first embolic leaves the catheter.
PVE hypertrophies tumor too: many centers pair it with chemotherapy or sequence resection promptly — the clock starts the day you embolize.

citations.text: Abulkhir A, Limongelli P, Healey AJ, et al. Preoperative portal vein embolization for major liver resection: a meta-analysis. Ann Surg. 2008;247(1):49-57.
May BJ, Madoff DC. Portal vein embolization: rationale, technique, and current application. Semin Intervent Radiol. 2012;29(2):81-89.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Prostatic Artery Embolization
procedures.overview: Superselective embolization of the prostatic arteries for lower urinary tract symptoms from benign prostatic hyperplasia, or for refractory prostatic hematuria. Technically demanding pelvic microcatheterization: variant anatomy is the rule and dangerous anastomoses (rectal, vesical, penile) are common. UK-ROPE and randomized data support meaningful IPSS improvement with preservation of sexual function [1,2].

labs: IPSS + quality-of-life score baseline; uroflowmetry (Qmax), post-void residual
PSA and prostate MRI (size, cancer screening, arterial planning); CTA pelvis maps prostatic artery origins
Urinalysis ± culture — treat infection first
Creatinine/eGFR; platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0 [4]
preop.meds: Pre-procedure NSAIDs; some centers give a short peri-procedural antibiotic + anti-inflammatory course; Foley placement helps landmark the urethra (and drainage in retention patients).
equipment: 4–5F sheath (radial or femoral), Cobra/RUC base catheter
High-flow 2.0–2.4F microcatheter + 0.014 microwire (a 'swan-neck' shaped tip helps)
Calibrated microspheres 100–300 μm (± 300–500 μm to finish)
Cone-beam CT — near-mandatory for confirming prostatic parenchymal territory and excluding nontarget supply
Nitroglycerin for spasm
indications: Moderate–severe LUTS (IPSS ≥13–18) from BPH refractory to or intolerant of medical therapy
Refractory hematuria of prostatic origin
Large glands (>80 mL) or patients unfit/unwilling for TURP/HoLEP
Retention with indwelling catheter — PAE as a path to catheter independence

absolute: Untreated prostate cancer as the symptom source (work up PSA abnormalities first)
Active urinary tract infection
relative: Severe atherosclerosis/tortuosity of iliac vessels
eGFR <30 (contrast load is substantial; stage or use CO2/CBCT strategies) [3]
Neurogenic bladder or detrusor failure (symptoms will not respond — urodynamics when in doubt)

steps.text: Identify the prostatic artery origin on each side — most commonly from the internal pudendal, superior vesical/gluteopudendal trunk, or obturator. Note rectal and penile anastomoses ahead of time.
steps.title: Plan from CTA/MRA

steps.text: Select each internal iliac anterior division; oblique ipsilateral 25–35° + caudal angulation profiles the prostatic origin. Hypertrophied BPH glands show a characteristic coiled, hypervascular blush.
steps.title: Pelvic angiography

steps.text: Microcatheter beyond extra-prostatic branches. Nitroglycerin liberally — spasm is constant. Confirm with gentle hand injection.
steps.title: Superselect the prostatic artery

steps.text: CBCT from the planned embolization position: enhancement should be confined to prostate parenchyma. Rectal wall, bladder, or penile enhancement → reposition or protectively coil the anastomosis.
steps.title: Cone-beam CT confirmation

steps.text: Embolize with 100–300 μm spheres to near-stasis; then advance deeper into intraprostatic branches and complete (Proximal Embolization First, Then Embolize Distal) — improves infarction and clinical response [2].
steps.title: Embolize — PErFecTED when feasible

steps.text: Bilateral embolization predicts success; unilateral-only is a common cause of partial response. Same sequence on the other side.
steps.title: Repeat contralaterally

steps.text: Final runs; remove Foley in clinic pathway patients or per retention protocol. Radiation housekeeping: document dose — PAE is among the highest-dose IR procedures; collimate ruthlessly.
steps.title: Complete

postop: Same-day discharge typical: NSAIDs, alpha-blocker continuation, ± short steroid taper per protocol
Post-PAE syndrome: pelvic pain/burning, urinary frequency, mild dysuria/hematuria, hematospermia for days–2 weeks — supportive
Retention patients: trial of void at 1–2 weeks
IPSS improvement accrues over 1–3 months (typical 10–13 point drop; gland volume −30% by 6 months)
Follow-up IPSS/Qmax at 1, 3, 6, 12 months

complications.name: Post-PAE syndrome
complications.management: NSAIDs, hydration, bladder analgesics; resolves within 1–2 weeks.

complications.name: Acute urinary retention
complications.management: Temporary Foley + alpha-blocker; nearly all resolve.

complications.name: Nontarget embolization — rectum, bladder, penis
complications.management: The defining hazard: rectal ulcer, bladder wall ischemia, penile ulceration. Prevent with CBCT and protective coiling; manage ischemic injury supportively with urology/colorectal.

complications.name: UTI/prostatitis
complications.management: Culture-directed antibiotics.

complications.name: Clinical failure / recurrence
complications.management: Re-PAE for recanalization/missed supply, or surgical therapy — PAE does not preclude TURP.

complications.name: Radiation skin injury
complications.management: Long fluoro + CBCT case: monitor dose metrics, rotate gantry angles; follow-up any dose >5 Gy peak skin.

pearls: The prostatic artery is a diagnosis of exclusion in the pelvis: prove what it is NOT feeding (rectum, penis, bladder) before particles fly — CBCT earns its dose.
Corona mortis and accessory pudendal variants are common; the pre-procedure CTA read is half the case.
Bilateral technical success is the strongest predictor of IPSS response; document why whenever you accept unilateral.
Counsel that maximal benefit takes months — the gland shrinks on biology's schedule, not fluoroscopy's.

citations.text: Ray AF, Powell J, Speakman MJ, et al. Efficacy and safety of prostate artery embolization for benign prostatic hyperplasia: an observational study and propensity-matched comparison with transurethral resection of the prostate (UK-ROPE study). BJU Int. 2018;122(2):270-282.
Carnevale FC, Moreira AM, Antunes AA. The 'PErFecTED technique': proximal embolization first, then embolize distal for benign prostatic hyperplasia. Cardiovasc Intervent Radiol. 2014;37(6):1602-1605.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Radioiodine (I-131) Therapy
procedures.time: Outpatient administration; days of radiation precautions
procedures.overview: Oral administration of radioactive iodine-131 for benign hyperthyroidism (Graves', toxic nodule/goiter) and for remnant ablation or treatment of differentiated thyroid cancer after thyroidectomy. I-131 is concentrated by thyroid tissue via the sodium-iodide symporter and delivers targeted beta-radiation; the interventional/procedural issues are dosing, TSH stimulation, iodine avoidance, and radiation-safety precautions rather than any percutaneous step [1].

labs: Negative pregnancy test (childbearing potential) — non-negotiable, day of therapy
TSH stimulation to drive uptake: thyroid hormone withdrawal or recombinant human TSH (thyrogen) per cancer protocol; TSH, thyroglobulin, and anti-Tg antibodies for cancer patients
Low-iodine diet ~1–2 weeks prior; avoid recent iodinated CT contrast (weeks); review amiodarone
Baseline renal function (iodine cleared renally)
preop.meds: Beta-blockade/antithyroid drugs managed around therapy for hyperthyroidism (antithyroid drugs held peri-dose to allow uptake). Steroids if ophthalmopathy risk. Antiemetics as needed.
equipment: Oral I-131 capsule/liquid dispensed and administered by authorized user under NRC/state radiation-safety rules
Radiation-safety materials: written precaution instructions, survey meter, dose calibration records
Post-therapy whole-body scan capability (cancer patients) to document iodine-avid disease
indications: Hyperthyroidism: Graves' disease, toxic adenoma, toxic multinodular goiter (definitive therapy)
Differentiated thyroid cancer (papillary/follicular): remnant ablation and adjuvant treatment of residual/metastatic disease after total thyroidectomy [1]
Treatment of iodine-avid metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer

absolute: Pregnancy (crosses placenta, ablates fetal thyroid) and breastfeeding — a negative pregnancy test is mandatory before dosing
Inability to comply with radiation-safety precautions
relative: Active/severe Graves' ophthalmopathy (RAI can worsen it — steroid cover or alternative therapy)
Recent iodinated contrast or iodine-rich diet/amiodarone (blocks uptake — delay)
Very large compressive goiter (surgery may be preferred)

steps.text: Verify indication, negative pregnancy test, adequate TSH stimulation, completed low-iodine diet, and no recent iodine load that would block uptake.
steps.title: Confirm eligibility and prep

steps.text: Fixed or dosimetry-based activity: lower activities for benign hyperthyroidism (toxic nodule vs Graves'), higher for cancer remnant ablation/metastatic disease per risk stratification [1].
steps.title: Select the activity

steps.text: Authorized user gives the oral dose under radiation-safety protocol; document activity and time.
steps.title: Administer

steps.text: Instruct on distance/time precautions: sleep separately, limit close contact especially with children and pregnant people, hydrate and void frequently, personal-item hygiene — duration scaled to activity and local regulation.
steps.title: Radiation-safety counseling

steps.text: Whole-body I-131 scan several days later maps iodine-avid remnant/metastatic disease and guides follow-up.
steps.title: Post-therapy imaging (cancer)

steps.text: Track thyroid function (expect eventual hypothyroidism after ablation — plan levothyroxine); thyroglobulin surveillance for cancer.
steps.title: Follow-up

postop: Radiation precautions for a defined period (commonly days) per administered activity and jurisdiction — the core 'post-op' instruction
Hydration and frequent voiding to clear renally excreted iodine and reduce bladder/gonadal dose
Expect and plan for hypothyroidism after ablative treatment — thyroid hormone replacement and TSH monitoring
Contraception counseling: avoid pregnancy for ~6–12 months; men counseled on transient fertility effects at high activities

complications.name: Hypothyroidism
complications.management: Anticipated endpoint for Graves'/ablation — levothyroxine replacement.

complications.name: Radiation sialadenitis / xerostomia
complications.management: Hydration, sialogogues (sour candy), salivary massage.

complications.name: Worsening Graves' ophthalmopathy
complications.management: Steroid prophylaxis, smoking cessation, or choose an alternative therapy in active moderate–severe eye disease.

complications.name: Radiation thyroiditis / transient hyperthyroidism
complications.management: Beta-blockade, analgesia; self-limited.

complications.name: Second malignancy / gonadal effects
complications.management: Minimize cumulative activity; hydration; contraception counseling; discuss at high therapeutic activities.

complications.name: Nausea
complications.management: Antiemetics, hydration.

pearls: Pregnancy test the day of therapy, every time — I-131 crosses the placenta and ablates the fetal thyroid.
Uptake requires a hungry, stimulated gland: low-iodine diet, TSH stimulation (withdrawal or thyrogen), and NO recent CT contrast — an iodine-loaded thyroid simply won't take up the dose [1].
Counsel that hypothyroidism is the goal, not a failure, when ablating Graves' or cancer remnant — the plan includes lifelong levothyroxine.
Hydrate and void frequently: most of the non-thyroidal dose leaves through the kidneys, and clearing it protects bladder and gonads.

citations.text: Haugen BR, Alexander EK, Bible KC, et al. 2015 American Thyroid Association management guidelines for adult patients with thyroid nodules and differentiated thyroid cancer. Thyroid. 2016;26(1):1-133.

procedures.title: Renal Artery Angioplasty & Stenting
procedures.overview: Selective renal arteriography with angioplasty ± stenting for renovascular disease. The modern lesson: randomized trials (CORAL, ASTRAL) showed no benefit of routine stenting over medical therapy for atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis [1,2] — so today's indications are the exceptions (flash pulmonary edema, refractory hypertension, FMD, salvage of a failing kidney), and FMD is treated with balloon angioplasty alone.

labs: Low bleeding-risk per SIR thresholds [3]
Creatinine/eGFR; duplex or CTA/MRA defining lesion location (ostial vs truncal) and kidney size
Antiplatelet loading (aspirin ± clopidogrel) before stenting per operator protocol
preop.meds: Local + moderate sedation. Heparin during selective work; antihypertensives ready — post-dilation pressure shifts can be abrupt.
equipment: 6F sheath or guiding catheter (renal double curve/RDC); femoral or radial approach
Selective catheters (Cobra, SOS/Simmons for down-going renals from femoral)
0.014–0.018" wire platforms; balloon-expandable stents for ostial lesions (precise placement); plain balloons for FMD
Pressure wire/gradient setup if lesion significance is uncertain (mean gradient ≥10 mmHg or Pd/Pa criteria)
CO2 capability for CKD
indications: Recurrent flash pulmonary edema or refractory heart failure with bilateral severe RAS (Pickering syndrome)
Hypertension refractory to maximal medical therapy (≥3 agents including a diuretic) with hemodynamically significant stenosis
Progressive renal insufficiency attributable to bilateral RAS or RAS to a solitary functioning kidney
Fibromuscular dysplasia with renovascular hypertension — angioplasty alone, no stent
Renal transplant artery stenosis

absolute: Kidney unlikely to benefit (atrophic <7 cm, resistive index suggesting parenchymal disease with chronic occlusion)
relative: Stable, medically controlled atherosclerotic RAS — trials favor medical management [1,2]
Severe aortic atheroma raising embolization risk
CKD where contrast risk outweighs benefit (consider CO2)

steps.text: Flush aortogram at the renal origins (L1–L2) to define ostial anatomy, accessory renals, and disease. Measure kidney size and note the take-off angle.
steps.title: Aortogram and lesion assessment

steps.text: Engage the ostium with a guiding catheter using a no-touch or telescoping technique to avoid scraping aortic atheroma into the kidney.
steps.title: 'No-touch' selective engagement

steps.text: If the stenosis is 50–70% or symptoms are equivocal, measure a translesional gradient rather than eyeballing — anatomy alone overcalls significance.
steps.title: Confirm significance

steps.text: Cross with a 0.014–0.018" wire kept in view at all times (wire perforation of a pole branch is the classic error). FMD: angioplasty alone to break the webs. Atherosclerotic ostial disease: balloon-expandable stent protruding ~1 mm into the aorta.
steps.title: Cross and treat

steps.text: Completion angiogram for dissection, embolization, and brisk parenchymal flow; final gradient if measured.
steps.title: Completion

postop: Access-site care and observation; renal function at 24–48 h
Dual antiplatelet therapy ~4 weeks then aspirin (institutional protocols vary)
Blood pressure surveillance — expect medication titration rather than cure in atherosclerotic disease
Duplex follow-up (baseline, then surveillance for in-stent restenosis)

complications.name: Atheroembolization to the kidney
complications.management: Prevention: no-touch technique, minimal ostial manipulation. Supportive; often irreversible function loss.

complications.name: Renal artery dissection/perforation
complications.management: Prolonged balloon inflation, stent/stent-graft; covered stent or coil for perforation.

complications.name: In-stent restenosis
complications.management: Repeat angioplasty ± drug-coated balloon.

complications.name: Contrast-induced AKI
complications.management: Hydration, CO2, staged procedures.

complications.name: Access complications
complications.management: Standard access-site management.

pearls: CORAL is the board answer: stenting added nothing to good medical therapy for routine atherosclerotic RAS [1] — know the exceptions cold (flash pulmonary edema, solitary kidney with progressive failure, truly refractory hypertension).
FMD gets a balloon, not a stent — the 'string of beads' responds to angioplasty and stents complicate a young patient's artery permanently.
Ostial lesions are aorto-ostial plaque: only a balloon-expandable stent placed with ~1 mm aortic protrusion scaffolds them accurately.
Keep the wire tip in sight in a segmental branch, never buried in the cortex — a 0.014" wire perforation can still explode into a page-turning perinephric hematoma.

citations.text: Cooper CJ, Murphy TP, Cutlip DE, et al. Stenting and medical therapy for atherosclerotic renal-artery stenosis (CORAL). N Engl J Med. 2014;370(1):13-22.
ASTRAL Investigators; Wheatley K, Ives N, Gray R, et al. Revascularization versus medical therapy for renal-artery stenosis. N Engl J Med. 2009;361(20):1953-1962.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Renal Mass Thermal Ablation
procedures.overview: Image-guided thermal destruction of small renal tumors — an alternative to partial nephrectomy for T1a (≤4 cm) renal cell carcinoma, especially in older patients, solitary kidneys, or those with comorbidities. Cryoablation (visible iceball) and microwave/RFA (heat) both achieve high local control with nephron preservation [1,2].

labs: HIGH bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [3]
Pre-ablation biopsy (often same session or prior) for histology
Creatinine/eGFR and split renal function considerations; cross-sectional imaging for approach and adjacent structures
Type & screen for central/large tumors
preop.meds: Local + moderate sedation or general anesthesia (probe placement precision, breath-hold). Analgesia; no routine antibiotics.
equipment: CT (± US/MRI) guidance; cryoablation probes (argon-helium) or microwave/RF antennae
Multiple probes for iceball sculpting to tumor + margin
Hydrodissection (D5W for RF/MW to avoid conducting; saline acceptable for cryo) and CO2 to displace bowel
Pyeloperfusion (warm saline via retrograde ureteral catheter) to protect the collecting system for central tumors
Thermocouples for margin monitoring
indications: Biopsy-proven or imaging-characteristic T1a RCC (≤4 cm), particularly in poor surgical candidates or solitary kidneys
Hereditary/multifocal RCC (nephron preservation across a lifetime of tumors)
Selected T1b in multidisciplinary discussion
Symptomatic/enlarging angiomyolipoma (often embolization-first, ablation selected)

absolute: Uncorrectable coagulopathy; active untreated infection
Tumor anatomy precluding safe ablation with no protective maneuvers available
relative: Central/hilar tumors abutting the collecting system or large vessels (ureteral injury/heat-sink) — pyeloperfusion/hydrodissection may help
Tumor adjacent to bowel without a displacement option
Very large tumors (incomplete ablation risk)

steps.text: CT plan for a posterior/posterolateral corridor. Identify at-risk neighbors (bowel, ureter, adrenal, nerves) and choose protection: hydrodissection, CO2, pyeloperfusion, or patient repositioning.
steps.title: Plan approach and protection

steps.text: Core biopsy for histology — many centers biopsy at the same session before ablating so pathology guides surveillance.
steps.title: Biopsy (if not already done)

steps.text: Position cryoprobes/antennae under CT to cover the tumor plus a ~5–10 mm margin. For cryo, space multiple probes ~1.5–2 cm apart to create a confluent iceball extending beyond the tumor edge.
steps.title: Place probes

steps.text: Hydrodissect bowel/critical structures away from the ablation zone; start pyeloperfusion for central tumors to keep the urothelium out of the lethal zone.
steps.title: Deploy protection

steps.text: Cryo: two freeze-thaw cycles (e.g., 10 min freeze / 8 min passive + active thaw / 10 min freeze), watching the iceball engulf the tumor + margin on CT (the iceball edge is ~0°C; lethal zone is a few mm inside). Heat-based: ablate to target time/temperature; the margin is monitored by thermocouples/CT.
steps.title: Ablate with monitoring

steps.text: Post-ablation contrast (if renal function allows) or immediate imaging checks that the ablation zone fully covers the tumor with margin and no active enhancement.
steps.title: Assess completeness

steps.text: Thaw fully before cryoprobe removal (removing a frozen probe fractures the kidney — a classic bleeding cause); cauterize/track-ablate heat probes on withdrawal.
steps.title: Withdraw with tract management

postop: Observation ~2–4 h (or overnight for larger/central tumors); monitor for bleeding and hematuria
Expect small perinephric hematoma and transient hematuria; significant/expanding → CT and possible embolization
Follow-up contrast CT/MRI at ~1, 3, 6, 12 months then annually: nodular/crescentic enhancement at the margin signals residual/recurrent tumor
Residual enhancement → repeat ablation (a strength of the technique — retreatable)
Renal function monitoring; nephron-preservation counseling

complications.name: Hemorrhage / perinephric hematoma
complications.management: Fully thaw before cryoprobe removal; correct coags. Significant bleeding → embolization; most hematomas managed conservatively.

complications.name: Collecting system / ureteral injury
complications.management: Pyeloperfusion protection; urinoma/stricture → nephrostomy/stent; delayed stricture surveillance.

complications.name: Bowel injury
complications.management: Hydrodissection/CO2 displacement is prevention; thermal bowel injury → surgery.

complications.name: Cryoshock (rare, large-volume cryo)
complications.management: Coagulopathy/multiorgan phenomenon with extensive freezing — limit ice volume; supportive ICU care.

complications.name: Residual/recurrent tumor
complications.management: Detected on surveillance enhancement; repeat ablation is usually successful.

complications.name: Nerve injury / flank paresthesia
complications.management: Hydrodissect near the psoas/genitofemoral; usually transient.

pearls: Cryoablation's gift is that you can see the kill zone — but the visible iceball edge is 0°C; the lethal isotherm is several millimeters inside, so push ice well past the tumor.
Never yank a frozen cryoprobe: complete the thaw or you crack the kidney and create the bleed you spent the case avoiding.
Protect the neighbors deliberately — hydrodissection (D5W for RF/MW), CO2 for bowel, pyeloperfusion for the ureter — the ablation is only as safe as your margins to critical structures.
Ablation is retreatable: a small residual nodule at follow-up is an expected, fixable event, not a failure of the strategy [2].

citations.text: Campbell SC, Clark PE, Chang SS, et al. Renal mass and localized renal cancer: AUA guideline part I. J Urol. 2021;206(2):199-208.
Atwell TD, Schmit GD, Boorjian SA, et al. Percutaneous ablation of renal masses ≤3 cm: comparison of cryoablation and radiofrequency ablation. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 2013;200(2):461-466.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Spine Injections (Facet, Epidural & Nerve Root Blocks)
procedures.overview: Image-guided injection of anesthetic ± corticosteroid for spinal pain: facet (zygapophyseal) joint and medial branch blocks for facetogenic pain, and epidural/transforaminal steroid injections for radicular pain. Fluoroscopy (or CT) confirms needle position and, critically, detects intravascular uptake via contrast before steroid is injected — the safeguard against the catastrophic cord/brain infarct from particulate embolization [1].

labs: Neuraxial injections warrant anticoagulation management stricter than routine low-risk procedures [2]
Diabetes counseling: transient glucose rise after steroid
Review MRI to match the injected level to the symptomatic pathology
preop.meds: Local anesthesia; injectate = local anesthetic ± corticosteroid. Non-particulate steroid (dexamethasone) preferred for cervical/upper transforaminal injections to avoid embolic infarct.
equipment: Fluoroscopy (± CT for complex/postsurgical anatomy)
22–25G spinal needles (curved-tip for foraminal targeting)
Non-ionic contrast for position + intravascular-uptake check (real-time/DSA in the cervical spine)
Anesthetic + steroid (non-particulate where embolization risk is highest)
indications: Radicular pain from disc herniation or foraminal stenosis (transforaminal/interlaminar epidural steroid)
Facet-mediated axial back/neck pain (intra-articular facet injection; medial branch block as a diagnostic step before radiofrequency ablation)
Diagnostic selective nerve root block to identify the symptomatic level before surgery
Postsurgical or degenerative pain not responding to conservative care

absolute: Overlying/systemic infection; true anesthetic/steroid/contrast allergy for the planned agents
relative: Anticoagulation/coagulopathy — manage per neuraxial (interlaminar/transforaminal) bleeding-risk windows [2]
Poorly controlled diabetes (steroid hyperglycemia) / CHF
Pregnancy (fluoroscopy) — defer or minimize

steps.text: Fluoro to the target level; oblique the beam to open the facet joint or the 'Scotty dog' for medial branch, or the safe triangle/subpedicular zone for transforaminal work.
steps.title: Localize and mark

steps.text: Facet: needle into the joint or onto the medial branch at the junction of transverse process and superior articular process. Transforaminal: to the subpedicular safe zone, staying superior/posterior in the foramen away from the radicular artery.
steps.title: Advance to target

steps.text: Inject a small contrast dose under live fluoro/DSA: confirm target opacification and, above all, absence of intravascular (arterial) uptake before any steroid. Vascular flow → reposition and retest [1].
steps.title: Contrast test — the safety step

steps.text: Deliver anesthetic ± steroid slowly; the immediate anesthetic response is diagnostic information for level-targeted blocks.
steps.title: Inject

steps.text: Save the contrast confirmation image; record the level, side, agent, and the patient's early pain response.
steps.title: Document

postop: Short observation for lower-extremity weakness/vasovagal; motor block from anesthetic is transient
Steroid effect develops over days — set expectations; a pain diary quantifies benefit for diagnostic blocks
Diabetics: expect a few days of higher glucose
Return precautions: new weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever, severe headache (dural puncture)

complications.name: Spinal cord/cerebral infarct from particulate embolization
complications.management: Prevention is everything: contrast/DSA to exclude arterial uptake, non-particulate steroid in high-risk regions [1].

complications.name: Vasovagal reaction
complications.management: Supine, fluids, observation.

complications.name: Dural puncture / post-dural headache
complications.management: Conservative care; blood patch if refractory.

complications.name: Transient steroid effects (hyperglycemia, flushing, insomnia)
complications.management: Reassurance; diabetic glucose monitoring.

complications.name: Epidural abscess/infection
complications.management: Sterile technique; fever + progressive pain/deficit → urgent MRI.

complications.name: Epidural hematoma
complications.management: Neuraxial anticoagulation precautions; emergent decompression for deficit.

pearls: The live contrast injection is not a formality — arterial uptake before a particulate steroid is the mechanism of cord infarct, and DSA in the cervical spine exists for exactly this.
Use non-particulate steroid (dexamethasone) for cervical and high thoracic transforaminal injections — the embolus you don't create needs no rescue.
Medial branch blocks are the diagnostic gatekeeper to facet radiofrequency ablation: concordant relief predicts RFA success.
Match the needle to the MRI: injecting the wrong level produces a falsely negative diagnostic block and a frustrated patient.

citations.text: Rathmell JP, Benzon HT, Dreyfuss P, et al. Safeguards to prevent neurologic complications after epidural steroid injections: consensus opinions from a multidisciplinary working group and national organizations. Anesthesiology. 2015;122(5):974-984.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Splenic Artery Embolization (Trauma)
procedures.overview: Adjunct to nonoperative management of blunt splenic injury in hemodynamically stable (or stabilizable) patients. Proximal embolization drops splenic perfusion pressure while collaterals (short gastrics, gastroepiploic, pancreatic branches) preserve viable, immunologically functional spleen; distal embolization targets focal injuries. Raises nonoperative salvage above 90% in higher-grade injuries [1].

labs: CT with arterial + portal venous phases: grade injury, find blush/pseudoaneurysm, exclude other operative injuries
CBC trend; type & cross 2 units; coags — correct per massive transfusion principles if activated [3]
Continuous hemodynamic monitoring; trauma surgery co-management is mandatory
eGFR when time allows; do not delay for labs in the borderline patient
preop.meds: Resuscitate in parallel. Moderate sedation or anesthesia support depending on stability.
equipment: 5F sheath, Cobra/SOS/Simmons catheter for celiac and splenic selection
Microcatheter for distal work or tortuous splenic anatomy
Proximal occlusion: Amplatzer vascular plug (oversize 30–50%) or 0.035 coils
Distal: microcoils ± gelfoam
Power injector for celiac/splenic DSA
indications: AAST grade III–V blunt splenic injury with contrast blush/extravasation, pseudoaneurysm, or AVF on CT
Moderate-to-severe injury with declining hemoglobin despite stability
Delayed splenic pseudoaneurysm on surveillance imaging
Splenic artery aneurysm >2–3 cm, pregnancy-anticipated, or symptomatic (elective indication)

absolute: Hemodynamic instability unresponsive to resuscitation — operative management (splenectomy), not angio
relative: Other indications for immediate laparotomy (hollow viscus injury, peritonitis)
Severe contrast allergy — premedicate if time allows [2]

steps.text: 5F catheter in the celiac, then selective splenic artery runs (the vessel is tortuous — a microcatheter often helps). Correlate with CT: extravasation, pseudoaneurysm, AVF, or abrupt branch cutoffs.
steps.title: Celiac and splenic angiography

steps.text: Multifocal injury, diffuse blush, or unstable-ish physiology → proximal embolization (fast, effective). Focal single-branch injury in a stable patient → superselective distal embolization (preserves more parenchyma but higher infarct rate in trials — territory-focused).
steps.title: Choose proximal vs distal strategy

steps.text: Land the plug/coils in the main splenic artery distal to the dorsal pancreatic artery and proximal to the pancreatica magna when feasible — this preserves pancreatic supply while letting short gastric and gastroepiploic collaterals reperfuse the spleen at lower pressure.
steps.title: Proximal embolization

steps.text: Amplatzer plug sized 30–50% over vessel diameter; confirm position with gentle runs before release. Post-deployment run shows stasis/near-stasis in the main splenic with early collateral reconstitution.
steps.title: Deploy and confirm

steps.text: For focal pseudoaneurysm/extravasation: microcatheter into the injured branch, microcoils across the injury (front and back door if the branch reconstitutes).
steps.title: Distal option

steps.text: Final celiac run documents result; check the left gastric and gastroepiploic collaterals opacify. Access closure and back to trauma ICU.
steps.title: Completion survey

postop: Trauma ICU monitoring with serial hemoglobin and abdominal exams
Expect low-grade fever, left upper quadrant pain, small left pleural effusion — the benign post-embolization course
Escalating pain, sepsis physiology, or persistent fevers beyond ~1 week → CT for infarct/abscess
Post-splenic-embolization vaccination is NOT routinely required after proximal SAE (immune function largely preserved), but institutional practice varies — document the discussion [1]
Graded return to activity per trauma service; surveillance imaging for higher-grade injuries

complications.name: Rebleeding / failed nonoperative management
complications.management: Repeat embolization if focal target; hemodynamic failure → splenectomy. Highest risk in grade V injuries.

complications.name: Splenic infarction (major)
complications.rate: up to 20% distal; less with proximal
complications.management: Most partial infarcts are asymptomatic and involute. Large symptomatic infarcts: analgesia, watch for abscess.

complications.name: Splenic abscess
complications.management: Fever + fluid/gas in infarcted spleen → percutaneous drainage + antibiotics; splenectomy for failure.

complications.name: Pancreatitis
complications.management: From dorsal pancreatic embolization/nontarget — supportive care.

complications.name: Coil/plug migration
complications.management: Prevent with proper oversizing; retrieve with snare if hemodynamically significant.

complications.name: Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) equivalent
complications.management: Preserved perfused spleen after proximal SAE retains immune function; counsel on fever precautions regardless.

pearls: The splenic artery is the collateral-richest visceral vessel — proximal occlusion is protective, not ischemic. That asymmetry is the whole trick of proximal SAE.
Know the dorsal pancreatic origin before you deploy: plugs that jail it explain most post-SAE pancreatitis.
A trauma patient who transiently responds to resuscitation is an angio candidate; one who never responds belongs in the OR.
Delayed pseudoaneurysms cause 'failed observation' — many centers re-image grade III+ injuries at 48–72 h.

citations.text: Stassen NA, Bhullar I, Cheng JD, et al. Selective nonoperative management of blunt splenic injury: an Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma practice management guideline. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2012;73(5 Suppl 4):S294-S300.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Suprapubic Bladder Catheterization
procedures.overview: Image-guided placement of a drainage catheter into the bladder through the anterior abdominal wall — the route of choice when the urethra is off-limits (trauma, stricture, obstruction) or for long-term drainage where a urethral Foley causes erosion. The bladder must be distended to displace bowel; US + fluoro make this a safe Seldinger procedure rather than the blind trocar stab of surgical lore [1].

labs: High bleeding-risk classification: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [2]
Urinalysis/culture; antibiotics for colonized urine
Review any available CT for bowel position anterior to the bladder
preop.meds: Local anesthesia ± light sedation; peri-procedural antibiotic prophylaxis.
equipment: US for the puncture window; fluoro for wire/catheter confirmation
Fill the bladder: existing small catheter, clamped diuresis, or 22G needle instillation if needed
Micropuncture or 18G needle, stiff wire, fascial dilators or peel-away sheath
Locking pigtail catheter (12–16F) initially; plan for Foley-type SPT at first exchange
indications: Urethral trauma with inability to pass a Foley (pelvic fracture urethral injury)
Urethral stricture/false passage/failed catheterization after urologic attempts
Long-term drainage: neurogenic bladder, chronic retention — SPT beats chronic urethral Foley for comfort and erosion [1]
Bladder outlet obstruction pending definitive management
Urinary diversion for urethral/perineal wounds or fistulae

absolute: Non-distensible or unidentifiable bladder (defunctionalized, tumor-contracted); bowel interposed on imaging that cannot be displaced; known bladder tumor along the tract (seeding)
relative: Uncorrected coagulopathy [2]
Prior lower abdominal/pelvic surgery with adhesed bowel loops (US/CT mapping essential)
Active skin infection at the site

steps.text: A full bladder rises above the pubis and pushes bowel away — fill via any available route until US shows a generous anechoic target (~300+ mL).
steps.title: Distend the bladder

steps.text: US midline or just off-midline, 2–4 fingerbreadths above the pubic symphysis; color Doppler for inferior epigastric vessels; confirm no bowel gas between wall and bladder.
steps.title: Map the window

steps.text: US-guided needle pass angled slightly caudal toward the bladder base; urine aspiration + contrast puff on fluoro confirms intravesical position.
steps.title: Puncture

steps.text: Coil a stiff wire in the bladder, dilate the tract sequentially (peel-away for larger tubes), stay midline where the wall is avascular.
steps.title: Seldinger dilation

steps.text: Lock the pigtail in the bladder; confirm position with contrast; secure at skin and connect to drainage.
steps.title: Catheter placement

steps.text: Document tract size and the plan: exchange to a Foley-type suprapubic tube after 2–4 weeks of tract maturation, then routine exchanges q4–8 weeks.
steps.title: Plan the upgrade path

postop: Monitor urine output and color — transient hematuria is expected; clot retention is not
Post-obstructive diuresis surveillance if chronic retention was decompressed (electrolytes, matching fluids)
First exchange over a wire at 2–4 weeks (immature tracts lose their way in seconds — always wire-guided early)
Teach tube care; bladder spasms are common early (anticholinergics help)

complications.name: Bowel injury
complications.rate: rare with imaging (the historic blind-trocar complication)
complications.management: Prevention: distended bladder + US interrogation of the path; surgical repair if transgressed.

complications.name: Hematuria/clot retention
complications.management: Irrigation, larger tube; cystoscopy for refractory bleeding.

complications.name: UTI / catheter-associated infection
complications.management: Colonization ≠ infection; treat symptomatic UTI, routine exchanges.

complications.name: Tract loss during early exchange
complications.management: Wire-guided exchanges until mature; if lost early, refill bladder and re-puncture.

complications.name: Extraperitoneal urine leak
complications.management: Larger catheter, decompression; usually seals.

complications.name: Bladder spasms
complications.management: Anticholinergics; reposition tip off the trigone.

pearls: The full bladder is the entire safety mechanism — if you can't fill it, you shouldn't blindly puncture it.
Aim low on the bladder (caudal angle): high punctures on an emptying bladder end up peritoneal.
In pelvic trauma, the SPT is also a urethral-injury treatment plan — coordinate with urology on tube size/position since it may live there for months.
First tube change is the highest-risk moment of the tube's life: over a wire, under fluoro, every time until the tract is mature.

citations.text: Harrison SC, Lawrence WT, Morley R, Pearce I, Taylor J. British Association of Urological Surgeons' suprapubic catheter practice guidelines. BJU Int. 2011;107(1):77-85.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Transarterial Chemoembolization (TACE)
procedures.overview: Catheter delivery of chemotherapy plus embolic to hepatic tumors — exploiting the near-total arterial supply of HCC against the dual (mostly portal) supply of normal liver. Standard of care for intermediate-stage (BCLC B) HCC and a mainstay bridge/downstaging tool for transplant [1,2].

labs: LFTs (bilirubin critical), albumin, INR, platelets; Child-Pugh & ALBI grade
AFP baseline; cross-sectional triphasic CT/MRI for LI-RADS, arterial supply mapping, PV patency
Platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L preferred though classed low bleeding-risk; INR ≤1.5–2.0 [3]
Creatinine/eGFR; ECOG performance status
preop.meds: Antiemetics, analgesia (post-embolization syndrome is expected), IV hydration; peri-procedural antibiotics for patients with biliary instrumentation/incompetent sphincter of Oddi (bilioenteric anastomosis) [4].
equipment: 5F sheath, visceral catheter (SOS/Cobra) for celiac/SMA; microcatheter for superselective lobar/segmental delivery
Cone-beam CT — confirms tumor-feeding vessels and complete tumor coverage, reduces nontarget delivery
cTACE: doxorubicin/cisplatin + lipiodol emulsion ± gelfoam; or DEB-TACE: drug-eluting microspheres (100–300 μm loaded with doxorubicin)
Nitroglycerin for spasm; power injector
indications: BCLC stage B (intermediate) HCC: multinodular, preserved liver function, no vascular invasion/spread
Bridging to transplant or downstaging within/toward Milan criteria
Unresectable HCC not amenable to ablation; palliation of symptomatic hypervascular tumors
Selected metastatic disease (e.g., neuroendocrine) — individualized

absolute: Decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C), extensive tumor burden with poor reserve
Main portal vein thrombosis with hepatofugal flow (whole-liver ischemia risk) — relative to selectivity
Untreatable severe contrast allergy; severe hepatic/renal failure
relative: Bilirubin >3 mg/dL, large tumor burden (>50% liver), refractory ascites
Biliary obstruction/incompetent sphincter (biloma/abscess risk)
Portal vein thrombosis (superselective, low-volume TACE may still be feasible)

steps.text: Celiac and SMA angiograms define hepatic arterial anatomy and variants (replaced/accessory right or left hepatic arteries are common) and confirm portal vein patency via the venous phase — you must know the portal supply before you embolize the arterial.
steps.title: Map the arterial supply

steps.text: Advance the microcatheter to the segmental/subsegmental artery feeding the tumor. Selectivity is everything: it maximizes tumor dose and spares normal parenchyma.
steps.title: Superselect the tumor feeders

steps.text: CBCT from the intended position shows tumor enhancement (complete coverage?) and excludes nontarget supply to stomach, duodenum, gallbladder, or diaphragm — reposition or protect as needed.
steps.title: Cone-beam CT confirmation

steps.text: cTACE: inject chemo-lipiodol emulsion slowly to tumor saturation, then gelfoam to stasis. DEB-TACE: infuse drug-eluting beads to near-stasis endpoint (slower, more controlled washout). Continuous fluoro — stop at the intended endpoint, avoid reflux.
steps.title: Deliver chemoembolic

steps.text: cTACE: dense lipiodol retention in tumor with sluggish feeding-artery flow. DEB: substasis/stasis in feeders. Do not over-embolize the main hepatic artery — you may need it again.
steps.title: Assess endpoint

steps.text: Final hepatic angiogram; document lipiodol distribution/bead delivery. Closure; admit for post-embolization syndrome management.
steps.title: Completion and closure

postop: Overnight/23-h admission: IV fluids, antiemetics, scheduled analgesia for post-embolization syndrome (pain, fever, nausea) — peaks 24–72 h
Monitor LFTs; transient transaminase rise expected; watch for hepatic decompensation in marginal reserve
Follow-up contrast MRI/CT at ~4–8 weeks: mRECIST response (loss of arterial enhancement = necrosis), plan repeat TACE for residual/recurrent viable tumor
AFP trend; multidisciplinary tumor board re-review (transplant listing, systemic therapy, ablation combos)
Counsel: TACE is often a repeated therapy, not a single event

complications.name: Post-embolization syndrome
complications.management: Fluids, antiemetics, analgesia; self-limited days. Distinguish from abscess by trajectory (persistent/rising fevers beyond a week).

complications.name: Liver failure / decompensation
complications.management: Patient selection (Child-Pugh, ALBI, tumor burden) is prevention; supportive care, hepatology; superselectivity limits parenchymal injury.

complications.name: Liver/biliary abscess or biloma
complications.management: Antibiotics + percutaneous drainage; antibiotic prophylaxis for at-risk biliary anatomy [4].

complications.name: Nontarget embolization (GI, gallbladder, skin)
complications.management: CBCT prevention; cholecystitis usually managed conservatively; GI ulceration supportive/endoscopic.

complications.name: Hepatic artery injury/dissection
complications.management: Gentle technique, spasm control; dissection usually managed conservatively but may cost future access.

complications.name: Tumor rupture / bleeding
complications.management: Large subcapsular tumors: resuscitate, may need bland embolization for hemostasis.

pearls: HCC steals its blood from the hepatic artery; normal liver leans on the portal vein — that dual-supply asymmetry is why TACE works and why portal vein patency is a safety checkpoint.
CBCT is the modern TACE's conscience: it finds the feeder you missed and the stomach you were about to embolize.
Superselectivity beats volume — a segmental complete treatment outperforms a lobar sloppy one, and preserves the liver for the next round.
Rising bilirubin after TACE in a Child-B liver is a warning, not a lab artifact — respect hepatic reserve at selection.

citations.text: Llovet JM, Real MI, Montaña X, et al. Arterial embolisation or chemoembolisation versus symptomatic treatment in patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma: a randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2002;359(9319):1734-1739.
Reig M, Forner A, Rimola J, et al. BCLC strategy for prognosis prediction and treatment recommendation: the 2022 update. J Hepatol. 2022;76(3):681-693.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Chehab MA, Thakor AS, Tulin-Silver S, et al. Adult and pediatric antibiotic prophylaxis during vascular and interventional radiology procedures: SIR practice parameter. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2018;29(11):1483-1501.

procedures.title: Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS)
procedures.overview: A percutaneous portosystemic shunt created through the liver parenchyma between a hepatic vein and a portal vein branch, stented open (covered stent-graft) to decompress portal hypertension. The two eternal tensions: enough decompression to stop bleeding/ascites, but not so much that encephalopathy or liver failure ensues. Target portosystemic gradient <12 mmHg for varices [1,2].

labs: This is a HIGH bleeding-risk procedure: correct platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L and INR toward ≤1.5–1.8; fibrinogen >150 mg/dL; type & cross [4]
MELD 3.0 and Child-Pugh for risk stratification (use calculators)
Cross-sectional imaging: portal vein patency, hepatic vein anatomy, tumor, ascites; echo for RA pressure and pulmonary pressures
Ammonia/baseline mental status documented
preop.meds: Peri-procedural antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) commonly given; correct coagulopathy at the table; anesthesia support (often general for airway protection in bleeders).
equipment: TIPS set (Rösch-Uchida or Colapinto/Ross needle system) with 10F sheath
Curved metallic guide + needle for the parenchymal puncture
Balloon (8–10 mm) for tract dilation
Dedicated covered stent-graft (ePTFE-lined, e.g., Viatorr/VCX) — bare stents are obsolete for the shunt
Pressure transducers (RA, hepatic vein, portal vein); CO2 for portal venography; wires (Amplatz), embolics for varices
indications: Secondary prevention / acute control of variceal hemorrhage refractory to endoscopic + medical therapy
