Early (pre-emptive) TIPS in high-risk cirrhotic variceal bleeders (Child-Pugh B with active bleeding or Child-Pugh C ≤13) [3]
Refractory ascites or hepatic hydrothorax
Budd-Chiari syndrome, veno-occlusive disease (selected)
Portal decompression before abdominal surgery in selected patients

absolute: Severe/progressive hepatic failure (very high MELD) where shunting precipitates fatal decompensation
Severe uncontrolled hepatic encephalopathy
Severe pulmonary hypertension (mean PAP >45 mmHg)
Congestive heart failure / severe tricuspid regurgitation (shunt increases preload)
Systemic infection / sepsis, unrelieved biliary obstruction
relative: Polycystic liver disease, extensive hepatic tumors along the tract
Portal vein thrombosis (cavernous transformation makes access hard — not absolute)
MELD >18–20 rising: escalating post-TIPS mortality — weigh urgency [2]

steps.text: US-guided right IJ access, 10F sheath to the right atrium. Catheterize the right hepatic vein; measure free and wedged hepatic venous pressures (baseline gradient). CO2 wedged portography can map the portal vein target.
steps.title: Right IJ access, catheterize the hepatic vein

steps.text: Rotate the curved TIPS needle anteriorly (from the right hepatic vein) and advance through parenchyma toward the right portal vein ~1–2 cm from the bifurcation (intrahepatic — extrahepatic puncture risks fatal hemorrhage). Aspirate blood; inject to confirm portal position.
steps.title: Portal puncture

steps.text: Pass a wire into the main portal/SMV, exchange in a catheter, and record the portosystemic pressure gradient. Portal venogram identifies varices.
steps.title: Portal venography and pressures

steps.text: Balloon-dilate the parenchymal tract (8 mm typical to start). Note the waists (hepatic vein end, parenchyma, portal end) — they guide stent landing.
steps.title: Dilate the tract

steps.text: Place the ePTFE-covered stent spanning from the portal vein entry to the hepatocaval junction — the bare portion (if Viatorr) anchors in the portal vein, the covered portion lines the parenchymal tract to the hepatic vein/IVC. Precise landing prevents both stenosis and over-shunting.
steps.title: Deploy the covered stent-graft

steps.text: Post-deployment gradient: target <12 mmHg (or ≥50% reduction) for variceal indications; for ascites, similar targets with attention to not over-shunting. Under-dilate initially if encephalopathy risk is high (can up-size later).
steps.title: Re-measure and titrate

steps.text: Persistent large varices despite adequate gradient → coil/glue embolization of the coronary/gastric varices via the shunt. Final portogram documents decompression.
steps.title: Embolize varices (if indicated)

postop: ICU/step-down monitoring; watch for bleeding (hepatic capsule/tract), hemodynamic shifts from increased preload
Doppler US baseline within 24–72 h, then surveillance at 1, 3, 6, 12 months (shunt velocity 90–190 cm/s typical; deviation suggests stenosis)
Monitor and treat new/worsening hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose ± rifaximin; refractory HE may require shunt reduction
Watch for hepatic decompensation (bilirubin/INR rise) — the dreaded early sign of over-shunting in a marginal liver
Continue treatment of underlying liver disease; hepatology co-management

complications.name: New/worsening hepatic encephalopathy
complications.management: Lactulose + rifaximin; protein moderation; refractory/disabling → shunt reduction or occlusion. Anticipate in older patients, high pre-TIPS ammonia, larger shunts.

complications.name: Intraperitoneal hemorrhage (capsular/extrahepatic puncture)
complications.management: The lethal early complication: resuscitate, angiography/embolization of hepatic artery or covered-stent the tract; emergent surgery rarely. Prevention: keep the portal puncture intrahepatic.

complications.name: Acute hepatic decompensation / liver failure
complications.management: Rising bilirubin/INR, encephalopathy: supportive, consider shunt reduction; transplant evaluation. Patient selection (MELD) is the real prevention [2].

complications.name: Shunt stenosis/thrombosis
complications.rate: much reduced with covered stents (<20% at 1 yr)
complications.management: Surveillance Doppler; angioplasty/re-line the shunt for recurrent gradient or symptoms.

complications.name: Cardiac decompensation
complications.management: Increased venous return can precipitate heart failure/flash pulmonary edema — echo screening pre-TIPS; diurese, rarely reduce shunt.

complications.name: Hemolysis, stent migration, biliary-vascular fistula
complications.management: Hemolysis usually self-limited; migration/fistula → endovascular correction.

pearls: MELD earns its keep here: elective TIPS mortality climbs steeply above ~18 — the score is the conversation, not just a number [2].
Keep the portal puncture intrahepatic. Extracapsular portal vein puncture is the classic cause of fatal hemorrhage — anatomy respect over speed.
Under-shunt on purpose in the encephalopathy-prone: you can always dilate a covered stent larger, but you cannot easily un-ring the encephalopathy bell.
Pre-emptive early TIPS in the right high-risk bleeder (Child B active bleeding / Child C ≤13) improves survival — know the criteria for consults [3].

citations.text: Boyer TD, Haskal ZJ. The role of transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) in the management of portal hypertension: update 2009. Hepatology. 2010;51(1):306.
Kim WR, Mannalithara A, Heimbach JK, et al. MELD 3.0: the model for end-stage liver disease updated for the modern era. Gastroenterology. 2021;161(6):1887-1895.
García-Pagán JC, Caca K, Bureau C, et al. Early use of TIPS in patients with cirrhosis and variceal bleeding. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(25):2370-2379.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Transjugular Liver & Renal Biopsy
procedures.overview: Core biopsy of the liver (or kidney) from inside its own veins — the coagulopathic patient's biopsy route. A sheath is steered from the right IJ into the right hepatic vein and a spring-loaded needle fires into parenchyma away from the capsule, so bleeding drains back into the venous system. Bundled hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) measurement often adds more clinical value than the cores themselves [1,2].

labs: The point of the procedure: proceed despite INR/platelet derangement that blocks percutaneous biopsy; correct only what's practical [3]
Review cross-sectional imaging: hepatic vein patency, liver size, and that the pathology question is diffuse (not focal)
Renal function if iodinated contrast planned (CO2 alternative)
preop.meds: Local + moderate sedation with breath-hold coaching.
equipment: Right IJ access (US-guided), 9–10F sheath
Multipurpose catheter + stiff wire to the right hepatic vein; balloon-tipped catheter if wedged pressures are measured by occlusion
Stiffened metal cannula directing an 18–19G spring-loaded core needle (Quick-Core type)
Pressure transducer for free and wedged hepatic venous pressures; CO2 or contrast for venography
indications: Liver biopsy with severe coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia uncorrectable to percutaneous thresholds [1]
Moderate–large ascites precluding percutaneous biopsy
Need for HVPG measurement (portal hypertension staging, response to beta-blockade) with same-session tissue [2]
Suspected acute liver failure or post-transplant dysfunction needing urgent tissue with abnormal coags
Transjugular renal biopsy: same coagulopathy/anatomy logic for medical renal disease (higher capsular perforation rate — right renal vein via IVC)

absolute: Occluded IJ/SVC/hepatic venous pathways (consider transfemoral or plugged percutaneous alternatives)
relative: Uncooperative patient (needle fires during breath-hold), severe contrast allergy (CO2 venography works), targeted focal lesion biopsy (transjugular samples random parenchyma — use image-guided percutaneous for masses)

steps.text: US-guided right IJ puncture; steer a multipurpose catheter into the right hepatic vein (the trajectory that points the needle into thick right-lobe parenchyma).
steps.title: Access and hepatic vein selection

steps.text: Free hepatic venous pressure, then wedged (balloon occlusion) pressure ×3; HVPG = wedged − free. ≥10 mmHg is clinically significant portal hypertension, ≥12 mmHg variceal bleeding threshold [2].
steps.title: Measure HVPG

steps.text: Gentle contrast/CO2 run confirms the vein, excludes wedge in a small branch, and shows the vein's axis for needle orientation.
steps.title: Confirm position with venography

steps.text: Exchange for the stiff cannula, tip 2–3 cm into the vein, rotated anteriorly (right hepatic vein) so the needle path dives into parenchyma away from the capsule.
steps.title: Advance the cannula and orient

steps.text: Advance the core needle, fire during suspended respiration; take 2–4 cores for adequacy (transjugular cores are thinner — count portal tracts with pathology if possible).
steps.title: Fire during breath-hold

steps.text: Post-biopsy venogram/hand run for capsular leak; remove system, brief pressure at the neck.
steps.title: Final check

postop: Bed rest 2–4 h with vitals; neck access means no position restrictions beyond comfort
Hemoglobin check if any concern; pain out of proportion or hypotension → CT (capsular perforation bleeds intraperitoneally)
Route HVPG numbers with the pathology requisition — the referrer needs both together
Same-day discharge feasible for stable outpatients; inpatients resume care immediately

complications.name: Capsular perforation with intraperitoneal bleed
complications.management: Resuscitate; angiography/embolization of the tract. Prevention: central firing position, anterior rotation, never fire near the capsule.

complications.name: Neck hematoma / carotid puncture
complications.management: Compression; US guidance is prevention.

complications.name: Arrhythmia during right-heart passage
complications.management: Reposition; resolves immediately.

complications.name: Hepatic vein/tract fistula (arterioportal or biliovenous)
complications.management: Most close spontaneously; embolize if symptomatic.

complications.name: Inadequate sample
complications.management: Additional passes; communicate portal-tract requirements with pathology up front [1].

pearls: This is two procedures billed as one: the cores answer 'what,' the HVPG answers 'how bad' — never skip the pressures in a portal hypertension workup [2].
Right hepatic vein + anterior cannula rotation = needle travels through the fattest parenchyma; a middle vein position aimed anteriorly points at the capsule.
In the shrunken cirrhotic liver, every millimeter matters — check needle throw length against the parenchymal thickness on pre-procedure imaging.
A 'wedged' pressure that mirrors RA pressure means you're not truly wedged — occlude with the balloon and confirm with a sluggish CO2 wedge run.

citations.text: Kalambokis G, Manousou P, Vibhakorn S, et al. Transjugular liver biopsy — indications, adequacy, quality of specimens, and complications — a systematic review. J Hepatol. 2007;47(2):284-294.
Bosch J, Abraldes JG, Berzigotti A, Garcia-Pagan JC. The clinical use of HVPG measurements in chronic liver disease. Nat Rev Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2009;6(10):573-582.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Tube Studies, Sinograms & Catheter Contrast Checks
procedures.overview: The humble fluoroscopic contrast injection through an existing tube or tract — nephrostogram, tube cholangiogram/cholecystogram, abscessogram, sinogram of a wound or fistula. Unglamorous and constantly requested, these studies answer the questions that drive management: Is the tube in position? Is the cavity collapsing? Does the leak persist? Can the tube come out? Low pressure and pre-imaging review are the whole technique [1].

labs: None routinely; this is among the lowest-risk procedures in the department [1]
Review the original placement images and operative notes first — know what cavity/system you expect before injecting
Check recent outputs/clinical course: the tube study interprets itself only in context
preop.meds: None usually; no sedation for the vast majority.
equipment: Fluoroscopy; water-soluble contrast (dilute 1:1 for large cavities)
Connecting tubing, syringes — gravity or gentle hand pressure only
Sterile prep of the hub; wire and exchange equipment on standby (a 'study' often becomes an exchange when the tube proves malpositioned)
indications: Assess drainage catheter position/function (output dropped, leaking around the tube, suspected dislodgment)
Abscess cavity surveillance before downsizing or removal (cavity collapsed? fistula to bowel?)
Tube cholangiogram/cholecystogram: duct patency, cystic duct patency before cholecystostomy removal, stone clearance
Nephrostogram: ureteral patency before PCN removal or capping trial
Sinogram of a surgical drain tract, wound, or suspected enterocutaneous fistula to map the track and connections

absolute: None meaningful — this is a diagnostic injection
relative: Sepsis: defer elective tube studies in the febrile patient and never inject an infected system under pressure (bacteremia on demand)
Significant contrast allergy (premedicate; dilute gadolinium alternatives rarely needed)

steps.text: A scout image documents baseline tube course and pigtail integrity — kinks, breaks, and migration are often visible before any contrast.
steps.title: Scout first

steps.text: Low-pressure hand injection under intermittent fluoro; fill the cavity/system just enough to answer the question. Pressure is the enemy — especially in biliary and renal systems where pus + pressure = rigors.
steps.title: Inject gently

steps.text: Rotate obliques/laterals to profile tracks and communications: fistulas hide behind cavities in a single AP view.
steps.title: Map in projections

steps.text: Cavity volume vs prior; catheter sideholes inside the collection; distal patency (contrast to bowel for biliary, to bladder for renal); leak/fistula present or resolved.
steps.title: Answer the specific question

steps.text: The trilogy of dispositions: leave/upsize (cavity persists), downsize/withdraw stepwise (tract maturing, cavity collapsing), or remove (cavity gone, distal patency restored, output minimal).
steps.title: Act on the answer

postop: Reconnect to drainage as before (never leave a formerly draining tube capped without a plan)
Rigors/fever after injection of a colonized system: cultures, antibiotics, observation — pressure injection is usually the culprit
Document the disposition explicitly: 'removed', 'downsized to X F', or 'recheck in Y weeks' — tube patients get lost without a named plan and owner
Removal criteria worth memorizing: output <10–20 mL/day, cavity collapsed, no fistula, patient afebrile

complications.name: Bacteremia/sepsis from pressurized injection
complications.management: Gravity-pressure injections in colonized systems; antibiotics if rigors follow.

complications.name: Tube dislodgment during study/manipulation
complications.management: Wire ready — a fresh tract can often be recovered immediately, an old one recannulated.

complications.name: Contrast reaction
complications.management: Standard reaction management.

complications.name: Missed fistula from underfilling
complications.management: Adequate volume + multiple obliques; delayed images for sluggish tracks.

pearls: The tube study is a management decision dressed as an imaging study — walk in knowing the three possible dispositions and leave having picked one.
Gravity is a pressure setting: in any colonized system, let contrast fall in rather than pushing it in.
Compare with the prior study side-by-side; 'cavity smaller' is only meaningful against last week's images.
Before removing a cholecystostomy, prove a patent cystic duct — remove it blind and the gallbladder simply refills and re-presents.

citations.text: Kandarpa K, Machan L, Durham JD, eds. Handbook of Interventional Radiologic Procedures. 5th ed. Philadelphia: Wolters Kluwer; 2016.

procedures.title: Tunneled Hemodialysis Catheter Placement
procedures.overview: A large-bore (14.5–16F) cuffed, tunneled catheter providing immediate high-flow access for hemodialysis or apheresis. A bridge — not a destination: KDOQI emphasizes minimizing catheter-dependence in favor of fistulas and grafts because of infection and central stenosis risk [1].

labs: Platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0–3.0 (SIR low bleeding-risk) [2]
Potassium — coordinate with nephrology if K+ critical; dialysis immediately after placement
Blood cultures cleared ×48 h if recent line infection
Review prior fistulograms/venograms for central patency
preop.meds: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended by SIR for tunneled catheter placement [3]; local anesthesia + moderate sedation.
equipment: US, micropuncture set, 0.035-inch stiff wire (e.g., Amplatz)
14.5–16F tunneled dialysis catheter (step-tip or split-tip), length chosen for right IJ ~19–23 cm to mid-right atrium
Tunneler, peel-away sheath with valve if available
Serial dilators
Heparin lock 1000 U/mL (or citrate per institution) to catheter fill volumes printed on hubs
indications: Urgent-start hemodialysis without usable AV access
Bridge during AV fistula/graft maturation or revision
Bridge to living-donor transplant or expected renal recovery (e.g., AKI on CRRT converting to iHD)
Plasmapheresis or apheresis courses

absolute: Untreated bacteremia — place a temporary non-tunneled line until cultures clear
relative: Central venous stenosis/occlusion on the planned side
Anticipated ipsilateral AV access creation — protect the arm and subclavian veins
Severe uncorrected coagulopathy

steps.text: Right IJ preferred; US-guided low-neck micropuncture access, wire to IVC under fluoro. Avoid the subclavian veins — central stenosis there can sacrifice a whole arm for future fistulas [1].
steps.title: Access

steps.text: Target the catheter tip in the mid-right atrium for reliable flows (catheters pull back when the patient sits up). Choose exit site on the chest ~8–10 cm from the venotomy with a gentle curve; the cuff should sit 1–2 cm inside the exit.
steps.title: Plan tip and tunnel

steps.text: Anesthetize the tract generously. Pull the catheter through the tunnel from chest exit to neck incision, keeping the tip trajectory smooth without kinks.
steps.title: Tunnel

steps.text: Serially dilate the venotomy, then advance the peel-away sheath over the stiff wire — control it constantly; this is the air-embolism step. Use Valsalva/humming or occlusive technique on sheath removal.
steps.title: Dilate and sheath

steps.text: Advance the catheter through the peel-away as it is split and withdrawn. Confirm no kink at the neck curve under fluoro.
steps.title: Deliver catheter

steps.text: Each lumen should aspirate briskly (a 20 mL syringe fills without hesitation — sluggish aspiration predicts poor flows on HD). Flush, instill heparin lock to exact fill volume, cap.
steps.title: Test and lock

steps.text: Close the neck incision with absorbable suture; suture the catheter at the exit site. Document tip position with a spot image.
steps.title: Secure and close

postop: May be used for dialysis immediately
Exit-site care and dressing changes per dialysis unit protocol; no submersion
Retention suture typically removed at 2–3 weeks once the cuff incorporates
Every visit: reinforce permanent-access planning (fistula referral) — catheters are the highest-infection access [1]

complications.name: Air embolism
complications.management: Prevent: Valsalva, occlusive sheath technique, Trendelenburg. If it occurs: left lateral decubitus + Trendelenburg, 100% O2, aspirate through catheter if tip in RA, supportive care.

complications.name: Catheter-related bloodstream infection
complications.management: Cultures; empiric vancomycin + gram-negative coverage dosed for HD. Tunnel infection or hemodynamic instability → remove. Salvage with guidewire exchange + antibiotic lock is selective.

complications.name: Central venous stenosis
complications.rate: up to 20–40% with long dwell (highest subclavian)
complications.management: Presents as arm/face swelling or failing ipsilateral fistula. Venoplasty when symptomatic; avoid future lines on that side.

complications.name: Catheter dysfunction (fibrin sheath, malposition)
complications.management: tPA 2 mg/lumen dwell; persistent → fluoroscopic exchange with fibrin sheath disruption (balloon) over wire.

complications.name: Arrhythmia during placement
complications.management: Usually wire-induced ectopy — withdraw the wire from the RV. Persistent arrhythmia is rare.

complications.name: Hemorrhage / venous laceration
complications.management: Large-bore dilation injury of the SVC/innominate presents with hypotension ± hemothorax: resuscitate, keep wire access, balloon tamponade, covered stent or surgery.

pearls: Split-tip and symmetric-tip designs reduce recirculation; whatever the design, mid-RA tip position is the most important determinant of flows ≥300 mL/min.
Left-sided catheters need extra length (23–27 cm) and are more prone to kinking at the innominate–SVC junction — follow the wire's natural curve.
A catheter that aspirates poorly on the table will not dialyze — fix it before leaving the room (reposition, exchange).
In catheter-dependent patients with exhausted neck veins, options include transhepatic, translumbar, or recanalization approaches — plan with the access surgeon.

citations.text: Lok CE, Huber TS, Lee T, et al. KDOQI clinical practice guideline for vascular access: 2019 update. Am J Kidney Dis. 2020;75(4 Suppl 2):S1-S164.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk — Part II: recommendations. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Chehab MA, Thakor AS, Tulin-Silver S, et al. Adult and pediatric antibiotic prophylaxis during vascular and interventional radiology procedures: SIR practice parameter. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2018;29(11):1483-1501.

procedures.title: Antegrade Ureteral Stent Placement
procedures.overview: Placement of a double-J ureteral stent from above — through a percutaneous nephrostomy tract — when retrograde cystoscopic stenting has failed or the tract already exists. The internal stent frees the patient from an external bag; the trade is cystoscopic-or-antegrade exchanges every 3–6 months and the decision between double-J, nephroureteral (NUS), and staying with a nephrostomy [1].

labs: New tract = high bleeding-risk: platelets ≥50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤1.5–1.8 [2]
Urine culture ideally sterile/treated; peri-procedural antibiotics standard
Creatinine; review CT for level and cause of obstruction (determines stent length/likelihood of success)
preop.meds: Moderate sedation; gram-negative-coverage prophylaxis (obstructed systems are colonized until proven otherwise).
equipment: Existing PCN tract or new US-guided access (posterior calyx)
Sheath (7–8F), angled catheter + hydrophilic wire to negotiate the obstruction, exchange for a stiff working wire
Double-J stent sized by patient height/ureteral length (commonly 22–26 cm, 6–8F) with pusher; NUS as alternative
Safety nephrostomy tube for tenuous cases
indications: Ureteral obstruction (malignant compression, stone, stricture, iatrogenic injury) after failed retrograde stenting
Conversion of an existing nephrostomy to internal drainage
Ureteral leak/fistula (stent as a scaffold for healing, often NUS initially)
Pre-operative marking/protection of the ureter for pelvic surgery

absolute: Active untreated pyonephrosis — decompress with a nephrostomy first, stent later when sterile (pushing an internal stent through pus trades drainage for sepsis)
relative: Bladder dysfunction/small contracted bladder (stent worsens symptoms; reflux), uncorrected coagulopathy for new tract creation [2], bladder outlet obstruction defeating internal drainage

steps.text: Through the existing PCN (or new posterior calyx access), antegrade nephrostogram to define the level and morphology of obstruction.
steps.title: Access and opacify

steps.text: Angled catheter + hydrophilic wire probe past the stenosis into the bladder — the case's technical crux. Steep obliques and a torque device help; a stiff-shaft catheter follows to exchange for a working wire.
steps.title: Cross the obstruction

steps.text: Contrast puff confirming the wire/catheter coils in the bladder, not submucosa — a stent deployed submucosally fails immediately.
steps.title: Confirm intravesical position

steps.text: Advance the double-J over the stiff wire with the pusher; deploy the distal coil in the bladder, withdraw to form the proximal coil in the renal pelvis. Fluoro-verify both curls.
steps.title: Measure and deploy

steps.text: Clean deployment + sterile urine: remove access or leave a capped safety PCN 24–48 h. Bloody urine, infected system, or tenuous crossing: leave the safety tube and internalize later.
steps.title: Decide the top end

postop: Expect stent-related symptoms and counsel hard: frequency, urgency, flank twinge with voiding (reflux) — otherwise patients think something is wrong
Hematuria for days is normal; clots or heavy bleeding are not
Safety PCN (if left): cap trial, then remove after confirmed stent function
Calendar the exchange: stents encrust and occlude — exchange q3–6 months (sooner in stone formers/pregnancy); an indwelling 'forgotten stent' is a preventable disaster [1]

complications.name: Stent occlusion/encrustation
complications.management: Scheduled exchanges; encrusted stents may need combined endourologic clearance.

complications.name: Sepsis after stenting an infected system
complications.management: PCN first, stent when sterile; broad-spectrum antibiotics and pressors as needed.

complications.name: Stent migration (proximal or distal)
complications.management: Reposition/retrieve antegrade or cystoscopically; correct length prevents.

complications.name: Bladder irritation / LUTS
complications.management: Anticholinergics/alpha-blockers; distal coil length matters.

complications.name: Ureteral injury/perforation while crossing
complications.management: Usually heals over the stent itself; NUS across the injury if a leak persists.

complications.name: Failure to cross the obstruction
complications.management: Leave a nephrostomy; reattempt after decompression days later — edematous ureters often open up.

pearls: Pyonephrosis rule: infected + obstructed = external drainage today, internal stent another day. No exceptions worth making.
Can't cross today ≠ can't cross ever — a week of PCN decompression uncrumples the ureter and turns failures into 5-minute crossings.
The contrast puff before deployment is the cheapest insurance in IR: bladder curl confirmed, career-length embarrassment avoided.
Every internal stent needs an owner and a date — build the exchange into the discharge paperwork, not the patient's memory [1].

citations.text: Hausegger KA, Portugaller HR. Percutaneous nephrostomy and antegrade ureteral stenting: technique — indications — complications. Eur Radiol. 2006;16(9):2016-2030.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Uterine Fibroid Embolization
procedures.overview: Bilateral uterine artery embolization infarcts fibroids while normal myometrium survives on collateral supply. Randomized trials show symptom relief comparable to surgery with shorter recovery, at the cost of a higher reintervention rate (FEMME, EMMY) [1,2]. SIR published updated quality-improvement guidelines in 2025 [3].

labs: Contrast-enhanced pelvic MRI: fibroid mapping, viability (enhancement), exclude mimics (adenomyosis, sarcoma red flags), identify ovarian artery supply
Abnormal bleeding pattern → endometrial biopsy per gynecologic criteria before embolization
CBC (anemia common), pregnancy test day-of, creatinine/eGFR [4]
Platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L; INR ≤2.0 (low bleeding-risk) [5]
preop.meds: Aggressive multimodal analgesia is the procedure's real challenge: pre-procedure NSAIDs (ketorolac), antiemetics, PCA or superior hypogastric nerve block; single-dose cefazolin 1 g commonly given. Foley for the procedure.
equipment: 4–5F sheath (radial or femoral), Roberts uterine catheter (RUC) or Cobra + Waltman loop
Microcatheter for spasm-prone or small uterine arteries
Calibrated particles: 500–700 μm (± 700–900 μm) trisacryl gelatin or PVA equivalents
Nitroglycerin for spasm; cone-beam CT if supply ambiguous
indications: Symptomatic uterine fibroids: heavy menstrual bleeding, bulk symptoms (pelvic pressure, urinary frequency), dysmenorrhea
Adenomyosis (symptom relief achievable; durability lower than for fibroids — counsel accordingly)
Fibroid-related anemia refractory to medical therapy
Patients seeking uterine preservation or poor surgical candidates

absolute: Pregnancy
Active pelvic infection
Suspected uterine, cervical, or endometrial malignancy — investigate first
relative: Pedunculated subserosal fibroid with narrow stalk (detachment concern — modern data less alarming, still individualize)
Prior pelvic radiation or surgery altering collaterals
Desire for future fertility — myomectomy is often first-line per guidelines; UFE remains an option after counseling [1,3]
GnRH agonist therapy within 3 months (small, spastic uterine arteries — consider washout)

steps.text: Single femoral (or radial) access. Select the contralateral internal iliac → anterior division → uterine artery (RUC/Cobra); Waltman loop or radial approach handles the ipsilateral side from one femoral puncture.
steps.title: Access and first side

steps.text: Catheter tip beyond the cervicovaginal branch in the transverse/ascending uterine artery. Angiogram shows the classic hypervascular fibroid blush without free reflux.
steps.title: Confirm safe position

steps.text: Infuse particles in dilute contrast slowly under fluoro. Endpoint: near-stasis or 'pruned tree' — sluggish flow in the main uterine artery with elimination of the fibroid blush ('5–10 beat stasis'). Overembolization adds pain and ischemia without benefit [3].
steps.title: Embolize to endpoint

steps.text: Bilateral embolization is standard — unilateral treatment predicts failure via cross-filling. Form the loop or re-form the catheter and repeat.
steps.title: Repeat on the other side

steps.text: Persistent fibroid perfusion or an enlarged ovarian artery on aortogram/MRI → selective ovarian angiography; embolize fibroid supply distally only after weighing ovarian reserve implications.
steps.title: Check for ovarian supply

steps.text: Closure device or manual hemostasis; ensure PCA/block is running before the anesthesia team steps away — pain peaks in the first 2–6 hours.
steps.title: Closure and analgesia handoff

postop: 23-h observation or robust same-day pathway with PCA/block; discharge on NSAIDs + opioid rescue, antiemetics, bowel regimen
Post-embolization syndrome (pain, low-grade fever, nausea, malaise, leukocytosis) peaks at 24–72 h — supportive care; distinguish from infection by trajectory
Expect first period may be heavier/irregular; symptom improvement accrues over 3–6 months (85–90% for bleeding and bulk)
MRI at 3–6 months assesses infarction completeness
Counsel: reintervention ~15–30% by 2–5 years (FEMME/EMMY long-term data) [1,2]

complications.name: Post-embolization syndrome
complications.management: Multimodal analgesia, antiemetics, reassurance; usually resolves within 7–10 days.

complications.name: Fibroid expulsion
complications.management: Cramping + discharge weeks later; many pass spontaneously; retained/infected tissue → hysteroscopic resection.

complications.name: Endometritis / pyomyoma
complications.management: Fever beyond PES window, purulent discharge, rising WBC → cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics; failure → hysteroscopy/hysterectomy. The rare catastrophic complication — counsel explicitly.

complications.name: Ovarian insufficiency
complications.management: From ovarian nontarget/collateral embolization. Counsel pre-procedure; manage with gynecology.

complications.name: Nontarget embolization (bladder, vagina, skin)
complications.management: Prevent with positioning distal to cervicovaginal branch and controlled injection.

complications.name: DVT/PE
complications.management: Large uteri compress pelvic veins; early ambulation, prophylaxis per risk.

pearls: Pain control is the procedure: a technically perfect UFE with unmanaged pain is a failed patient experience — block early, medicate proactively.
Spasm is the enemy of completion: downsize to a microcatheter and use nitroglycerin rather than pushing particles into a spastic vessel (reflux).
Nonenhancing fibroids on pre-MRI will not respond — do not promise what infarction cannot deliver.
Ovarian artery supply is the classic cause of 'UFE failure'; look for it before, during, and when symptoms persist.

citations.text: Manyonda I, Belli AM, Lumsden MA, et al. Uterine-artery embolization or myomectomy for uterine fibroids (FEMME). N Engl J Med. 2020;383(5):440-451.

de Bruijn AM, Ankum WM, Reekers JA, et al. Uterine artery embolization vs hysterectomy in the treatment of symptomatic uterine fibroids: 10-year outcomes from the randomized EMMY trial. Am J Obstet Gynecol. 2016;215(6):745.e1-745.e12.
Society of Interventional Radiology. Quality improvement guidelines for uterine artery embolization for symptomatic leiomyomata. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2025 (updated guideline).
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Varicocele Embolization
procedures.overview: Retrograde embolization of the internal spermatic (gonadal) vein for varicocele — outcomes comparable to surgical ligation with faster recovery. Left side dominates (90%: left gonadal vein drains perpendicular into the left renal vein). The same skill set treats pelvic venous congestion via ovarian vein embolization [1].

labs: Scrotal duplex US documenting reflux and grade
Semen analysis when fertility is the indication (baseline for follow-up)
Creatinine/eGFR; platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0–3.0 [3]
Right-sided/atypical varicocele → cross-sectional imaging of retroperitoneum
preop.meds: Local + light sedation; most go home in 2 hours.
equipment: 5F sheath — right IJ (straight shot down both gonadal veins) or right femoral
Selective catheters: Simmons/MPA/Cobra for left renal vein → gonadal ostium; recurve shapes for right gonadal (off anterolateral IVC below the right renal vein)
Microcatheter for tortuous or duplicated channels
0.035/0.018 pushable and detachable coils; ± STS foam sclerosant (sandwich technique)
indications: Clinically palpable varicocele with abnormal semen parameters in an infertility evaluation
Symptomatic varicocele (dull ache, heaviness) refractory to conservative measures
Adolescent varicocele with testicular growth arrest/asymmetry
Recurrence after surgical ligation (embolization maps and closes the actual refluxing channels)

absolute: Contrast allergy uncontrolled (premedicate) [2]
relative: Isolated right varicocele or acute onset in an older man — image the retroperitoneum first (mass obstructing the gonadal vein/IVC until excluded)
Renal insufficiency (small contrast loads; CO2 possible)

steps.text: Jugular approach favors the caudal-directed gonadal ostia. Select the left renal vein, then the internal spermatic vein origin (inferior wall); Valsalva venogram confirms reflux and maps duplications and collaterals down to the inguinal ligament.
steps.title: Access and select

steps.text: Advance catheter/microcatheter to the level of the superior pubic ramus/inguinal canal region — occlusion must start low or perforators re-establish reflux.
steps.title: Descend to the target zone

steps.text: Deploy coils starting just above the inguinal ligament, then successively upward — 'sandwich' the vein, ± sclerosant foam between coil nests to obliterate parallel channels (avoid foam refluxing into the pampiniform plexus — that causes phlebitis).
steps.title: Embolize from below upward

steps.text: Repeat Valsalva venography at each stage: duplicated trunks, renolumbar communications, and cross-pelvic collaterals are the anatomy of recurrence. Coil refluxing channels selectively.
steps.title: Hunt collaterals

steps.text: Final coil nest in the main trunk 3–5 cm below the renal vein junction (left) — far enough to avoid protrusion/migration into the renal vein. Completion venogram: no residual reflux.
steps.title: Finish high

steps.text: Recurve catheter at the right gonadal ostium; same bottom-up strategy. Respect the more direct IVC angle — migration risk is higher; detachable coils reassure.
steps.title: Right side (when treated)

postop: Same-day discharge; light activity 24–48 h
Counsel: dull ache/low-grade phlebitis sensation up to a week — NSAIDs
Fertility indication: repeat semen analysis at ~3 months (a full spermatogenic cycle)
Clinical recurrence → duplex + repeat venography (collateral hunt)

complications.name: Pampiniform phlebitis
complications.management: NSAIDs, scrotal support, warm compresses; self-limited days–2 weeks.

complications.name: Recurrence
complications.management: Repeat venography finds missed duplications/renolumbar collaterals; re-embolize — success remains high.

complications.name: Coil migration (renal vein/pulmonary)
complications.management: Snare retrieval; prevention: correct sizing, detachable coils near ostia.

complications.name: Vein perforation during descent
complications.management: Retroperitoneal contrast staining is typically self-limited — reposition and complete via an alternate channel.

complications.name: Contrast reaction / AKI
complications.management: Standard prophylaxis pathways [2].

pearls: Start low, finish high: coils only at the top of the gonadal vein are a temporary inconvenience to the collateral circulation.
Valsalva is your contrast agent multiplier — reflux anatomy only declares itself under provocation.
New right-sided varicocele in an adult = retroperitoneal imaging before the angio suite. Classic board (and real-world) trap.
The identical playbook (ovarian vein reflux, bottom-up coils ± foam) treats pelvic venous congestion — one skill, two diseases [1].

citations.text: Society of Interventional Radiology. Standards of practice: endovascular management of chronic pelvic pain of venous origin. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2026 (standards document).
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Vascular Malformation Embolization & Sclerotherapy
procedures.overview: Image-guided treatment of congenital vascular anomalies: direct-puncture sclerotherapy for low-flow venous and lymphatic malformations, and transarterial (or direct) embolization targeting the nidus for high-flow AVMs. Classification drives everything — treat by ISSVA category, not by appearance [1]. These are chronic diseases managed over staged sessions, not one-visit cures [2].

labs: Low bleeding-risk per SIR [3]; baseline CBC/coags for large lesions (extensive VMs cause localized intravascular coagulopathy — check D-dimer/fibrinogen)
MRI with contrast is the roadmap: flow characteristics, compartments, relationship to nerves
Diagnostic angiography for high-flow lesions to define nidus and feeders
preop.meds: General anesthesia common (sclerosant injection is painful; ethanol requires monitoring). Dexamethasone for airway-adjacent lesions.
equipment: Low-flow: 21–23G needles/angiocaths for direct puncture, contrast for lesion-o-gram, sclerosant (STS foam, ethanol, bleomycin, doxycycline for LMs), tourniquet/compression for outflow control
High-flow: microcatheter systems, liquid embolics (Onyx/nBCA), coils/plugs for outflow or fistulous components
US for puncture; fluoro for sclerosant/embolic monitoring; MRI for follow-up
indications: Symptomatic venous malformation (pain, swelling, functional impairment, phleboliths with recurrent thrombophlebitis)
Macrocystic lymphatic malformation (mass effect, infection, cosmesis)
AVM with pain, ulceration/bleeding, high-output physiology, or threatened function
Preoperative devascularization before surgical resection

absolute: No safe access to the lesion; embolizing proximal feeders of an AVM without reaching the nidus (worsens the disease by recruiting collaterals)
relative: Lesions abutting critical nerves/skin where sclerosant injury is likely (dose-limit, stage)
Pulmonary hypertension or right-to-left shunt for ethanol (systemic release risk)
Active infection over the access site

steps.text: Confirm the ISSVA diagnosis (VM vs LM vs AVM vs tumor such as hemangioma) with MRI ± angio — the treatment, agent, and counseling all pivot on this [1].
steps.title: Classify first

steps.text: US-guided direct puncture; inject contrast to define compartment volume and venous outflow rate before committing sclerosant.
steps.title: Low-flow: puncture and lesion-o-gram

steps.text: Compress/tourniquet outflow, then inject sclerosant (foamed STS or ethanol; bleomycin where swelling must be minimized) to fill—not overfill—the compartment. Multiple compartments = multiple punctures.
steps.title: Low-flow: control outflow and sclerose

steps.text: Superselective microcatheterization (or direct nidus puncture); deliver liquid embolic into the nidus itself. Proximal feeder occlusion alone is the cardinal error.
steps.title: High-flow: reach the nidus

steps.text: Treat a safe volume and stop — plan serial sessions 6–12 weeks apart with MRI reassessment rather than chasing a single-session cure [2].
steps.title: Stage

postop: Expect significant swelling 24–72 h — elevate, ice, NSAIDs/steroid taper for large treatments; airway-adjacent lesions may need observation
Skin surveillance over treated superficial compartments (blistering/necrosis)
Ethanol cases: monitor for hemoglobinuria — hydrate; watch for cardiopulmonary events intra/post-op
MRI follow-up at ~3 months to plan the next stage

complications.name: Skin blistering/necrosis
complications.management: Wound care; avoid by dose-limiting superficial compartments and diluting sclerosant near dermis.

complications.name: Nerve injury
complications.management: Most neuropraxias recover over weeks–months; avoid ethanol adjacent to major nerves.

complications.name: Ethanol systemic toxicity (cardiopulmonary collapse, hemoglobinuria)
complications.management: Dose limits (~0.5–1 mL/kg max), staged injections, anesthesia monitoring; supportive/ICU care.

complications.name: DVT/PE from outflow sclerosant escape
complications.management: Outflow compression during injection; anticoagulate if it occurs.

complications.name: AVM worsening after proximal-only embolization
complications.management: Prevention: nidus-targeted therapy; salvage via direct puncture of recruited nidus.

complications.name: Swelling with airway/compartment compromise
complications.management: Steroids, airway protection, staged small-volume treatments in high-risk locations.

pearls: ISSVA discipline: 'hemangioma' is a tumor of infancy that involutes; malformations never do — misclassification leads to wrong treatment for years [1].
For AVMs the nidus is the disease: coiling a feeder is like ligating the artery in 1970 — the malformation recruits and returns angrier.
Ethanol is the most effective sclerosant and the most dangerous — respect per-session dose limits and anesthesia monitoring.
Counsel from day one that this is staged, chronic management; 'control of symptoms' — not angiographic cure — is the honest endpoint for most [2].

citations.text: Wassef M, Blei F, Adams D, et al. Vascular anomalies classification: recommendations from the International Society for the Study of Vascular Anomalies. Pediatrics. 2015;136(1):e203-e214.
Legiehn GM, Heran MK. Venous malformations: classification, development, diagnosis, and interventional radiologic management. Radiol Clin North Am. 2008;46(3):545-597.

Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Diagnostic Venography (Extremity, Caval, Renal & Portal)
procedures.overview: Catheter-based contrast opacification of the venous system — extremity runoff, IVC (cavagram), selective renal veins, and the portal system (usually transhepatic, transsplenic, or via TIPS access). Mostly supplanted by duplex US and CT/MR venography for diagnosis, it survives where dynamic filling, pressures, and sampling matter: pre-filter cavagrams, venous compression syndromes, renin sampling, and intervention planning.

labs: Low bleeding-risk per SIR [1]; transhepatic portal access is higher risk — treat like a liver puncture
Creatinine for contrast; review duplex/CT first so the venogram answers a specific question
For renin sampling: coordinate antihypertensive washout with the referring service
preop.meds: Local anesthesia ± light sedation.
equipment: US-guided venous access (femoral, IJ, arm); 5F sheath
Multi-sidehole catheters for caval/large-vein runs; selective catheters (Cobra, MPA) for renal/portal branches
Pressure transducer setup — gradients are half the study's value (≥3 mmHg mean across an iliac lesion is commonly cited as significant)
CO2 delivery for renal-protective imaging; tilting table/Valsalva for extremity reflux studies
indications: Cavagram before IVC filter placement or retrieval (caval size, duplication, thrombus, renal vein level)
Mapping chronic deep venous obstruction before recanalization/stenting (May-Thurner, post-thrombotic iliocaval disease)
Upper extremity/central venograms for dialysis planning or SVC syndrome
Selective renal venography: renal vein renin sampling, nutcracker syndrome evaluation, tumor thrombus mapping
Portal venography with pressures during TIPS, portal interventions, or transhepatic portal access

absolute: None beyond lack of safe access
relative: Contrast allergy (premedicate; CO2 works well in veins), severe renal insufficiency (CO2 preferred), pregnancy (shield/defer)

steps.text: Venography is an answer machine, not a survey: know whether you're measuring (caliber, level), sampling (renin), pressuring (gradient), or mapping (collaterals) before contrast goes in.
steps.title: Answer a defined question

steps.text: US-guided access; advance a multi-sidehole catheter for large-vessel runs or a selective catheter into the target branch over a wire.
steps.title: Access and catheterize

steps.text: Power-inject the infrarenal IVC; identify renal vein inflow (unopacified streams), measure caval diameter (>28–30 mm changes filter choice), exclude duplication and thrombus.
steps.title: Cavagram technique

steps.text: Renal veins: gentle hand injections; renin samples from each renal vein + infrarenal IVC, labeled meticulously. Iliocaval disease: pull-back pressures and obliques for compression points.
steps.title: Selective runs, pressures, samples

steps.text: Via TIPS access, transhepatic, or transsplenic route: portal pressures (with RA pressure for the gradient), then a portogram defining flow direction and varices.
steps.title: Portal system

steps.text: Venous access: brief compression. Transhepatic tracts: embolize the tract (gelfoam/coils) on the way out.
steps.title: Close

postop: Brief observation; venous access sites rarely misbehave
Transhepatic/transsplenic access: hemoglobin check and longer observation — treat as a solid-organ puncture
Hydration if a significant contrast load; results routed with the specific measurements the referrer needed

complications.name: Access-site hematoma/thrombosis
complications.management: Compression; anticoagulation rarely needed.

complications.name: Contrast reaction / CIN
complications.management: Premedication pathway; CO2 substitution in veins is excellent.

complications.name: Bleeding from transhepatic portal access
complications.management: Tract embolization is prevention; angiography/embolization if hemodynamically significant.

complications.name: Vein perforation with wire/catheter
complications.management: Veins are forgiving — low pressure means most perforations are clinically silent; reposition and continue.

pearls: Unopacified renal vein inflow on a cavagram is information, not artifact — it marks the renal levels for filter planning.
A mega-cava (>28–30 mm) is a filter-choice fork: bird's nest or bilateral iliac filters — measure, don't eyeball.
Iliac vein lesions lie on single planes — obliques plus a pull-back gradient beat any single AP run for May-Thurner.
Label renin tubes at the table, with the fluoro image saved for each sample site; a mislabeled tube invalidates the entire study.

citations.text: Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. Society of Interventional Radiology consensus guidelines for the periprocedural management of thrombotic and bleeding risk in patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided interventions. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

Gloviczki P, Comerota AJ, Dalsing MC, et al. The care of patients with varicose veins and associated chronic venous diseases: clinical practice guidelines of the Society for Vascular Surgery and the American Venous Forum. J Vasc Surg. 2011;53(5 Suppl):2S-48S.

procedures.title: Vertebroplasty & Kyphoplasty
procedures.overview: Percutaneous injection of PMMA bone cement into a painful vertebral compression fracture (vertebroplasty), optionally after balloon cavity creation and height restoration (kyphoplasty). After early sham-controlled trials questioned benefit, VAPOUR and subsequent data support augmentation for acute, severely painful osteoporotic fractures in selected patients [1,2].

labs: MRI (STIR/edema) or bone scan confirming an active, painful fracture at the treated level — the single best patient-selection tool
Low bleeding-risk: platelets ≥20 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0–3.0 [3]
Malignancy workup where pathologic fracture suspected (biopsy at the same session is common)
Neuro exam documented
preop.meds: Local + moderate sedation (or general for multilevel/pathologic). Antibiotics sometimes added to cement or given IV per practice, especially in immunocompromised.
equipment: Biplane or high-quality single-plane fluoro (± CT); bone access/trocar needles (11–13G)
Kyphoplasty balloon tamps and inflation system (if height restoration planned)
PMMA bone cement with opacifier; cement delivery system (low-pressure, remote injection to reduce operator radiation)
Bone biopsy device (for pathologic fractures)
indications: Painful acute/subacute osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture refractory to medical therapy, with fracture edema on MRI (or uptake on bone scan)
Painful pathologic/malignant vertebral fracture (myeloma, metastasis) for pain palliation and stabilization
Selected traumatic fractures without retropulsion/neurologic compromise
Fracture-related immobility where kyphoplasty may restore some height

absolute: Asymptomatic/healed fracture; pain not concordant with the fracture level
Local or systemic infection (osteomyelitis/discitis at the level)
Retropulsed fragment with cord compression/myelopathy (surgical), uncorrectable coagulopathy
relative: Fracture with significant canal compromise or unstable posterior elements
Severe vertebra plana (technically difficult)
Cement allergy

steps.text: Correlate the painful level clinically with MRI edema/bone-scan uptake and fluoroscopic anatomy. Treating a chronic healed level is the classic reason for a 'failed' augmentation.
steps.title: Confirm the target level

steps.text: Under fluoro (AP + lateral), advance the bone needle through the pedicle into the anterior third of the vertebral body. Stay lateral-to-medial without breaching the medial pedicle cortex (cord/nerve) until safely within the body. Unipedicular or bipedicular per fill goal.
steps.title: Transpedicular access

steps.text: For suspected malignancy, take a core through the access before cement (the cement destroys future sampling).
steps.title: Biopsy if indicated

steps.text: Inflate balloon tamps to create a cavity and attempt height restoration/kyphosis correction; deflate and remove, leaving a low-pressure space for controlled cement fill.
steps.title: Kyphoplasty cavity (optional)

steps.text: Inject PMMA in its doughy phase slowly under continuous lateral fluoroscopy, watching the cement interdigitate. STOP immediately at any leak toward the posterior body, epidural space, or veins — this real-time vigilance is the core safety maneuver.
steps.title: Inject cement under live fluoro

steps.text: Adequate fill crosses the fracture lines and reaches the posterior quarter without breaching it. Bilateral fill for symmetry if unipedicular is insufficient.
steps.title: Fill to endpoint

steps.text: Allow partial curing, then remove the needle with a stylet/rotation to avoid pulling a cement tail. Final images document the cement cast.
steps.title: Let cement cure and withdraw

postop: Bed rest ~1–2 h supine while cement fully cures, then mobilize — many report rapid pain relief
Neuro checks; new radicular pain or weakness → urgent CT for epidural/foraminal cement
Treat the underlying disease: osteoporosis therapy (this fracture predicts the next), oncologic management for pathologic fractures
Counsel on adjacent-level fracture risk; some occur within weeks
Follow-up for pain response and new fractures

complications.name: Cement extravasation (usually asymptomatic)
complications.management: Live-fluoro vigilance and stop-at-leak technique prevent harm. Epidural/foraminal leak with deficit → steroids, urgent surgical decompression.

complications.name: Pulmonary cement embolism
complications.management: Doughy cement + slow injection reduces venous leak; symptomatic PE managed supportively/anticoagulation.

complications.name: Adjacent-level fracture
complications.management: Partly natural history of osteoporosis; optimize bone-health therapy; treat symptomatic new fractures.

complications.name: Infection / discitis
complications.management: Sterile technique; established infection needs antibiotics ± surgery (cement is a nidus).

complications.name: Neurologic injury (pedicle breach)
complications.management: Meticulous AP/lateral trajectory, respect the medial pedicle cortex; deficit → imaging and surgical consult.

complications.name: Rib/pedicle fracture, bleeding
complications.management: Supportive; access-site care.

pearls: MRI edema is the patient-selection oracle: augmenting an active, painful fracture works; augmenting a chronic healed one is how trials got 'negative.'
The medial pedicle cortex is a line you do not cross until you are safely in the body — it is all that separates your needle from the cord.
Inject doughy, inject slow, and watch the posterior quarter like a hawk — most catastrophic outcomes are posterior/epidural cement seen too late.
Every augmentation is also an osteoporosis (or oncology) referral — the next vertebra is listening.

citations.text: Clark W, Bird P, Gonski P, et al. Safety and efficacy of vertebroplasty for acute painful osteoporotic fractures (VAPOUR): a multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Lancet. 2016;388(10052):1408-1416.
Buchbinder R, Johnston RV, Rischin KJ, et al. Percutaneous vertebroplasty for osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2018;(11):CD006349.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.

procedures.title: Y-90 Radioembolization (TARE)
procedures.overview: Transarterial delivery of Y-90-loaded microspheres (glass or resin) that lodge in the tumor microvasculature and deliver high-dose beta radiation locally. Uniquely, it is a two-visit procedure: a mapping angiogram (with Tc-99m MAA to measure lung shunt and simulate distribution) precedes treatment. 'Radiation segmentectomy' can be ablative for solitary HCC [1,2].

labs: LFTs (bilirubin), albumin, INR, platelets; tumor markers
Triphasic CT/MRI for tumor burden, vascular invasion, target volume
Mapping angiogram data: hepatic arterial anatomy, Tc-99m MAA SPECT/CT lung shunt fraction and tumor-to-normal ratio
Low bleeding-risk classification: platelets ≥20–50 ×10⁹/L, INR ≤2.0 [4]
preop.meds: Antiemetics; usually well tolerated (microembolic, minimal post-embolization syndrome vs TACE). No routine antibiotics.
equipment: Mapping: 5F/microcatheter, coils for prophylactic occlusion of aberrant/GI vessels (GDA, right gastric) when needed
Tc-99m MAA for the simulation study
Treatment day: same catheter position; Y-90 device — glass (TheraSphere) or resin (SIR-Spheres) microspheres
Radiation safety: authorized user, dosimetry/physics, shielded delivery apparatus, contamination monitoring
CBCT for confirming target perfusion and excluding nontarget
indications: Unresectable HCC (including selected portal vein thrombosis — Y-90 is microembolic, better tolerated than TACE with PVT)
Radiation segmentectomy for early solitary HCC not suited to ablation/resection
Metastatic colorectal cancer to liver (salvage/selected lines)
Neuroendocrine and other hypervascular hepatic metastases; downstaging/bridging

absolute: Uncorrectable hepatopulmonary shunting delivering >30 Gy to lungs single / >50 Gy cumulative (radiation pneumonitis)
Uncorrectable flow to GI tract that would deliver spheres to bowel/stomach
Severely limited hepatic reserve (radioembolization-induced liver disease risk)
relative: Bilirubin >2 mg/dL (higher REILD risk) — favor selective/segmental treatment
Prior liver-directed radiation, limited pulmonary reserve
Contrast allergy (premedicate) [3]

steps.text: Celiac/SMA and selective hepatic angiography; identify variant anatomy and extrahepatic vessels (GDA, right gastric, falciform, accessory phrenic) that could shunt spheres to the GI tract. Coil-embolize risky vessels or plan to inject distal to them.
steps.title: Mapping angiogram (visit 1)

steps.text: Inject Tc-99m MAA from the planned treatment position(s) as a particle surrogate. SPECT/CT calculates lung shunt fraction and tumor-to-normal uptake — the safety and dosimetry backbone.
steps.title: MAA simulation

steps.text: Physics/authorized user compute activity: MIRD (glass) or BSA/partition models (resin), correcting for lung shunt. Reduce/deny dose if lung dose limits would be exceeded. Radiation segmentectomy uses high segmental dose (>190–400+ Gy glass) for an ablative effect.
steps.title: Dose planning

steps.text: Re-access, reproduce the mapping catheter position exactly (anatomy verified against mapping run and CBCT). Reproducibility is the safety principle — spheres go where MAA went.
steps.title: Treatment day positioning

steps.text: Administer microspheres through the shielded delivery system under fluoroscopy with the physics team, monitoring for stasis (resin especially — avoid reflux). Follow strict radiation safety and contamination protocols.
steps.title: Deliver Y-90

steps.text: Bremsstrahlung SPECT/CT or Y-90 PET confirms microsphere distribution matches the target and excludes significant nontarget deposition.
steps.title: Post-delivery imaging

steps.text: Brief precautions per regulatory guidance; most patients go home the same day feeling well.
steps.title: Discharge with radiation precautions

postop: Mild fatigue common; post-embolization syndrome much less than TACE
LFT monitoring; watch for radioembolization-induced liver disease (REILD: jaundice, ascites weeks–months later) especially with whole-liver/high tumor burden
Follow-up MRI/CT at ~1–3 months (response by size and enhancement evolves slower than TACE; expect delayed necrosis)
Radiation safety counseling per authorized-user instructions
Multidisciplinary re-assessment: repeat lobe treatment, transplant, systemic therapy

complications.name: Radioembolization-induced liver disease (REILD)
complications.management: Prevention via selective treatment and bilirubin selection; supportive care, steroids sometimes used.

complications.name: Radiation pneumonitis
complications.management: Prevented by MAA lung-shunt limits (dose reduction/denial); manage established pneumonitis with steroids.

complications.name: GI ulceration (nontarget spheres)
complications.management: Prevention: meticulous mapping, prophylactic coiling, distal injection. Established ulcers are refractory — PPIs, endoscopy, rarely surgery.

complications.name: Radiation cholecystitis
complications.management: Usually conservative; cholecystectomy occasionally needed.

complications.name: Lymphopenia / fatigue
complications.management: Supportive; monitor counts.

complications.name: Access-site complications
complications.management: Standard arterial access care.

pearls: Y-90 is radiation therapy delivered by catheter — the physics team and authorized user are co-pilots, not consultants. Dosimetry drives the case.
MAA is the promise you make to the tumor: spheres follow the same path. Reproduce the mapping position exactly or the simulation is worthless.
Because it is microembolic (not flow-occlusive), Y-90 tolerates portal vein thrombosis better than TACE — a key selection advantage.
Radiation segmentectomy (very high dose to one or two segments) can be curative-intent for solitary HCC unsuited to ablation [2].

citations.text: Salem R, Lewandowski RJ, Mulcahy MF, et al. Radioembolization for hepatocellular carcinoma using Yttrium-90 microspheres: a comprehensive report of long-term outcomes. Gastroenterology. 2010;138(1):52-64.
Lewandowski RJ, Gabr A, Abouchaleh N, et al. Radiation segmentectomy: potential curative therapy for early hepatocellular carcinoma. Radiology. 2018;287(3):1050-1058.
ACR Committee on Drugs and Contrast Media. ACR Manual on Contrast Media. American College of Radiology; 2025.
Patel IJ, Rahim S, Davidson JC, et al. SIR consensus guidelines for periprocedural management of bleeding risk — Part II. J Vasc Interv Radiol. 2019;30(8):1168-1184.
